33595.d: A Russian RSCC government communications satellite at 11°W providing C-band and Ku-band services for Russian domestic communications and reaching into the Americas. Express-AM44's western Atlantic position allows it to serve the Russian government's communications requirements for its diplomatic missions and state interests in the Americas, supplementing the domestic satellite coverage provided by Express satellites over Russian territory.

33749.d: An SES satellite (formerly New Skies Satellites) at 183°E providing C-band communications across the Pacific. NSS-9 serves the Pacific Ocean's island communities and maritime routes with the wide-area C-band coverage that reaches smaller, cheaper ground terminals — important in Pacific island markets where sophisticated Ku-band equipment is cost-prohibitive for local broadcasters and telecommunications operators.

34111.d: A Telesat satellite carrying one of the most storied brand names in satellite history. The original Telstar-1, launched in 1962, made the first live transatlantic television relay — a moment that changed how humans thought about global communications. Telstar-11N continues that legacy providing Ku-band services over North America and the Atlantic, carrying the Telstar name on a mission that would have seemed like science fiction to the engineers who watched Telstar-1's flickering black-and-white images cross the ocean.

34713.d: The second WGS satellite, doubling US military broadband capacity in space. WGS-2 demonstrated that demand for military bandwidth would grow exponentially — each new satellite was met with instant saturation as commanders found new uses for the data capacity. Drone operations in Afghanistan and Iraq consumed bandwidth that would have seemed unimaginable to military planners a decade earlier.

34941.d: An SES satellite acquired from bankrupt Protostar Ltd. providing services over Asia-Pacific. SES purchased Protostar-2's orbital slot and customer contracts during a bankruptcy restructuring — the satellite secondary market where operational satellites transfer between operators through financial transactions is a significant part of the commercial satellite industry. SES-7 now serves Asia-Pacific cable and broadcasting customers from its acquired position.

35362.d: Malaysia's communications satellite providing direct-to-home TV broadcasting and broadband across Southeast Asia. MEASAT satellites are operated by Malaysia's national satellite company, providing the backbone for Malaysian satellite broadcasting and supporting connectivity across Indonesia, Philippines, and surrounding nations. Malaysia's position at 91.5°E provides excellent coverage of the densely populated South and Southeast Asian markets.

35491.d: America's on-orbit spare — a fully operational weather satellite kept in standby above the Americas, ready to activate within hours if a primary GOES fails. It proved its value in 2012 when NOAA activated it during Superstorm Sandy, adding extra coverage as the historic storm carved up the East Coast. Not every hero is in active service.

35493.d: One of the original O3b (Other 3 Billion) satellites — a constellation concept created specifically to bring broadband connectivity to developing nations and remote maritime regions. FM-5 is part of the original O3b Fleet constellation that now operates as SES Networks' medium Earth orbit broadband service. O3b targets maritime cruise ships, remote island communities, and enterprise customers in emerging markets where terrestrial and GEO satellite broadband is too slow, too expensive, or unavailable.

35496.d: An ambitious but ultimately unsuccessful attempt to provide direct-to-smartphone mobile satellite service in North America. TerreStar-1 was one of the largest commercial GEO satellites ever built at launch — nearly 7 tons — designed to use an enormous 18-meter antenna to communicate directly with LTE smartphones without special satellite hardware. The company went bankrupt before the service launched, but the technical concept TerreStar proved has since been revived by Starlink, AST SpaceMobile, and Lynk in LEO approaches.

35681.d: Spain's first Earth observation satellite carries a 600 km wide-field imager — extraordinary coverage for its resolution class. This wide-area view makes it ideal for monitoring agricultural conditions across entire countries and rapid disaster assessment. It proved European small satellite startups could build operational Earth observation systems.

35682.d: The UAE's first satellite and the foundation of one of the world's most ambitious national space programs. DubaiSat-1 trained the Emirati engineers who would go on to build the Hope Mars Mission. The UAE went from its first satellite to Mars in barely a decade — one of the fastest national space program evolutions in history.

35684.d: One of SpaceQuest's AprizesSat IoT messaging constellation providing two-way data communication for remote asset tracking and environmental monitoring. These early IoT constellations pioneered the satellite data collection market that Globalstar, Iridium, and commercial startups now compete in fiercely — before the term 'satellite IoT' even existed.

35686.d: Part of SpaceQuest's early satellite IoT constellation providing store-and-forward messaging for remote locations. Its applications range from livestock tracking in Australia's outback to pipeline monitoring in the Arctic — use cases that defined the commercial satellite IoT market a decade before Starlink's IoT services arrived.

35696.d: AsiaSat's C-band satellite serving the Asia-Pacific with particular strength in coverage of Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Pacific. AsiaSat-5 carries C-band capacity preferred by Asian broadcasters for its resistance to tropical rainfall and its wide beam coverage — allowing smaller networks to reach vast geographic areas with a single transmission.

35755.d: A Sky Perfect JSAT satellite providing C-band services across Asia and Australia. The Japanese commercial satellite market has evolved significantly — from basic broadcasting to high-throughput broadband services. JCSAT-12's C-band payload provides reliable communications across tropical Southeast Asia where C-band's rain fade resistance makes it preferred for mission-critical applications.

35756.d: Australia's satellite television and broadband platform, with specific capacity allocated for Australia's military and government requirements. Optus D3 supports Australian defense communications and provides the government-assured capacity that Australia needs for emergency management coordination across its vast territory during bushfires, floods, and cyclones — natural disasters that regularly affect Australia's remote and communications-deprived inland regions.

35873.d: A Canadian DTH satellite carrying the HD channels that defined Bell Satellite TV's competitive positioning. As Canadian consumers upgraded to HDTV, Nimiq-5 provided the bandwidth headroom for the hundreds of HD channels Bell needed to match cable's expanding HD lineups. The migration to HD satellite broadcasting doubled and tripled bandwidth requirements — satellites like Nimiq-5 made it possible without degrading existing standard-definition service.

35931.d: India's ocean wind satellite monitors the Indian Ocean — the birthplace of the monsoon that waters the subcontinent. Its scatterometer measures wind vectors precisely enough to predict monsoon onset timing and intensity, critical for agricultural planning across South Asia where a late monsoon triggers food insecurity for hundreds of millions.

35932.d: SwissCube, Switzerland's first satellite and one of the most celebrated student-built CubeSats in spaceflight history. Designed and constructed entirely by students at the École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), the 1 kg spacecraft launched in 2009 to study airglow — the faint natural luminescence in Earth's upper atmosphere — using a compact onboard camera and spectrometer. Remarkably, SwissCube remains operational and is still transmitting data more than 15 years after launch, far outliving its planned mission and becoming a point of enduring national pride for Swiss space engineering.

35942.d: A Hispasat commercial satellite serving the Americas, particularly Brazil and Latin America's growing satellite TV and broadband markets. Amazonas satellites provide the backbone for direct-to-home broadcasting across the Amazon basin and interior Brazil — bringing digital television to communities accessible only by river where no cable will ever reach and terrestrial signals cannot penetrate the jungle canopy.

35943.d: Germany's first dedicated military communications satellite, operated by the Bundeswehr. COMSATBw-1 provides UHF and SHF connectivity for German armed forces — marking Germany's transition from reliance on US and NATO infrastructure to sovereign military space communications. It supports German deployments in Afghanistan, Mali, and other operational areas where assured communications independent of allied networks is strategically important.

35946.d: The first commercial satellite offering 8-band multispectral imagery at 50 cm resolution made it possible to distinguish subtle differences in vegetation health, water quality, and soil type invisible to previous satellites. Its coastal blue band penetrates shallow water for coral reef mapping — used by NOAA and marine conservationists worldwide.

35951.d: The newest active DMSP satellite, F18 monitors global cloud patterns, precipitation, and ocean surface winds for US military operations. Its J-sensor measures charged particle precipitation — the same particles causing aurora and disrupting radio communications at high latitudes, directly affecting military communications in polar regions.

36032.d: A high-power SES Ku-band satellite at 57°E providing connectivity across the Middle East, Africa, and South Asia. NSS-12's Ku-band spot beams deliver direct-to-home broadcasting to Middle Eastern subscribers and enterprise broadband to African and South Asian businesses. The 57°E slot provides optimal coverage of the Gulf Cooperation Council countries — one of the world's most valuable DTH broadcasting markets given the region's high disposable income and appetite for international content.

36033.d: A Telenor satellite at 0.8°W adding capacity to the Scandinavian DTH neighborhood. Thor-6 expanded the channel capacity available at the 0.8°W position as Norwegian, Swedish, and Danish broadcasters transitioned to HD — each HD channel requiring three times the bandwidth of its standard definition predecessor. Together the Thor satellites form the backbone of Scandinavian satellite television, serving markets with some of the highest pay television penetration rates in the world.

36036.d: ESA's Soil Moisture and Ocean Salinity satellite carries the most unusual instrument in Earth orbit: an interferometric microwave radiometer synthesizing a 1,000 km wide antenna from a Y-shaped array of 69 small receivers. SMOS measures soil moisture and ocean salinity — the variables linking Earth's water, energy, and carbon cycles at planetary scale.

36037.d: ESA's second PROBA technology demonstrator became one of the best solar observation satellites ever launched. Its SWAP instrument captures the widest-field extreme ultraviolet images of the Sun's corona from any spacecraft, while LYRA measures solar UV irradiance 20 times per second — fast enough to capture solar flare onset with millisecond timing. Despite being classified as a technology demonstrator, PROBA-2 has published hundreds of science papers.

36097.d: An Intelsat C and Ku-band commercial communications satellite serving transatlantic routes. Intelsat has connected continents since it launched the world's first commercial communications satellite in 1965 — EARLY BIRD allowed the first live transatlantic TV broadcast. IS-14 continues that legacy, carrying broadband, enterprise data, and government communications between the Americas, Europe, and Africa on the same routes that Intelsat has served for six decades.

36101.d: A Eutelsat satellite at 36°E providing direct-to-home broadcasting for Russia and Eastern Europe. The 36°E position is Russia's primary satellite television neighborhood — millions of Russian households have their dishes aimed at this slot, receiving NTV+, Tricolor, and hundreds of Russian and international channels. Eutelsat-36B provides the Ku-band capacity that makes Russian DTH possible at a scale unmatched by Russian domestic satellite operators.

36106.d: An Intelsat Ku-band satellite covering the Middle East, South Asia, and the Asia-Pacific. IS-15's position provides coverage from Eastern Europe to Southeast Asia — a footprint that includes some of the fastest-growing satellite markets in the world. The satellite serves both commercial broadcasting and government communications across a region where terrestrial infrastructure varies enormously from dense urban fiber to complete connectivity absence.

36108.d: The third WGS satellite with improved flexibility for routing traffic between different frequency bands. WGS-3 completed the initial planned constellation and provided coverage over Europe, the Middle East, and Asia — the primary areas of US military operations throughout the 2010s. Its digital channelizers can split its bandwidth into over 1,900 circuits simultaneously.

36110.d: Part of China's Yaogan Earth observation constellation. The complete Yaogan fleet spans optical, radar, and signals intelligence variants and can revisit any point on Earth multiple times daily — a space-based surveillance capability that has fundamentally changed the strategic information balance in the Indo-Pacific.

36121.d: One of China's early second-generation reconnaissance satellites, launched in 2009 as part of the rapidly maturing Yaogan program. Yaogan-8 belongs to a generation of individual (non-triplet) Yaogan satellites that carried either high-resolution electro-optical cameras or synthetic aperture radar for military imaging missions. At 15+ years old and still being tracked, Yaogan-8 has outlasted its designed operational life — its presence in the catalog reflects either continued residual functionality or the value of maintaining it as a tracked object for space situational awareness purposes. It is one of the oldest operational Yaogan satellites still in orbit, a relic of China's initial push to build genuine space-based reconnaissance capability in the mid-2000s.

36131.d: A DirecTV satellite at 103°W providing additional Ku-band capacity for the US direct-to-home market. DirecTV-12 was positioned at 103°W to further expand the multi-satellite neighborhood accessible from standard DirecTV dish installations. Each additional satellite in the 99°-103°W arc can be received without dish repointing, allowing DirecTV to continuously expand its channel capacity and resolution quality without requiring customers to upgrade their hardware.

36287.d: The first geostationary satellite of China's BeiDou-2 navigation system — China's answer to GPS, GLONASS, and Galileo. BeiDou GEO satellites provide both navigation signals and a unique Short Message Service that allows two-way messaging without internet connectivity, originally developed for Chinese fishing vessels in remote waters and later used by hikers, disaster responders, and military units throughout China. BeiDou GEO satellites hover over the Asia-Pacific, providing stronger signals than the US GPS system in that region.

36395.d: SDO photographs the Sun in ten wavelengths of light simultaneously, every 12 seconds, at a resolution four times sharper than HD television — producing more science data per day than any other NASA mission. This continuous stream of imagery has revealed the intricate dance of magnetic loops in the Sun's corona and let scientists track solar flares and eruptions in real time. If you've ever seen a stunning close-up video of the Sun, it almost certainly came from SDO.

36397.d: An Intelsat Ku-band satellite at 58°W serving the Americas. IS-16's position at 58°W optimally covers the South Atlantic and northeastern South America — particularly Brazil's coastal cities and the offshore oil platforms of the Brazilian pre-salt oil fields, where satellite is the primary communications link for offshore workers and automated platform monitoring systems.

36411.d: Originally GOES-15, this satellite was transferred to the US Space Force and repurposed as Enhanced Weather System-G2 for military Pacific coverage — watching weather across the vast Pacific theater from Alaska and Hawaii to the South Pacific. One satellite's retirement became another mission's foundation.

36413.d: Lead satellite of the Yaogan-9 triplet, launched March 2010 — the first-ever Chinese naval ocean surveillance satellite formation and the direct predecessor to the later, more numerous Yaogan-25 and Yaogan-30 ELINT triplet families. Flying in a tight three-satellite formation, Yaogan-9's group uses time-difference-of-arrival signal processing to triangulate the location of ships broadcasting radar or radio signals across the Pacific — the founding demonstration of a naval surveillance technique China has since proliferated across five additional triplet generations.
36413.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (JB-8 Founding Triplet)

36414.d: Second satellite of the pioneering Yaogan-9 triplet, part of China's first operational demonstration of satellite-based naval signals triangulation. The techniques proven by this formation directly informed the design of every subsequent Yaogan ELINT triplet generation — Yaogan-16, 17, 20, 25, and 30 — that followed over the next decade.
36414.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (JB-8 Founding Triplet)

36415.d: Third satellite completing the original Yaogan-9 triplet, the formation that proved China's naval ocean surveillance triangulation concept in 2010. A full generation of Chinese ELINT satellite triplets — spanning Yaogan-9, 16, 17, 20, 25, and 30 — traces its operational lineage directly back to this founding three-satellite group.
36415.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (JB-8 Founding Triplet)

36499.d: An EchoStar high-power satellite at 119°W — the most powerful Ku-band satellite in North America when launched. EchoStar-14's extraordinary transmit power enables reception with tiny satellite antennas, making DISH service practical for RV owners, truck drivers with compact mobile dishes, and apartment dwellers where full-size dishes are prohibited. The 119°W position extends DISH's coverage to complement the 110°W primary position.

36508.d: CryoSat-2 has one job: weigh the world's ice. Using a radar altimeter precise enough to measure ice thickness to within centimeters, it monitors the polar ice caps, glaciers, and sea ice across both poles. Its data showed the Arctic lost roughly 4,000 cubic kilometers of sea ice volume in the decade after its 2010 launch — a finding that directly feeds into sea level rise projections. ESA's most continuously operated Earth science satellite.

36516.d: An SES satellite at 101°W providing C-band and Ku-band services for North American cable and broadcasting markets. SES-1 sits at a prime US cable distribution position, carrying programming for cable networks distributed to headends across the United States. SES (originally Société Européenne des Satellites) grew from a single Astra satellite in 1988 to one of the world's largest satellite operators — its Galaxy acquisition brought it into the US cable distribution business.

36581.d: An Astra satellite at 23.5°E serving Central and Eastern European markets with both Ku-band DTH broadcasting and Ka-band broadband. The 23.5°E position provides a complementary satellite neighborhood to the main Astra 19.2°E cluster, offering additional coverage options for Central European broadcasters and allowing SES to serve markets where 19.2°E isn't the optimal position for signal strength.

36582.d: The companion to COMSATBw-1, together providing Germany with continuous global military communications coverage. COMSATBw-2 carries additional Ka-band capacity for high-data-rate applications. Germany's investment in dedicated military satellites reflected the Bundeswehr's transition from a territorial defense force to an expeditionary military capable of sustained global operations.

36590.d: One of BeiDou-2's geostationary navigation satellites, part of China's regional navigation system. BeiDou's GEO component is unique among global navigation constellations — GPS, GLONASS, and Galileo use only medium Earth orbit satellites, but BeiDou incorporates GEO and inclined geosynchronous orbits to provide stronger, more reliable signals across the Asia-Pacific, where the GEO satellites appear at favorable elevation angles.

36592.d: Part of Arabsat's flagship 26°E position, carrying additional capacity for the Arab world's most-concentrated broadcasting neighborhood. Badr-5 provides extended C-band coverage deeper into Sub-Saharan Africa alongside its Ku-band Arab broadcasting role — making 26°E a content hub that reaches not just Arab nations but African countries whose populations access Arabic satellite television as an alternative to scarce local broadcasting.

36605.d: Flying as close as 150 meters from TerraSAR-X, TanDEM-X performs radar interferometry with two spacecraft acting as one giant antenna. Together they produced the TanDEM-X World DEM: the most precise global elevation model ever made, measuring Earth's surface height to within 2 meters everywhere on the planet. Every global terrain visualization you've ever seen uses this dataset.

36744.d: South Korea's first geostationary satellite combined three missions: weather observation, ocean monitoring, and communications. It covered Asia-Pacific with half-hourly weather imagery while simultaneously monitoring ocean color and supporting maritime communications — three jobs from one spacecraft parked 36,000 km up.

36745.d: A major Arab world communications satellite, operated by the Arab Satellite Communications Organization since 1976 — one of the oldest satellite operators in the developing world. Arabsat-5A provides C and Ku-band services for direct-to-home television broadcasting, corporate networks, and government communications across 22 Arab nations. Arabsat's pan-Arab mandate — providing sovereign satellite capacity independent of Western commercial providers — remains as relevant today as when it was founded during the early satellite era.

36792.d: An EchoStar satellite at 61.5°W providing direct-broadcast coverage for Puerto Rico and the Caribbean. EchoStar-15's position at 61.5°W optimizes coverage for Caribbean markets where DISH and EchoStar compete for satellite television subscribers. Puerto Rico's 3 million US citizens and the broader Caribbean tourist market make this coverage area commercially significant for US direct-to-home satellite operators.

36795.d: The third Cartosat-2 series satellite maintains India's uninterrupted sub-meter mapping capability. India's Cartosat program has produced the most comprehensive high-resolution map dataset assembled by a developing nation — used for defense, urban planning, agricultural policy, and disaster management across a country of 1.4 billion people.

36828.d: The first BeiDou-2 Inclined Geosynchronous Orbit satellite — a unique orbital slot that combines GEO altitude with a ground track that oscillates north-south over Asia rather than remaining fixed over the equator. This inclined orbit provides stronger signal elevation angles over high-latitude regions of China including Beijing and northeastern China, where GPS signals from equatorial GEO satellites appear at shallow angles that can be blocked by buildings and terrain.

36830.d: Egypt's main broadcasting satellite, carrying the highest concentration of Arabic-language TV channels available to viewers across North Africa and the Middle East. NileSat-201 hosts hundreds of Arabic channels at 7°W — an orbital position that has become the primary gateway for Egyptian and Arab television reaching diaspora communities in Europe and the Americas. Egyptian state television, independent channels, and regional broadcasters all use NileSat to reach the massive Arabic-speaking population.

36831.d: The Regional African Satellite Communications replacement satellite, an organization created by 44 African nations to provide sovereign satellite communications across the continent. RASCOM-QAF 1R provides C and Ku-band services serving rural communities across Africa with telephone access, internet connectivity, and television broadcasting. RASCOM represents Africa's attempt at satellite sovereignty — providing communications infrastructure that doesn't depend on European or American operators who have historically dominated African satellite markets.

36834.d: An electro-optical imaging satellite in China's Yaogan series. The complete Yaogan fleet produces a space-based picture of global activity unprecedented for any nation outside the United States — central to China's monitoring of the Taiwan Strait, South China Sea, and Belt and Road infrastructure projects worldwide.

36868.d: The first Advanced Extremely High Frequency satellite — MILSTAR's successor and the most capable protected military communications satellite ever built. AEHF provides 10 times more throughput than MILSTAR while being even more jam-resistant and survivable. A single AEHF satellite can support more users than the entire MILSTAR constellation combined. It also supports allied nations: Canada, the Netherlands, Australia, and the UK all use AEHF capacity. After launch in 2010, a thruster failure almost stranded it in the wrong orbit — engineers spent 14 months using backup thrusters to nurse it to the correct altitude.

37165.d: Yaogan-11, the fourth satellite in China's JB-6 optical reconnaissance family, launched September 2010. Each successive Yaogan-2/4/7/11/24/30 generation has incrementally improved resolution and coverage, part of the systematic, unhurried expansion pattern China has applied to nearly every category of military reconnaissance satellite since the mid-2000s.

37168.d: SBSS-1 (USA 216) — the pathfinder satellite for the US military's Space Based Space Surveillance program, and the first American satellite built specifically to watch other satellites from orbit rather than watch Earth. Carrying a 30 cm telescope on a two-axis gimbal, SBSS was designed to examine every object in geosynchronous orbit at least once per day, replacing a surveillance role previously handled by the Midcourse Space Experiment. The $823 million program suffered years of launch delays before finally reaching orbit in September 2010, and its data now feeds directly into the US Space Surveillance Network's catalog of active satellites and debris.
37168.a: US Space Force / Boeing / Ball Aerospace

37179.d: Lead satellite of the Shijian-6 04 pair, launched in 2010 to extend China's long-running space radiation monitoring program. By this fourth generation, Shijian-6 data had been feeding Chinese satellite design standards for radiation hardening for the better part of a decade, part of the unglamorous but essential research underlying every operational Chinese spacecraft's ability to survive its mission life.

37180.d: Companion satellite to Shijian-6 04A, completing the fourth Shijian-6 generation pair. Together the two spacecraft continue mapping how single-event upsets — radiation-induced electronic glitches — vary across different orbital positions and altitudes, directly informing component selection for later-generation Chinese satellites operating in harsher radiation environments.

37185.d: The backup and supplemental satellite for SiriusXM's satellite radio service — the system that provides uninterrupted music, news, and sports to 30+ million subscribers across North America. XM-5 ensures that SiriusXM subscribers never lose signal even when moving through areas where one primary satellite might be obscured. It carries the full SiriusXM channel lineup and can assume primary coverage duties if either of the main GEO satellites experiences technical problems.

37188.d: Part of Globalstar's 52°-inclined second-generation constellation, contributing L and S-band relay capacity that in 2022 became the invisible infrastructure behind one of the most consequential consumer safety features Apple has ever shipped — the ability for any iPhone 14 or later to text emergency services from literally anywhere on the surface of the Earth.

37189.d: One of Globalstar's 24 second-generation satellites, launched in the early 2010s to modernize a network originally built for satellite phones and asset tracking. A decade later, this same constellation would be reserved almost entirely — 85% of its capacity — for Apple's Emergency SOS service, redirecting a legacy satellite phone business toward consumer smartphone safety features nobody anticipated when these satellites launched.

37190.d: Part of Globalstar's second-generation LEO constellation, relaying voice and data communications for maritime, aviation, and industrial customers alongside its newer role supporting Apple's iPhone satellite messaging. In April 2026, Amazon announced it would acquire Globalstar entirely, folding its satellites and spectrum into the Amazon Leo broadband network — a consolidation that will eventually see satellites like this one succeeded by Amazon-built replacements.

37191.d: One of Globalstar's second-generation satellites, part of a 24-satellite constellation that transmits over 1.8 billion low-bandwidth messages annually. Its coverage notably weakens near the poles and high Arctic — a gap that matters little for its core Apple Emergency SOS customer base, since iPhone users triggering emergency satellite texts are rarely doing so from Greenland's ice sheet.

37192.d: Part of Globalstar's constellation supporting mobile satellite phone, asset tracking, and — since 2022 — Apple's Emergency SOS via satellite. The company's satellites use simple analog bent-pipe relay technology rather than complex on-board digital processing, a design choice that has kept the constellation reliable and cost-effective for over 15 years of continuous operation.

37193.d: One of Globalstar's second-generation satellites, launched as part of the company's response to a near-catastrophic failure of its original 1990s constellation, whose solar-panel-mounted amplifiers degraded years ahead of schedule. This satellite's continued operation underpins both legacy satellite phone customers and the newer, much larger population of iPhone users relying on Emergency SOS via satellite.

37207.d: A Japanese direct-to-home broadcasting satellite at 110°E — one of the most-watched orbital positions in Japan. BSAT satellites deliver Japanese national broadcast services including NHK's public broadcasts and commercial channels directly to home dish antennas. Japan's 4K Ultra HD broadcasting began on BSAT satellites, making Japan the world's first country to offer regular 4K satellite TV programming to home viewers.

37210.d: A BeiDou-2 geostationary navigation satellite providing positioning and Short Message Service across Asia. China has made BeiDou's Short Message Service a distinctive feature — the ability to send and receive text messages through the navigation satellite itself, not through a separate cellular network, has made BeiDou the preferred navigation system for Chinese fishing fleets, mountaineering expeditions, and military units operating beyond cellular coverage.

37214.d: FY-3B expanded China's polar weather capabilities with improved microwave humidity sounders that profile water vapor through clouds — critical for tropical cyclone intensity forecasting. It feeds China's global numerical weather prediction system, which now rivals Western models in accuracy over Asia and the western Pacific.

37216.d: The fourth original COSMO-SkyMed satellite completed Italy's radar constellation with sub-12-hour global revisit. It has since supported NATO operations, tracked illegal fishing in the Mediterranean, and helped authorities detect unauthorized construction on protected coastlines — all with radar that sees through clouds and darkness.

37218.d: A large geostationary satellite intended to provide mobile satellite phone services across North America, designed to communicate with handheld devices using a massive 22-meter antenna. SkyTerra's company went bankrupt before the service launched, and the satellite was acquired by LightSquared and later Ligado Networks. SkyTerra-1 represents the generation of large-antenna GEO mobile satellite concepts that preceded the current generation of LEO direct-to-device constellations.

37224.d: O/OREOS (USA 219) — a NASA CubeSat about the size of a loaf of bread that asked one of astrobiology's oldest questions using one of its smallest instruments. Launched November 2010, O/OREOS carried two independent experiments in a single 5.5 kg satellite: one testing whether live microorganisms could survive space's radiation and temperature extremes, the other monitoring how organic molecules — the building blocks of life — degrade when directly exposed to the space environment. It was NASA's first CubeSat ever built to run two completely separate science experiments autonomously, and its six-month mission helped establish that low-cost nanosatellites could deliver genuine astrobiology research, not just technology demonstrations.

37225.d: FASTSAT-HSV01 (USA 220), a NASA-Air Force microsatellite about the size of a small refrigerator, launched in November 2010 to host six independent science and technology experiments on a single modular platform — a 'satellite bus' concept designed to prove that multiple unrelated experiments could share one spacecraft economically. FASTSAT's most famous cargo was NanoSail-D, a tiny CubeSat it carried and later ejected, which went on to successfully deploy one of the first solar sails ever unfurled in low Earth orbit — a thin reflective membrane that used sunlight pressure itself as a source of thrust.
37225.a: NASA / US Air Force Space Test Program

37237.d: Europe's first dedicated high-throughput Ka-band broadband satellite, operated by Avanti Communications. HYLAS-1 ('Highly Adaptable Satellite') brought satellite broadband to underserved communities in the UK, Ireland, and across Europe with a flexible payload that could be reconfigured to concentrate capacity where demand was highest. Avanti pioneered the European satellite broadband market for rural and enterprise customers who lack terrestrial broadband alternatives.

37238.d: An Intelsat C-band and Ku-band satellite at 66°E serving the Indian Ocean, South Asia, and East Africa. IS-17 is the primary Intelsat asset for serving the Indian subcontinent and East African markets — providing broadcast distribution to Indian cable operators and broadband connectivity to enterprise users across a region stretching from Kenya to Bangladesh.

37256.d: Part of BeiDou-2's inclined geosynchronous orbit component. IGSO satellites were a Chinese innovation in navigation constellation design — the combination of GEO, IGSO, and MEO satellites gives BeiDou higher signal strength over Asia than any other navigation system, making it particularly effective in dense urban canyons where signals from lower-elevation satellites are frequently blocked.

37258.d: Europe's first dedicated high-throughput Ka-band broadband satellite, delivering up to 90 Gbps of residential internet capacity across Europe and the Mediterranean. KA-SAT pioneered the consumer satellite broadband market in Europe — bringing rural French farmhouses, Greek island communities, and remote Scandinavian villages into the broadband era with download speeds competitive with early DSL. When Russian forces cut Ukraine's ViaSat network in February 2022, KA-SAT was one of the satellites affected — highlighting how critical satellite broadband had become to Ukraine's wartime communications.

37264.d: Hispasat's primary satellite for the Americas at 30°W, providing direct-to-home broadcasting and broadband across Latin America and the Iberian Peninsula. Hispasat-30W-5 serves the massive Brazilian and Hispanic Latin American television and internet markets, distributing programming to cable head-ends, DTH platforms, and VSAT networks across the largest satellite services market in the developing world.

37265.d: South Korea's KT SAT communications satellite providing Ku-band services for Korean domestic markets and maritime communications across northeast Asian waters. KoreaSat-6 supports South Korean broadcasting while serving maritime users in the Yellow Sea, Sea of Japan, and East China Sea — waters that see some of the world's heaviest commercial and military vessel traffic, all requiring satellite communications for navigation safety and operational coordination.

37384.d: A BeiDou-2 inclined geosynchronous orbit navigation satellite providing enhanced coverage over East Asia. The IGSO orbital component was specifically designed to address China's military requirement for high-availability navigation over the Asia-Pacific theater — ensuring Chinese forces have reliable precision navigation signals even in environments where GPS might be disrupted by adversary jamming.

37387.d: India's second-generation resource satellite monitors agricultural conditions across the subcontinent. Its cameras feed India's national crop estimation system — a satellite-driven program that guides food security policy for 1.4 billion people and agricultural export decisions affecting world grain markets.

37389.d: Singapore's first satellite and a milestone for Southeast Asia's emergence in space. Built collaboratively by Nanyang Technological University and the DSO National Laboratories, X-SAT carries an optical imager capable of 10-meter resolution — enough to track urban development, deforestation, and environmental changes across the region. At just 105 kg, it demonstrated that small nations can build capable Earth observation spacecraft.

37392.d: An Intelsat satellite providing Ku-band capacity for South America from 21.5°W. IS-28 covers Brazil and the South Atlantic — one of Intelsat's prime positions for Brazilian market services. The satellite supports Brazilian broadcasters distributing content to remote communities across the Amazon basin and Mato Grosso, where terrestrial broadcast towers cannot economically serve the scattered population.

37393.d: UAE's primary government and commercial communications satellite, operated by Yahsat — the Abu Dhabi government-controlled satellite operator. YahSat-1A provides Ka, Ku, C, and X-band services for Emirati government communications, military connectivity, and commercial broadband across the Middle East, Africa, and Europe. The X-band payload specifically serves UAE and allied military communications — Yahsat has grown into one of the few non-US operators providing military satellite communications to NATO-aligned forces.

37481.d: The first Space-Based Infrared System satellite — the most sensitive missile warning spacecraft ever built. SBIRS watches the entire Earth with infrared sensors powerful enough to detect the heat signature of a ballistic missile within seconds of launch, anywhere on the planet. It replaced the aging Defense Support Program satellites and provides the US with its first warning of any ICBM, SLBM, or theater ballistic missile launch in real time. The satellite that tells the President a nuclear attack has been launched.

37602.d: A Telesat satellite at 63°W serving Latin American communications markets — the replacement for a satellite that suffered a partial antenna deployment failure. Telstar-14R's successful operation restored full Ku-band capacity for Brazilian and South American telecommunications operators who depend on this orbital slot for regional connectivity. Brazil is one of the most important markets in Telesat's global satellite fleet.

37605.d: An Indian communications and navigation satellite that achieved two firsts simultaneously. GSAT-8 was India's first satellite to operate Ka-band transponders for high-throughput broadband, and it carried the first GAGAN (GPS-Aided Geo Augmented Navigation) operational payload providing precision approach guidance to Indian airports. GAGAN, built jointly by ISRO and the Airports Authority of India, makes every Indian airport capable of satellite-guided precision landings — a critical safety upgrade for the country's rapidly expanding aviation sector.

37606.d: A joint Singapore-Taiwan satellite providing C and Ku-band services across Southeast Asia and the Indian Ocean. ST-2 covers the Strait of Malacca — one of the world's most strategically important shipping chokepoints, through which 25% of global seaborne trade passes annually. Maritime satellite communications for the thousands of vessels transiting Singapore's waters, combined with broadcasting and enterprise services across Southeast Asia, make ST-2's position at 88°E commercially and strategically significant.

37677.d: A Chinese communications satellite providing C and Ku-band connectivity for government and commercial customers across China, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific. Zhongxing satellites serve the dual purpose of supporting Chinese state broadcasting internationally while carrying commercial voice, data, and video services for corporate and government customers across Asia.

37739.d: Part of Globalstar's LEO constellation, one of 24 satellites launched in the early 2010s that now carries the critical infrastructure load of Apple's iPhone Emergency SOS feature — a service credited with helping rescue stranded hikers, crash survivors, and travelers in areas with zero cellular coverage since its 2022 launch.

37740.d: One of Globalstar's second-generation satellites, contributing capacity to a constellation now more famous for the invisible role it plays inside hundreds of millions of iPhones than for its original satellite phone business. When an iPhone user triggers Emergency SOS via satellite with no cellular signal, satellites like this one are what receives that message.

37741.d: Part of Globalstar's 52°-inclined constellation, launched to replace a first-generation fleet whose premature degradation nearly ended the company. Its second life, powering Apple's Emergency SOS service since 2022, has proven far more consequential than its original satellite phone mission ever was.

37742.d: One of Globalstar's second-generation satellites, launched by Thales Alenia Space in the early 2010s. Following Amazon's April 2026 agreement to acquire Globalstar and its spectrum licenses, satellites like this one will eventually be succeeded by new spacecraft built by MDA Space as part of the expanding Amazon Leo network.

37743.d: Part of Globalstar's mobile satellite services constellation, providing voice, data, and — since 2022 — critical emergency messaging capacity for Apple devices. The network transmits over 1.8 billion messages annually across six continents, a scale that would have seemed extraordinary when these satellites were designed purely as a satellite phone replacement fleet.

37744.d: One of Globalstar's 24 second-generation satellites, launched to restore a constellation that came close to commercial collapse in the mid-2000s. Its subsequent selection as the exclusive satellite partner for Apple's iPhone Emergency SOS feature in 2022 transformed Globalstar from a struggling niche satellite phone operator into critical global safety infrastructure.

37748.d: An SES satellite at 103°W providing mixed C-band and Ku-band services for North American markets. SES-3 carries both traditional C-band cable distribution capacity and Ku-band for direct broadcast and enterprise services — reflecting the dual-purpose nature of SES's North American fleet, which serves both the legacy cable distribution business and the emerging direct-to-consumer satellite services market.

37749.d: Kazakhstan's second communications satellite, providing Ku-band services for Kazakh government networks and commercial customers. Kazakhstan's vast territory — 9th largest country on Earth — and sparse terrestrial infrastructure make satellite communications essential. KazSat-2 carries government-reserved capacity for national security and public administration alongside commercial services for corporate and consumer customers across Kazakhstan's enormous steppes.

37763.d: Part of BeiDou-2's regional navigation constellation. The accumulation of multiple IGSO satellites provides BeiDou with more simultaneous navigation signals over Asia than GPS — meaning Chinese users typically have more satellite signals available for positioning, improving both accuracy and reliability, particularly in challenging reception environments like cities and mountain valleys.

37775.d: An Astra satellite at 19.2°E — the fourth spacecraft SES Astra maintains at this orbital position simultaneously. Having multiple satellites at 19.2°E provides redundancy that protects the European DTH market against a single satellite failure that would otherwise knock out television service for hundreds of millions of viewers. Astra-1N's addition also provided the headroom for Ultra HD broadcasting at 19.2°E — the next-generation video standard now being rolled out across European satellite TV.

37776.d: A joint B-SAT and JCSAT satellite at 110°E providing expanded capacity for Japan's most critical broadcasting position. The 110°E slot is where Japanese households point their satellite dishes for national broadcasting — any failure at this position would disrupt television reception for millions of viewers. BSAT-3C provides essential backup and additional capacity for Japan's broadcasting resilience.

37779.d: Pakistan's primary national communications satellite, providing direct-to-home TV broadcasting and broadband connectivity across Pakistan, the Middle East, and South Asia. PakSat-1R is operated by SUPARCO — Pakistan's space agency — and carries capacity reserved for the Pakistan military alongside commercial services. For a nuclear-armed nation in a complex regional security environment, sovereign satellite communications represent both a practical necessity and a strategic capability.

37781.d: China's first ocean dynamics satellite measures sea surface height, wind, temperature, and wave height simultaneously. These parameters describe how the ocean stores and redistributes solar heat — ultimately driving the weather patterns affecting every person on Earth. HY-2A built the baseline for China's growing ocean science program.

37791.d: Turkey's first domestically developed satellite, built entirely by Turkish engineers at TUBITAK UZAY. It monitors Turkey's agriculture and coastlines — and contributed imagery for emergency response after the devastating 2023 Kahramanmaraş earthquakes. RASAT is the foundation of Turkey's ambition to become a satellite technology exporter.

37792.d: Expanding SpaceQuest's satellite messaging constellation. AprizesSat satellites popularized the 'bent pipe' IoT relay model — devices send bursts to overhead satellites that store the data and dump it to ground stations when visible, enabling asset tracking anywhere on Earth with simple, low-power transmitters.

37793.d: The sixth AprizesSat satellite continuing the orbital IoT data relay network. AprizesSat customers have included Antarctic research stations, Amazon monitoring programs, and migratory animal tracking projects — any application needing occasional small data bursts from places where continuous connectivity is impossible or uneconomic.

37809.d: An SES satellite at 87°W serving the eastern United States and Canada with C-band and Ku-band capacity. SES-2 provides the cable distribution backbone for eastern North American markets — the densest concentration of cable television subscribers in the world, from New England through the Mid-Atlantic to the southeastern US. Its Ku-band capacity also serves government and enterprise customers operating in the eastern US corridor.
37809.t: US East Coast Cable / Commercial Communications

37810.d: An Arabsat satellite providing capacity specifically focused on Gulf Cooperation Council countries and North Africa. Arabsat-5C carries high-power Ku-band transponders for direct-to-home broadcasting in Saudi Arabia, UAE, Egypt, and surrounding countries — serving the Arab world's enormous appetite for satellite television, where per-household TV viewing rates exceed most global averages.

37816.d: A Eutelsat satellite at 7°W focused on Arabic broadcasting for North Africa and the Middle East — complementing the Nilesat system at the same orbital neighborhood. The 7°W position is the Arab world's second most important satellite television position after Arabsat at 26°E, carrying hundreds of Arabic channels reaching North African audiences. Eutelsat and Nilesat's co-presence at 7°W creates a dense Arabic-language media neighborhood visible across the Mediterranean.

37826.d: A SES satellite providing direct-to-home television for Mexico and Latin America under the QuetzSat brand. Named after the Quetzal — the iridescent bird sacred to Maya and Aztec civilizations and the symbol of freedom in Central American culture — QuetzSat-1 delivers satellite television as an alternative to Mexico's cable-dominant pay TV market. The satellite serves both urban subscribers seeking more channels and rural Mexican communities where cable infrastructure doesn't reach.

37834.d: An Intelsat satellite serving the Pacific Ocean and Pacific Island nations with C-band and Ku-band capacity. IS-18's Pacific position is critical for island nations stretching from Hawaii to Fiji to Micronesia — communities separated by thousands of kilometers of ocean where satellite is the only practical communications link. Intelsat's Pacific fleet carries not just entertainment but emergency communications, government operations, and the internet connections that connect Pacific islands to the world.

37836.d: A Eutelsat satellite at 16°E providing Ku-band services across Central and Eastern Europe, including coverage of Ukraine, Poland, the Baltic states, and Southeastern Europe. Before satellite internet became ubiquitous, Eutelsat-16A was the primary broadband delivery vehicle for internet service providers serving rural Eastern European communities. Its position provides a coverage footprint that has become increasingly significant for communications during the Ukraine conflict.

37841.d: India's SRM University student satellite carries a greenhouse gas sensor studying CO2 and water vapor distribution in Earth's atmosphere — combining educational objectives with real scientific contribution. A model for how university satellite programs can simultaneously train engineers and advance science, building India's deep bench of space engineering talent.

37843.d: The satellite that proved high-throughput GEO broadband could compete with fiber. When ViaSat-1 launched in 2011, its 140 Gbps Ka-band capacity exceeded the combined capacity of all commercial communications satellites serving North America at that time — a 10-fold increase in a single spacecraft. The resulting Exede internet service brought affordable broadband to millions of rural Americans who had no other option. ViaSat-1 made it economically possible for rural families to access streaming video — a social equalizer that changed the meaning of rural life in America.

37849.d: Named after Verner Suomi, the father of satellite meteorology, Suomi NPP bridges NASA science and NOAA operations. Its VIIRS instrument captures the sharpest daily global view of Earth's surface of any operational weather satellite — and its 'Black Marble' nighttime images became the most shared Earth science photographs ever published. It processes 4 terabytes of data every day.

37875.d: Yaogan-12, part of the JB-10 series alongside Yaogan-5 and Yaogan-21 — Chinese reconnaissance satellites whose orbital characteristics and launch pattern closely mirror known SAR or optical imaging missions. Launched in 2011, Yaogan-12 continues China's steady cadence of military Earth observation satellite deployments feeding an increasingly comprehensive space-based intelligence architecture.

37930.d: The third Chuangxin technology demonstration satellite, launched in 2011 to continue validating small-satellite bus technologies and data relay capabilities for the Chinese Academy of Sciences. By this point the Chuangxin program had shifted from purely experimental status toward informing the design choices behind larger operational Chinese satellite constellations — a common pattern in Chinese space development where small, low-risk missions validate technology before it's scaled into major programs.

37933.d: A high-power AsiaSat commercial satellite serving both direct-to-home broadcasting and VSAT broadband customers across Asia. AsiaSat-7's C-band payload provides the wide-area coverage that broadcasters need while its Ku-band transponders serve enterprise network customers connecting offices, factories, and retail locations across China and Southeast Asia.

37948.d: The fifth BeiDou-2 IGSO satellite, completing the regional coverage needed for BeiDou's standalone navigation service across the Asia-Pacific. By 2012, the BeiDou-2 constellation of GEO and IGSO satellites was providing navigation services to China and neighboring countries — making China the first nation besides the US and Russia to deploy an independent regional navigation system in full operation.

37951.d: Russia's first modern data relay satellite, designed to provide communications links for Russian spacecraft in low Earth orbit — including cosmonauts on the ISS and robotic scientific missions. Luch-5A functions as Russia's equivalent to NASA's TDRS system, relaying data from the Russian segment of the ISS to mission control in Moscow without needing a direct line of sight to Russian ground stations.

38012.d: France's 50 cm commercial imaging satellite documents war damage, maps disaster zones, and monitors illegal deforestation in near real-time. Together with Pleiades-1B, it can image any target on Earth within 24 hours — responsiveness previously achievable only by military satellites. Commercial imaging has entered every domain previously exclusive to government programs.

38014.d: Nigeria's communications satellite — Africa's most populous nation's connection to sovereign satellite capacity. NigComSat-1R provides C, Ku, Ka, and L-band services for Nigerian government networks, direct-to-home TV distribution, broadband internet, and mobile satellite services. Nigeria's original NigComSat-1 failed in 2008, and this replacement satellite marked Nigeria's second attempt to establish indigenous satellite capability — critical for a nation of 200 million people in a region where commercial satellite capacity is chronically underserved.

38038.d: China's land resources satellite provides multispectral and panchromatic imagery for geological mapping and resource assessment across China's vast territory. The Ziyuan ('Resource') series represents China's investment in space-based sovereign resource monitoring — tracking its own forests, minerals, water resources, and agricultural land from orbit.

38040.d: Part of Globalstar's second-generation LEO constellation, relaying data for asset tracking, maritime communications, and Apple's iPhone Emergency SOS network. Apple has invested over $700 million total in Globalstar's infrastructure to support the feature, including a $252 million loan specifically to help fund constellation replenishment satellites.

38041.d: One of Globalstar's second-generation satellites, contributing to a network whose most famous customer — Apple — didn't exist as a partner when these satellites were designed and launched. The 2022 Emergency SOS partnership redirected the entire commercial trajectory of a company that had spent the 2000s and early 2010s as a struggling satellite phone also-ran.

38042.d: Part of Globalstar's constellation, one of 24 satellites supporting mobile communications and Apple's Emergency SOS service. In April 2026, Amazon announced an agreement to acquire Globalstar entirely and merge its operations into Amazon Leo — meaning satellites like this one represent the final generation of Globalstar-branded spacecraft before Amazon ownership begins.

38043.d: One of Globalstar's second-generation satellites, launched in the early 2010s and now approaching the end of its originally planned 15-year design life. Its successor satellites, funded partly by Apple and now planned under Amazon's ownership, will maintain the Emergency SOS service that has become Globalstar's primary reason for existing.

38044.d: Part of Globalstar's LEO mobile satellite constellation, relaying communications for maritime, industrial, and consumer smartphone emergency use. The constellation's simple bent-pipe satellite design — receive, amplify, retransmit, no onboard processing — has proven remarkably durable and cost-effective across 15+ years of continuous operation.

38045.d: One of Globalstar's second-generation satellites, part of the constellation reserving 85% of its total capacity for Apple's iPhone Emergency SOS via satellite feature. Since the service's 2022 launch, Apple has publicized cases of the feature contributing to real rescues — stranded hikers, crash victims, and travelers located and saved using exactly this kind of satellite link.

38070.d: The fourth WGS satellite, built to an enhanced Block II design with 4.9 GHz of instantaneous bandwidth. WGS-4 supports operations in the Pacific Command area of responsibility — covering the vast Pacific Ocean from Hawaii to the South China Sea, a region that has grown in strategic importance as US military posture shifts toward the Indo-Pacific.

38077.d: The densest known object orbiting Earth — a solid tungsten alloy sphere 36 cm in diameter weighing 386 kg. LARES tests general relativity: specifically, the 'frame-dragging' effect where Earth's rotation drags spacetime itself along with it. By tracking LARES with extreme precision, scientists can measure this relativistic effect to within 1% — one of the most precise tests of Einstein's general theory ever performed.

38087.d: An SES C-band and Ku-band satellite at 22°W serving the Atlantic Ocean region including Europe, the Americas, and Africa. SES-4 occupies an Atlantic position that allows simultaneous coverage of three continents — a coverage geometry valuable for maritime communications across the Atlantic shipping lanes, for connecting European businesses with their American counterparts, and for distributing broadcasting to audiences that span the Atlantic.

38091.d: Part of BeiDou-2's geostationary component, providing navigation coverage across the Asia-Pacific. BeiDou GEO satellites contribute to the positioning accuracy achievable across the region by providing additional pseudorange measurements from fixed, precisely-known orbital positions — complementing the moving MEO satellites that form the core navigation network.

38093.d: The first Mobile User Objective System satellite — the US Navy's 4G LTE network in space. MUOS provides smartphone-like connectivity to military users in the field, at sea, and in the air, replacing the aging UHF Follow-On system. Sailors, marines, and special operations forces can now access secure voice and data through handheld terminals not much larger than a consumer satellite phone. MUOS represented a generational leap in military tactical communications.

38098.d: An Intelsat satellite at 72°E serving the Indian Ocean, South Asia, Africa, and Australia simultaneously. IS-22's UHF military payload serves Australian Defence Force communications alongside commercial services — an unusual dual-purpose arrangement that gives Australia's military satellite communications on a commercially operated spacecraft. The commercial C-band and Ku-band capacity serves media, enterprise, and maritime users across the Indian Ocean.

38107.d: A Chinese commercial satellite serving the Asia-Pacific broadcasting and broadband markets. APStar satellites are operated by Asia Pacific Satellite, one of China's oldest commercial satellite operators, providing C and Ku-band services to customers across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and Australia. APStar has built a strong commercial position in markets where Chinese diplomatic relationships and competitive pricing create advantages over Western satellite operators.

38245.d: The companion to Yahsat-1A, focused on high-throughput Ka-band broadband across Sub-Saharan Africa. YahSat-1B launched the YahClick satellite internet service — bringing affordable broadband to remote African communities in countries like Nigeria, South Africa, and Tanzania where terrestrial fiber serves only major cities. UAE's Yahsat has positioned itself as Africa's most important satellite internet provider, filling a connectivity gap where European and American operators have been slower to invest.

38254.d: The second AEHF satellite, completing the first pair needed for global protected communications coverage. AEHF-2 provides the US strategic nuclear command structure, President, Secretary of Defense, and combatant commanders worldwide with guaranteed communications that remain functional even during a nuclear exchange — resistant to jamming, nuclear EMP, and scintillation effects. Positioned at a different orbital slot from AEHF-1 to maximize global coverage.

38257.d: Yaogan-14, part of the JB-11 pair alongside Yaogan-28 — one of the smaller, more specialized Yaogan sub-families whose limited two-satellite membership suggests a narrower or more experimental mission scope than the larger triplet-based ELINT and imaging series. Launched in 2012, Yaogan-14's specific sensor payload remains undisclosed by Chinese authorities.

38331.d: A Japanese commercial satellite extending Sky Perfect JSAT's Ku-band coverage across the Asia-Pacific. JCSAT-13 serves Japan's maritime industry — one of the world's largest — providing vessel communications and broadband to ships operating across the Pacific and Indian Oceans. Japan's dependence on maritime trade for virtually all its natural resource imports makes satellite communications for shipping a matter of economic security.

38332.d: Vietnam's second satellite, providing additional capacity as Vietnam's digital economy has grown. VINASAT-2 supports Vietnam's expanding satellite television market, rural broadband programs, and maritime communications for Vietnam's extensive fishing fleet — which operates across some of the most contested maritime territory in the world in the South China Sea, where reliable communications are both practically and strategically important.

38337.d: Shizuku — Japanese for 'water droplet' — maps Earth's entire water cycle from orbit. Its microwave radiometer measures soil moisture, sea surface temperatures, snow cover, ocean wind speed, and atmospheric water vapor simultaneously. This data feeds weather models, flood prediction systems, and climate research. Japan designed Shizuku to partner with NASA's Aqua satellite, and the two spacecraft coordinate their observations to provide the most complete picture of Earth's water ever assembled.

38338.d: South Korea's sharpest commercial satellite delivers 70 cm imagery — detailed enough to identify individual vehicles and assess military facilities. It has been repeatedly used to monitor North Korea's nuclear and missile programs with detail previously available only to intelligence agencies with classified satellite access.

38342.d: Bell Canada's latest Nimiq satellite adding 4K Ultra HD capacity for the next generation of Canadian satellite television. Nimiq-6 positions Bell Satellite TV for the transition to 4K sports broadcasting — the application where satellite's nationwide reach and picture quality are most difficult for cable companies to match. Each 4K sports event requires roughly four times the satellite bandwidth of its HD predecessor, making new high-capacity satellites like Nimiq-6 essential infrastructure for the 4K era.

38352.d: A classified Chinese military communications satellite in geostationary orbit, operating under the Zhongxing designation which covers both commercial and government payloads. The 'A' variants in the Zhongxing numbering typically indicate military missions. Zhongxing-2A supports PLA strategic communications and is part of China's dedicated military satellite communications network distinct from the commercial Zhongxing fleet.

38354.d: Yaogan-15, launched in 2012 into a distinctive 100.7° inclination — a super-synchronous, slightly retrograde sun-synchronous orbit uncommon among Yaogan satellites. This unusual orbital geometry has led some analysts to assess Yaogan-15 as testing a different sensor type or coverage pattern than the broader Yaogan optical and SAR families.

38356.d: An Intelsat satellite covering the Asia-Pacific and Indian Ocean regions. IS-19's combined coverage footprint serves the high-growth media and broadband markets of Southeast Asia, China, and South Asia — regions where demand for satellite capacity is growing faster than ground infrastructure can be built. The satellite's Ku-band capacity serves direct-to-home broadcasting for markets with millions of satellite dish installations.

38358.d: NuSTAR opened a new window on the universe — high-energy X-rays that no previous telescope could focus into an image. It accomplishes this with a 10-meter focal length telescope that had to be extended by a mast after reaching orbit. NuSTAR has imaged the remnants of exploded stars, mapped the spin rates of black holes, and detected 'zombie' stars — neutron stars that should have stopped spinning but haven't. At just $165 million, it's one of NASA's most cost-effective science missions.

38551.d: A Hughes/EchoStar high-throughput Ka-band satellite delivering HughesNet Gen4 broadband across North America. EchoStar-17 pioneered the consumer Ka-band spot beam broadband architecture in the US market — delivering up to 50 Mbps residential internet to rural households that cable and DSL will never economically reach. Its 140 Gbps total throughput represented a tenfold increase over previous-generation satellites, proving that satellite broadband could be a genuine alternative to terrestrial services for rural Americans.

38552.d: Europe's rapid-scan weather satellite images Europe every 5 minutes during severe events — switching to 2.5-minute scans during crises. That extra temporal resolution gives meteorologists the frames needed to issue life-saving warnings with enough lead time for evacuation during flash floods and severe thunderstorm outbreaks.

38652.d: An SES satellite at 5°E providing Ku-band direct-to-home broadcasting for Scandinavia and sub-Saharan Africa. SES-5 occupies the 5°E position shared with the Astra-4A satellite, where its power is focused on Scandinavian DTH broadcasting — the Nordic countries have among Europe's highest rates of satellite television reception. The same satellite also provides an African beam, demonstrating how geostationary satellites can serve geographically disparate markets from a single orbital slot.

38709.d: A Canadian maritime surveillance satellite carrying an AIS receiver — listening for position broadcasts ships are legally required to transmit. From 650 km up, a single AIS satellite receives signals from ships across a 4,000 km wide swath, providing maritime domain awareness over vast stretches of ocean that shore-based stations cannot reach.

38734.d: Part of Russia's Gonets-M ('Herald') satellite messaging constellation — the Russian equivalent of ORBCOMM, providing store-and-forward data relay for remote sensors, vehicle tracking, and environmental monitoring across Russia's vast and often roadless territory. Gonets satellites are particularly important across Siberia and the Russian Arctic, where no cellular network will ever economically reach and satellite messaging remains the only practical connectivity option.

38736.d: One of the Gonets-M constellation satellites, relaying low-bandwidth data messages across Russia's 11 time zones. The system succeeded the earlier Gonets-D1 satellites and provides Russia with a sovereign messaging network for scientific expeditions, remote industrial sites, and Arctic shipping operations that cannot depend on any foreign satellite messaging system.

38740.d: A high-power Intelsat satellite at 68.5°E with C-band coverage spanning Africa, the Middle East, and Asia alongside high-power Ku-band beams for sub-Saharan Africa. IS-20 is Intelsat's primary vehicle for delivering broadcasting and broadband to sub-Saharan Africa — a continent where satellite remains the primary distribution platform for television and where internet access growth is among the fastest in the world.

38741.d: Avanti's second high-throughput Ka-band broadband satellite, extending coverage to Africa and additional European markets. HYLAS-2 targeted Sub-Saharan Africa's enormous unmet demand for affordable broadband — providing internet to businesses, institutions, and residential customers across markets where terrestrial infrastructure reaches only major cities. Avanti's African expansion positioned it as a pioneer in satellite-delivered digital inclusion for the continent.

38749.d: An Intelsat high-power satellite at 58°W serving the Americas with C-band and high-power Ku-band capacity. IS-21 is a primary Intelsat asset for Brazil — the world's fifth-largest country by area and a major satellite market. Its high-power Ku-band beams enable direct-to-home broadcasting across Brazil's vast interior, where terrestrial television struggles to reach and satellite is the standard entertainment medium for rural households.

38755.d: The first of two SPOT-6/7 satellites updating Europe's SPOT commercial imagery program, SPOT-6 delivers 1.5-meter imagery with a 60 km swath — wide enough to image entire metropolitan regions in a single pass. Together with SPOT-7, the pair can revisit any point on Earth daily, making the SPOT series relevant for the operational applications that define modern commercial Earth observation.

38767.d: One of The Aerospace Corporation's experimental technology demonstration CubeSats, part of a fleet dating back to 1999 that has quietly pioneered many of the CubeSat capabilities now taken for granted across the industry. AeroCube-4.5, launched 2012, built directly on AeroCube-4's breakthrough achievement — the first 3-axis stabilized attitude control system to fit in a single CubeSat unit, with 1-degree pointing accuracy. Aerospace Corporation's original AeroCube-4 went on to collect thousands of Earth images over an impressive seven-year mission life, convincing NASA and defense customers that CubeSats could execute genuine operational missions rather than remaining pure technology experiments.

38771.d: The backbone of European polar weather observations, MetOp-B carries the most comprehensive atmospheric sounding suite on any operational weather satellite. Its IASI instrument measures temperature and humidity profiles through 8,461 spectral channels simultaneously — compared to the handful previous sounders used. This data improves 24-hour forecasts by the equivalent of a full extra day of accuracy.

38778.d: The primary Astra satellite at 28.2°E, carrying Sky UK's main channel lineup including Sky Sports, Sky News, and Sky One to over 11 million UK subscribers. The 28.2°E position is so associated with Sky UK that British consumers simply call it 'the Sky satellite.' Astra-2F's UK spot beam was deliberately shaped to restrict reception in southern Europe, which caused controversy when British expatriates in Spain found they could no longer receive UK channels — a calculated sacrifice of coverage for rights management.

38779.d: India's comprehensive communications satellite carrying the second GAGAN navigation payload alongside C-band and Ku-band communications. GSAT-10's GAGAN instrument extended the satellite-based navigation augmentation network that provides precise landing guidance across India. With two GAGAN-equipped satellites in orbit, the system achieved the redundancy required for aviation safety certification — an air traffic management milestone that India's aviation regulatory authorities had been working toward for years.

38867.d: An Intelsat C-band and Ku-band satellite at 307.5°E serving North and South America and the Atlantic. IS-23's position in the western Atlantic provides coverage optimized for the US East Coast and Latin America — carrying video distribution for US programmers delivering content to Latin American cable operators, and corporate data networks connecting North American companies with their South American operations.

38953.d: The sixth BeiDou-2 geostationary satellite. China's BeiDou-2 system achieved full regional coverage of the Asia-Pacific in 2012 — providing GPS-equivalent navigation to Chinese military forces and civilian users without dependence on US GPS infrastructure. China's decision to build BeiDou was explicitly motivated by concerns that the US might deny GPS to China during a conflict.

38977.d: The second Luch-5 satellite, extending Russian orbital communications relay coverage. Luch-5B supports Russian-segment ISS operations and relays data from Russian Earth observation satellites to ground stations during the majority of their orbit when they're over oceans or foreign territory. Russia's data relay capability significantly reduces the number of ground stations required to operate its space program.

38978.d: A Yamal satellite providing both commercial and government communications across Russia and neighboring countries. The 300K designation reflects its Ka-band payload for broadband internet, a capability Gazprom added to reach residential customers in Russia's vast underserved regions. Russia has enormous unmet demand for rural broadband — Yamal satellites fill connectivity gaps across the world's largest country.

38991.d: Embratel Star One's C-band satellite supporting Latin American cable television distribution. Star One C3 serves Brazil's growing video distribution market as cable operators transition from analog to digital broadcasting. Brazil's extraordinarily competitive cable and satellite television market — with Oi, Claro, and Net competing alongside satellite providers — makes reliable satellite distribution infrastructure essential for content owners reaching national audiences.

38992.d: A Eutelsat satellite at 21.5°E providing Ku-band capacity for Eastern Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Eutelsat-21B's position provides direct coverage of Turkey, Egypt, and surrounding regions — high-growth markets for both satellite television and broadband. The satellite serves as a distribution platform for regional broadcasters reaching audiences across the Eastern Mediterranean and Black Sea region.

39008.d: An EchoStar satellite at 61.5°W providing high-power Ku-band capacity for DISH service in the Caribbean and Latin America. EchoStar-16 carries advanced spot beams that provide DISH-quality coverage across Caribbean islands that are often too far from EchoStar's primary North American satellites for reliable service. The Caribbean is a growing market for US satellite television services, particularly on American territories and popular tourist destinations.

39011.d: Lead satellite of the Yaogan-16 triplet, launched November 2012 as the second generation of China's naval ELINT surveillance formation following the pioneering Yaogan-9 group. Yaogan-16 refined the triangulation geometry and signal processing first demonstrated two years earlier, expanding China's ability to geolocate naval radar and communications emissions across a wider stretch of the Pacific.

39012.d: Second satellite of the Yaogan-16 triplet, part of China's expanding naval ocean surveillance constellation. By 2012, this second ELINT triplet generation gave PLA naval intelligence overlapping coverage with the earlier Yaogan-9 formation, improving both the frequency and geographic reach of ship-emission geolocation across the Western Pacific.

39013.d: Third satellite completing the Yaogan-16 triplet formation. Together with its two siblings, this satellite triangulates naval radio and radar emissions across the Pacific, feeding a targeting and maritime domain awareness architecture Western analysts consider central to China's anti-access/area-denial naval strategy.

39017.d: A Chinese commercial communications satellite providing C and Ku-band services primarily for Asia-Pacific customers. China's commercial satellite operators have expanded their reach across Southeast Asia, Africa, and Latin America, reflecting China's Belt and Road Initiative strategy of using satellite communications as soft power infrastructure — building connectivity relationships that create economic and political dependencies on Chinese space assets.

39019.d: The second Pleiades satellite completes the pair that can image any target on Earth within 24 hours. Together they have documented war damage in Ukraine, mapped Beirut after the 2020 port explosion, and tracked Amazon deforestation in near real-time — demonstrating that commercial imaging now operates in every domain previously exclusive to government space programs.

39020.d: A Eutelsat satellite at 70.5°E providing Ku and C-band services across Central Asia, South Asia, and the Indian Ocean. Eutelsat-70B covers the critical connectivity gap between the Middle East and Southeast Asia — serving maritime users in the Arabian Sea, enterprise customers in Central Asian countries along China's Belt and Road corridor, and broadcasters reaching South Asian diaspora audiences.

39022.d: A Gazprom Space Systems high-power Ku-band satellite providing expanded communications capacity for Russia and the Middle East. Yamal-402 was built by Thales Alenia Space — a final example of Western-Russian commercial satellite cooperation before sanctions and political deterioration made such projects impossible. Its high-power transponders enable direct-to-home broadcasting across Russia's vast territory and provide commercial capacity for Middle Eastern operators seeking Russian satellite infrastructure.

39030.d: Turkey's first domestically produced Earth observation spacecraft, delivering 2.5-meter imagery for military reconnaissance and civilian mapping. Built with the explicit goal of reducing Turkey's dependence on foreign imagery for national security decisions — a sovereignty calculation that drives space programs worldwide.

39034.d: The fourth Skynet-5 satellite and the most capable in the British military communications fleet. Skynet-5D carries improved anti-jam capabilities developed in response to the demonstrated threat of adversary interference with military satellite links. The satellite also provides enhanced polar coverage to support UK military operations in the Arctic — a growing priority as climate change opens the High North to competition.

39035.d: The first satellite in Mexico's MEXSAT national communications system, providing L-band mobile satellite services across Mexico and Central America. Despite being part of a national government program, MEXSAT-3 primarily serves civil communications — connecting remote communities in Mexico's mountains and jungles that have no terrestrial connectivity. The satellite is also available for government emergency communications during hurricanes and earthquakes.
