40970.d: SNAP-3 Alice, part of the Surrey Nanosatellite Applications Platform series developed at Surrey Space Centre in the UK — one of the world's pioneering academic small-satellite research programs. SNAP satellites have historically served as low-cost testbeds for new attitude control, imaging, and communications technologies, feeding directly into the surrounding UK small-satellite industry that Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd (SSTL) later commercialized worldwide.

40982.d: APStar's commercial satellite at 142°E providing C-band coverage of the Asia-Pacific with extensive Pacific Ocean coverage for maritime communications. APStar-9's broad Pacific footprint makes it valuable for shipping and fishing industries operating across the Pacific — providing communications for vessels on trans-Pacific voyages where no other connectivity is available.

40984.d: Turkey's companion broadcasting satellite providing coverage specifically optimized for Turkey's west and south — including coverage of Turkey's Black Sea coast and the Balkans — while Turksat-4A covers the east and the Middle East. Together they provide overlapping coverage of Turkey's entire territory with high signal strength for small consumer dish reception of Turkish and international television programming.

41021.d: A classified Chinese military communications satellite, part of China's dedicated PLA communications architecture. Zhongxing-2C provides encrypted military communications links for Chinese armed forces — supporting everything from strategic command communications to tactical military operations. China's military satellite communications network is maintained separately from commercial systems to ensure operational security and reliability.

41026.d: One of China's Yaogan Earth observation satellites. The complete fleet's ability to revisit any global target multiple times daily using optical, radar, and signals intelligence sensors simultaneously represents a surveillance capability that has permanently altered the information environment in the Indo-Pacific region.

41028.d: An Indian Ku-band communications satellite supporting India's national direct-to-home television infrastructure. GSAT-15 carries the high-power Ku-band transponders that deliver DTH broadcasting to over 30 million Indian homes with satellite dishes — one of the world's largest direct-to-home subscriber bases. It also hosts India's third GAGAN navigation augmentation payload, further strengthening India's satellite-based aviation navigation network.

41029.d: A high-power Arabsat satellite reinforcing the 26°E broadcasting neighborhood. Badr-7 provides the capacity needed for high-definition expansion across the Arab satellite TV market — which has been rapidly upgrading from SD to HD channels. The Badr position at 26°E now hosts more satellite channels than any other orbital slot in the world serving the Arab and surrounding regions.

41034.d: Laos's first national satellite — a milestone for one of Southeast Asia's smallest and landlocked nations. LaoSat-1 was built by China and represents an extension of Chinese telecommunications infrastructure development into Southeast Asian nations through commercial and diplomatic channels. It provides C and Ku-band direct-to-home broadcasting and VSAT communications across Laos, and potentially neighboring countries, filling connectivity gaps in a country where mountainous terrain makes terrestrial infrastructure expensive to deploy.

41036.d: A Telesat C and Ku-band satellite at 15°W serving the Americas and Europe across the Atlantic. Telstar-12V's broad Atlantic coverage connects North and South America with Europe — serving the transoceanic corporate data networks, broadcasting distribution, and maritime communications that flow across the world's most commercially active ocean. Its C-band capacity serves Latin American cable operators while Ku-band serves European and American enterprise users.

41038.d: Part of China's Yaogan military Earth observation constellation. Alongside optical satellites, the Yaogan series includes SAR and signals intelligence variants capable of monitoring foreign military activities through any weather or lighting conditions — a complete all-domain awareness system built entirely domestically.

41105.d: Russia's Elektro-L No.2 is among the world's highest-resolution geostationary weather satellites, with full-disk images at 1 km visible resolution. Stationed at 76°E over Siberia, it covers Russia's vast territory from a fixed point — the only practical way to watch weather across 5 time zones from a single geostationary position.

41121.d: A classified Russian military satellite in geostationary orbit, operating under the anonymous Cosmos designation Russia uses for defense space assets. Based on orbital position, independent analysts assess COSMOS 2513 as a Russian Meridian or Raduga-class military communications satellite. Russia's military GEO communications satellites support strategic nuclear forces, naval communications, and military command networks across Russia's vast territory.

41167.d: Singapore's second Earth observation satellite provides multispectral and hyperspectral imaging for monitoring Singapore's territory and surrounding maritime domain. Singapore's satellite program reflects the city-state's recognition that future economic advantage lies in mastering space-based information systems — a strategic bet as significant as its earlier bets on semiconductors and finance.

41173.d: Named after the Monkey King of Chinese legend who can see demons in disguise, DAMPE hunts for dark matter — the invisible substance making up 27% of the universe that has never been directly detected. In its first 530 days, it recorded 1.5 million high-energy cosmic ray electrons and positrons with the highest energy resolution of any space instrument. In 2026 it published a landmark result in Nature: direct evidence for a 'cosmic speed limit' on particle acceleration near Earth, rewriting cosmic ray physics after a century of uncertainty.

41182.d: Part of ORBCOMM's OG2 second-generation constellation, launched in 2015 to modernize the company's original satellite messaging network. This satellite carries both the core VHF data relay payload and an AIS receiver, allowing ORBCOMM to sell ship-tracking data to maritime customers as a side business alongside its traditional industrial IoT messaging service.

41183.d: One of ORBCOMM's OG2 satellites, representing a generational leap in the company's messaging capability — faster relay times, larger data packets, and dual-purpose AIS ship tracking built into the same spacecraft. OG2 satellites launched aboard SpaceX Falcon 9 rockets, including one of the earliest commercial payloads to fly on a reused Falcon 9 booster.

41184.d: Part of ORBCOMM's second-generation constellation, providing significantly faster satellite IoT messaging than the company's original 1990s fleet. This satellite's AIS payload also contributes ship-tracking data used by maritime security agencies, insurance companies, and shipping lines monitoring vessel positions worldwide.

41185.d: One of ORBCOMM's OG2 satellites, launched in 2015 to replace and expand upon the company's aging first-generation constellation. The upgrade transformed ORBCOMM from a niche asset-tracking provider into a broader satellite IoT and maritime intelligence company serving logistics, energy, and shipping customers worldwide.

41187.d: Part of ORBCOMM's second-generation constellation, combining faster M2M data relay with AIS vessel tracking in a single spacecraft. OG2 satellites represented ORBCOMM's answer to a rapidly evolving satellite IoT market that, by the mid-2010s, had begun facing competition from newer entrants building even more advanced messaging constellations.

41188.d: One of ORBCOMM's OG2 satellites, providing near-real-time machine-to-machine data relay for trucking, heavy equipment, and maritime customers. The satellite's dual M2M and AIS payload reflects ORBCOMM's strategy of squeezing multiple revenue streams out of each spacecraft rather than building single-purpose satellites.

41189.d: Part of ORBCOMM's second-generation OG2 constellation, completing the modernization of a satellite messaging network that traces its origins back to 1998. This satellite's combined M2M and AIS capabilities continue serving customers who need to track assets and vessels in locations where cellular networks simply don't reach.

41191.d: Russia's Express-AMU1 provides direct-to-home TV broadcasting across Russia — the backbone of Russian national television distribution. It replaced older Soviet-era satellites that had sustained the national broadcasting network through economic chaos and carried Russian state channels to all 11 time zones. Control of satellite TV distribution gives the Russian government a powerful tool for national information management across its vast territory.

41194.d: China's first geostationary high-resolution optical satellite, the most powerful geostationary imager ever built at launch. Unlike polar satellites that pass once daily, Gaofen-4 watches continuously from 36,000 km — achieving 50-meter resolution while staring at a typhoon for hours. The satellite that gave China a geostationary eye that never blinks.

41238.d: Belarus's first communications satellite, built by China and representing the deepening Sino-Belarusian cooperation. Belintersat-1 provides C and Ku-band services for Belarusian government communications, broadcasting, and broadband — reducing Belarus's dependence on Russian and European satellite capacity. Belarus's investment in national satellite infrastructure reflects the strategic importance its government places on communications sovereignty in a country that has historically occupied a contested position between major powers.

41240.d: Jason-3 continues a sea level record begun in 1992, measuring ocean surface height to within 3.3 cm. Three decades of combined Jason data have documented over 100 mm of sea level rise — one of the most definitive measurements of human-caused climate change made from space. Its successor data feeds the coastal flood risk models protecting the world's largest cities.

41241.d: A NavIC satellite completing India's geostationary navigation component. NavIC operates in L5 and S-band frequencies — S-band is unique to NavIC and penetrates ionospheric interference more effectively than the L-band frequencies used by GPS and other global systems, making NavIC particularly accurate during periods of high solar activity when the ionosphere is most disturbed.

41310.d: A Eutelsat satellite at 9°E providing Ku-band broadcasting for Central and Eastern European markets alongside Ka-band broadband via the EDRS-B hosted payload. Eutelsat-9B hosts ESA's European Data Relay Satellite B laser communications terminal — one of the nodes in Europe's space data relay network that transfers data from Earth observation satellites via optical laser link. The combination of commercial Ku-band broadcasting and an ESA laser terminal on one spacecraft is a novel cost-sharing arrangement.

41335.d: ESA's ocean and land monitoring satellite measures sea surface temperature, ocean color, and surface topography as part of the Copernicus program. Its altimeter tracks global sea level within 3 cm — critical for detecting El Niño and La Niña cycles that drive weather worldwide. It also monitors wildfires and vegetation health across Europe and Africa.

41380.d: An SES high-throughput satellite at 108.2°E serving Southeast Asia with Ku-band DTH and enterprise services. SES-9 was launched on a SpaceX Falcon 9 that made history as the first to attempt an orbital stage landing at sea — the booster came close but ultimately splashed into the ocean. The satellite provides broadcasting capacity for Indonesia, Vietnam, and other Southeast Asian markets where satellite television penetration is growing rapidly.

41382.d: Eutelsat's satellite at 65°W providing Ka-band high-throughput broadband and Ku-band broadcasting across Latin America. Eutelsat-65 West A was specifically designed for Brazilian and Latin American markets — its Ka-band spot beams provide the fastest satellite internet available from geostationary orbit across Brazil, enabling rural Brazilian households and businesses to access broadband speeds that approach fiber quality.

41384.d: Part of India's NavIC constellation approaching full operational capability. NavIC's seven-satellite design was specifically optimized for the Indian subcontinent — positioning India to provide navigation services for civilian aviation, maritime, and military users across South Asia without dependence on GPS, and eventually offering NavIC as an international navigation alternative to neighboring countries.

41434.d: A supplementary BeiDou-2 IGSO satellite added to increase constellation robustness. As BeiDou has gained government-mandated adoption in Chinese military systems, civilian aviation, maritime navigation, and smart city infrastructure, the need for high availability and redundancy has grown — each additional IGSO satellite reduces the risk of service gaps during satellite maintenance periods.

41469.d: The seventh NavIC satellite, completing India's national navigation constellation. With IRNSS-1G operational, India became only the fifth entity in the world — after the US, Russia, China, and Europe — to operate a fully independent navigation system. NavIC now provides positioning for Indian military systems, commercial shipping in the Indian Ocean, fishing vessels, and increasingly for civilian smartphones across South Asia.

41471.d: A Sky Perfect JSAT high-power satellite serving Japan's direct-to-home broadcasting and broadband markets. JCSAT-2B supports 4K ultra-high definition broadcasting in Japan — the world's first broadcaster of regular 4K satellite TV content. Japan's NHK launched 4K broadcasts using JCSAT capacity, making Japan the global leader in high-definition satellite broadcasting technology deployment.

41473.d: Yaogan-30, launched May 2016 — the seed satellite that gave its name to an entire family of Chinese naval ELINT triplets launched in the years that followed. Unlike its triplet-formation namesakes, Yaogan-30 itself flew alone, with orbital characteristics suggesting it belongs to the broader JB-6 optical reconnaissance lineage rather than the ELINT triangulation role its later numerical siblings would take on. Its 2016 launch preceded the first genuine Yaogan-30 triplet by over a year.

41552.d: Thaicom's high-power Ku-band satellite serving Southeast Asia's broadcasting and broadband markets. Thaicom-8 provides enhanced direct-to-home TV coverage and VSAT broadband across Thailand and neighboring countries — supporting both commercial and government connectivity requirements in a region where satellite remains essential for bridging the digital divide between major cities and rural populations.

41556.d: China's second Ziyuan-3 stereo mapping satellite, producing 3D terrain models of China at 2-meter horizontal resolution — the spatial foundation for China's autonomous vehicle navigation system and its national geographic information infrastructure.

41581.d: An Intelsat high-power Ku-band satellite at 95°W expanding direct-to-home capacity for Latin American markets. IS-31 specifically serves DirecTV Latin America's 10+ million subscribers in South America — powering one of the continent's largest pay television services. Every South American DirecTV customer pointing their dish at 95°W is receiving their signal through IS-31.

41586.d: A later BeiDou-2 geostationary satellite maintaining the regional navigation network. China has distributed BeiDou's GEO satellites to provide the strongest signal coverage over the Asia-Pacific where China's naval and military forces, fishing fleets, and commercial shipping concentrate — making Chinese navigation independence most critical in exactly the region where geopolitical tensions are highest.

41588.d: A satellite co-hosting payloads for Asia Broadcast Satellite and Mongolia's first national communications satellite — an unlikely partnership that gave one of the world's least densely populated nations its own space presence. Mongolia, the world's most sparsely populated country with vast distances between nomadic communities, depends on satellite for communications infrastructure that no terrestrial network can economically provide across the Gobi Desert and northern steppes. MongoSat-1's portion brings television and broadband to Mongolian herder communities.

41589.d: A Eutelsat all-electric satellite at 117°W providing Ka-band high-throughput broadband for the Americas. Eutelsat-117 West B was built using the revolutionary Boeing 702SP all-electric platform — the same technology as ABS-3A, the satellite used gravity and electric ion thrusters over months to reach geostationary orbit rather than conventional chemical propulsion. The Ka-band capacity serves US and Mexican broadband markets, with Eutelsat competing directly against ViaSat and HughesNet for American satellite internet customers.

41591.d: Indonesia's dedicated satellite for BRI (Bank Rakyat Indonesia) — the world's first satellite owned and operated by a commercial bank. BRI is Indonesia's largest bank by assets and serves tens of millions of low-income customers across Indonesia's 17,000 islands. Many of these customers live in villages reachable only by boat or small aircraft. BRISAT provides the satellite communications backbone for BRI's rural banking network — connecting thousands of remote bank branches and automated teller machines across the archipelago.
41591.t: Indonesian Banking / Enterprise Satellite (World's First Bank-Owned Satellite)

41592.d: An EchoStar satellite at 110°W providing additional capacity for DISH Network's primary position. EchoStar-18 expanded DISH's 4K Ultra HD channel capacity at the critical 110°W position — DISH was an early adopter of 4K satellite broadcasting, and each 4K channel requires roughly four times the bandwidth of an HD channel. EchoStar-18's extra capacity made DISH's 4K channel expansion possible without sacrificing existing HD channel capacity.

41599.d: India's latest generation mapping satellite, Cartosat-2C provides sub-meter imagery supporting India's digital infrastructure and defense mapping. When launched, it accompanied a record 19 co-passengers on a single PSLV — demonstrating ISRO's commercial launch capability that helped establish India as a competitive small satellite launch provider.

41601.d: Planet's third SkySat and first with improved optics delivering 72 cm resolution. SkySat-C1 is the pathfinder for Planet's second-generation constellation that can image any point on Earth every day at sub-meter resolution — a surveillance cadence previously achievable only by nations with dedicated intelligence satellite programs.

41603.d: Indonesia's most capable domestically built satellite carries an AIS receiver, multispectral camera, and amateur radio payload. The AIS component is strategically critical for Indonesia — the world's largest archipelago with 17,000 islands and the busiest shipping lanes — providing independent monitoring of vessel movements through Indonesian waters.

41622.d: The fifth and final operational MUOS satellite, completing the global 4G military mobile network. MUOS-5 provides on-orbit spare capacity and full constellation redundancy. Together the five MUOS satellites provide US and allied military forces with persistent, anti-jam, worldwide mobile communications — transforming how naval, ground, and air forces coordinate in real-time in contested environments.

41725.d: China's first mobile satellite phone system satellite — the Chinese equivalent of Inmarsat, providing mobile satellite voice and data services across China and neighboring areas. Tiantong ('Sky Link') gives China sovereign mobile satellite communications for remote area users, maritime vessels in Chinese waters, and military forces. Before Tiantong, Chinese users in remote areas depended on Inmarsat — a foreign operator subject to service denial in geopolitical conflicts. Tiantong eliminates that dependency.

41727.d: China's first high-resolution SAR satellite delivers 1-meter radar imagery day and night through any weather. It monitors China's 18,000 km coastline continuously, tracking illegal fishing, ship traffic, oil spills, and coastal erosion. The satellite that gave China sovereign all-weather maritime surveillance that no previous Chinese system possessed.

41729.d: A Sky Perfect JSAT satellite positioned to provide redundancy and backup capacity for the Japanese broadcasting network. The reliability demanded by Japanese broadcasters and consumers is exceptionally high — NHK and commercial Japanese networks provide essentially zero-interruption broadcasting, requiring satellite capacity backup plans that JCSAT-16 helps support.

41744.d: A classified US intelligence satellite in geostationary orbit, tracked by amateur observers in an orbit suggesting signals intelligence collection. Notably observed approaching within kilometers of a Chinese TJS-3 satellite in 2018 — appearing to conduct an on-orbit inspection of the Chinese spacecraft. This rendezvous, the first publicly observed US-China satellite proximity event, highlighted the intensifying competition in geostationary orbit where both nations deploy satellites capable of approaching and characterizing each other's spacecraft.

41745.d: A classified US satellite launched alongside USA-270 in 2016 into geostationary orbit. The dual launch of two classified GEO satellites simultaneously is unusual, suggesting either redundancy requirements or complementary mission profiles where two spacecraft working in concert provide capabilities unavailable to a single satellite. The Space Force maintains a growing fleet of classified GEO satellites as the geostationary belt becomes an increasingly contested strategic environment.

41747.d: An Intelsat Ku-band satellite at 45°W providing direct-to-home broadcasting capacity for Brazil. IS-36 is a dedicated Brazilian DTH satellite — all its capacity focused on delivering Brazilian and international television channels directly to Brazilian home satellite dishes. Brazil has over 30 million DTH subscribers, making it one of the world's largest satellite television markets and one of Intelsat's most important single-country positions.

41752.d: India's advanced geostationary weather satellite doubling coverage over the Indian Ocean with 6-channel imaging and 19-channel sounding. INSAT-3DR's early warning data directly feeds India's cyclone evacuation programs — transforming from operations that lost tens of thousands of lives in the 1990s to ones that evacuate millions with minimal casualties today.

41771.d: SkySat-C4 contributed to Planet's drive toward daily tasked imagery. At scale, this capability transformed Earth observation from a scientific tool to an operational intelligence product used by hedge funds tracking oil tankers, governments monitoring disputed territories, and humanitarians assessing disaster damage.

41772.d: The fifth SkySat satellite, continuing Planet's systematic build toward daily global revisit. The SkySat constellation operates in sun-synchronous orbits chosen for consistent lighting conditions — ensuring imagery from different dates is directly comparable for change detection analysis.

41773.d: Part of Planet's 2016 batch deployment that demonstrated high-resolution satellite imagery could be deployed at the pace of a software company, not a traditional aerospace program. Planet's batch launch strategy made it the most rapidly deployed commercial high-resolution imaging system ever built.

41774.d: Part of Planet's 2016 batch deployment that increased constellation coverage to where Planet could begin offering 'daily revisit' as a commercial product. Daily cadence was the threshold at which satellite imagery became useful for monitoring dynamic situations like active construction, agricultural seasons, and conflict.

41784.d: A student-built nanosatellite from PES Institute of Technology in Bangalore — one of the few university satellites to reach orbit from India's private engineering colleges. PISat demonstrated attitude control and Earth imaging, representing India's growing ecosystem of student space programs that parallel ISRO's official programs.

41785.d: Algeria's second Earth observation satellite monitors North Africa's diverse terrain — from advancing Saharan dunes to the coastal Mediterranean zone. Monitoring desertification across one of the world's largest countries requires space-based observation at scales that make satellite imagery not optional but essential for national resource management.

41787.d: Satellogic's Pathfinder tested the hyperspectral and video imaging technology for its commercial constellation. Satellogic's approach — combining hyperspectral science imaging with commercial video in a microsatellite — proved the science-commercial divide in Earth observation was artificial, and that the same small spacecraft could serve both markets.

41790.d: India's ocean wind scatterometer, fast-tracked as an emergency replacement after its predecessor's instrument failed. India couldn't afford a monsoon season gap in ocean wind data. SCATSAT was approved and launched in record time — the urgency reflecting how critically Indian forecasters depended on ocean wind measurements for cyclone and monsoon prediction.

41793.d: An Indian C-band and Ku-band communications satellite providing both wide-area broadcasting and broadband services across the Indian subcontinent. GSAT-18 supports India's growing cable and DTH television ecosystem, providing the distribution backbone for regional language channels that reach specific linguistic communities — an important service in a country with 22 officially recognized languages and hundreds of regional dialects, where satellite television in one's native language is culturally significant.

41794.d: The companion to Sky Muster 1, doubling NBN satellite capacity for rural Australia. Together Sky Muster 1 and 2 provide the backbone for Australian satellite broadband connecting remote communities across a continent-sized country. The service has been particularly transformative for children in remote areas accessing online education — students in outback Queensland and Western Australia now have the same educational resources as city students, connected through satellites 35,800 km overhead.

41836.d: Japan's backup geostationary satellite, Himawari-9 is on standby to assume primary operations within hours if Himawari-8 fails. A coverage failure during typhoon season could affect hundreds of millions across Asia-Pacific who depend on accurate track forecasts. Backup satellites are the unsung insurance policy of operational meteorology.

41838.d: China's Shijian-17 ('Practice-17') is officially a technology demonstration satellite but attracted significant attention when it began maneuvering near other satellites in GEO. It performed close inspection passes of other spacecraft and appeared to demonstrate robotic arm or proximity operations technology. Analysts assess it as testing counter-satellite inspection or servicing capabilities — skills directly applicable to both satellite servicing and anti-satellite operations.

41852.d: The companion satellite to AeroCube-8C, forming the second AeroCube-8 mission pair. Like its sibling, AeroCube-8D tested novel spacecraft subsystems in the harsh reality of actual orbital operation — the only environment where certain design assumptions can truly be validated, since ground testing can simulate vacuum and thermal cycling but never perfectly replicates years of continuous radiation exposure and atomic oxygen erosion.

41853.d: One of a pair of 1.5U CubeSats in the AeroCube-8 generation, built to test novel spacecraft technologies for The Aerospace Corporation. AeroCube-8C continued the program's tradition of validating individual subsystem innovations — from miniaturized sensors to new communication protocols — each mission adding another building block to Aerospace's accumulated small-satellite technology library, freely shared with NASA, defense, and academic customers.

41857.d: The first satellite in China's Yunhai-1 ('Cloud Sea') series, launched November 2016 into a 780 km sun-synchronous orbit. Chinese state media describes Yunhai-1 satellites as providing atmospheric, marine, and space environment detection alongside disaster prevention services — but Western analysts assess the series as military meteorological satellites, given the program's PLA sponsorship and its close resemblance to defense weather satellite designs elsewhere. Weather data has always had dual-use military value: an accurate forecast determines whether a missile launch window, an amphibious landing, or a reconnaissance flight proceeds.

41866.d: The most advanced weather satellite ever operated by the United States, GOES-16 watches the Americas with a 16-band imager capturing full-disk images every 15 minutes and storm targets every 30 seconds. Its lightning mapper tracks every lightning strike across the Americas in near real-time. When you see a satellite image of an Atlantic hurricane on the news, it came from this spacecraft.

41869.d: Part of China's Tianlian (Sky-Link) data relay constellation — China's equivalent of NASA's TDRS system. Tianlian satellites relay communications and data from Chinese crewed spacecraft, the Tiangong space station, and robotic missions to ground stations in China. Without Tianlian, Chinese astronauts would be in contact with ground control only during the roughly 10-15 minutes per orbit when their spacecraft is over Chinese territory. Tianlian-1-04 provides coverage of the Asia-Pacific region and the vast Pacific Ocean.

41875.d: Turkey's premier military reconnaissance satellite delivering 80 cm resolution — sharp enough for detailed intelligence assessment of military facilities and infrastructure. It provides sovereign intelligence gathering independent of NATO allies, reflecting Turkey's strategic calculation that national security decisions require domestic intelligence sources.

41877.d: India's continuation of uninterrupted land monitoring feeds the national agricultural crop mapping system — estimating crop acreage across India before harvest to guide food security policy. Satellite imagery directly influencing the food trade decisions of the world's most populous nation.

41879.d: The eighth WGS satellite, positioned over the Indo-Pacific to support the US military's rebalance toward the Asia theater. WGS-8 fills coverage gaps in the Pacific constellation and provides redundancy for the most strategically contested communications environment the US military faces — where adversary anti-satellite capabilities and electronic warfare are most sophisticated.

41882.d: China's third-generation geostationary satellite carries 14 spectral channels and a lightning mapping imager — making China only the third nation after the US and Japan to deploy space-based lightning detection at geostationary altitude. Full-disk imagery every 15 minutes has dramatically improved China's typhoon track forecasting accuracy.

41884.d: CYGNSS #5, flying with seven sisters to build continuous tropical ocean coverage. Its GPS-reflectometry approach captures wave height patterns encoding wind speed information invisible to traditional weather satellites looking straight down through rain-filled clouds.

41885.d: The fourth CYGNSS spacecraft, contributing to the first constellation built specifically to measure wind speeds inside tropical cyclones. Before CYGNSS, meteorologists were essentially blind to inner-core hurricane winds — the data gap responsible for most major hurricane intensity forecast failures. CYGNSS cut that uncertainty by nearly half.

41886.d: CYGNSS spacecraft #2, part of the eight-satellite GPS-reflectometry constellation. The full constellation revisits any tropical ocean point every 7 hours — fast enough to catch rapid intensification events that turn a Category 1 into a Category 4 overnight, the deadliest and most forecast-resistant phenomenon in tropical meteorology.

41887.d: One of eight NASA CYGNSS satellites using a revolutionary approach to measure hurricane winds — instead of emitting radar, it listens for GPS signals bouncing off the ocean surface. Rougher seas scatter GPS differently, revealing wind speed even through the rain bands that made hurricane eyewalls invisible to all previous satellites. Eight microsatellites doing what billion-dollar instruments couldn't.

41888.d: The eighth CYGNSS spacecraft completes the tropical belt coverage. CYGNSS has since found a second life mapping floods — detecting inundation in the Amazon and Mekong deltas faster than any previous system. A hurricane hunter that became a flood detector: space missions rarely stay confined to their original purpose.

41890.d: CYGNSS #7, part of eight satellites launched from a single Pegasus XL air-launched rocket — all eight deployed in under three minutes. Each microsatellite cost roughly $3M, cheap enough that NASA could afford eight instead of one expensive instrument. The constellation model proved more hurricane-worthy than any single large satellite.

41891.d: CYGNSS #3, the GPS hurricane hunter. Scientists discovered it can also measure soil moisture over land — an unexpected capability now used to improve flood forecasting in the Mississippi, Amazon, and Ganges basins. Satellites designed for one purpose routinely reveal capabilities their designers never imagined.

41893.d: A high-throughput EchoStar Ka-band satellite at 97.1°W providing HughesNet broadband internet across North America. EchoStar-19 carries 138 Ka-band spot beams with over 220 Gbps of total throughput — making it the highest-capacity satellite HughesNet had ever operated when launched. It provides the satellite internet service that connects hundreds of thousands of rural American homes and businesses that cable and fiber networks have not reached.

41899.d: Yijian, a Chinese satellite launched in 2016 whose designation translates roughly to 'One Sword' or 'A Single Sword.' Details of its specific mission and sensor payload have not been extensively publicized outside Chinese sources, consistent with the more opaque disclosure practices China applies to many of its smaller experimental and technology-demonstration satellites.

41903.d: A SKY Perfect JSAT satellite at 110°E providing Ku-band coverage for Japan and Southeast Asia. The 110°E position is one of Asia's most valuable geostationary slots — visible from Japan, China, Southeast Asia, and Australia simultaneously. JCSAT-15's Japanese broadcasting payload serves Japan's domestic satellite television market while its regional coverage enables SKY Perfect JSAT to compete for Southeast Asian broadcasting and enterprise communications customers.

41904.d: A Brazilian commercial communications satellite operated by Embratel Star One — Brazil's leading satellite operator. Star One D1 provides Ku and Ka-band services for direct-to-home TV, broadband internet, and corporate networks across Latin America. Brazil is Latin America's largest satellite market, driven by a vast territory, uneven terrestrial infrastructure, and a television-hungry population of 215 million people.

41911.d: The second TJS satellite, continuing China's military GEO communications expansion under the experimental designation. TJS-2's position in geostationary orbit provides it with continuous coverage of a fixed geographic region — consistent with military communications support for Chinese forces, intelligence collection, or data relay functions. The rapid pace of TJS launches suggests an urgent military operational requirement.

41914.d: One of the early Jilin-1 'Smart Video' satellites, capturing 4K high-definition color video of Earth's surface from sun-synchronous orbit — not just still images, but genuine moving footage of activity on the ground. Jilin-1 video satellites drew international attention when Chang Guang released footage purportedly showing a US Air Force F-22 Raptor in flight, demonstrating both the constellation's capability and China's willingness to publicize sensitive surveillance imagery for strategic messaging purposes.

41917.d: One of the 66 operational Iridium NEXT satellites that replaced the original 1990s Iridium constellation between 2017 and 2019 — one of the largest satellite replacement campaigns in commercial space history, launched entirely on SpaceX Falcon 9 rockets. Unlike its predecessor, whose polished antenna panels famously produced brilliant 'Iridium flares' visible to the naked eye, Iridium NEXT's redesigned antennas don't flare — a minor loss for amateur astronomers, but a sign of improved solar panel efficiency. Iridium NEXT also hosts Aireon, a real-time global air traffic surveillance system that tracks aircraft ADS-B signals even over oceans and polar regions where no ground radar exists.

41918.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, one of 66 cross-linked satellites providing truly global satellite phone and data coverage — the only commercial network offering pole-to-pole connectivity, since Iridium's satellites communicate with each other via inter-satellite links rather than requiring a ground station within view. This mesh architecture means a phone call can route through space between multiple satellites before ever touching the ground.

41919.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, built by Thales Alenia Space and Northrop Grumman to replace the aging original Iridium constellation launched in the 1990s. Iridium was infamous in the space industry as a $5 billion commercial venture that went bankrupt within a year of launching in 1999 — before finding new life as essential infrastructure for maritime, aviation, military, and polar research communications.

41920.d: Part of the 66-satellite Iridium NEXT constellation, providing satellite phone and data connectivity across the entire globe including the poles — coverage no other commercial satellite network offers, since most systems (including Starlink for years) skip extreme polar latitudes. Iridium's polar coverage makes it the default choice for Antarctic research stations, polar shipping routes, and military operations near the poles.

41921.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, part of a constellation whose predecessor generation created some of the most spectacular naked-eye satellite phenomena ever observed — sudden, brilliant 'Iridium flares' up to 100 times brighter than Venus, caused by sunlight reflecting off the original satellites' flat antenna panels. Iridium NEXT's redesigned antennas eliminated this effect entirely, quietly ending an era of predictable amateur astronomy events that had delighted skywatchers for two decades.

41922.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, hosting the Aireon payload that gives air traffic controllers real-time radar-equivalent surveillance of aircraft over oceans, polar regions, and remote areas where ground-based radar has never existed. Aireon has fundamentally changed oceanic air traffic management, allowing tighter aircraft spacing over the Atlantic and Pacific that previously required wide separation due to limited surveillance.

41923.d: One of the 66 Iridium NEXT satellites launched between 2017 and 2019, replacing a constellation whose 1990s-era satellites were beginning to fail faster than replacements could be built. The full replacement campaign required eight dedicated SpaceX Falcon 9 launches — one of the largest and most technically ambitious satellite replacement projects any commercial operator has ever undertaken.

41924.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, providing the satellite phone connectivity that ship captains, polar researchers, and remote expedition teams have relied on for decades. Iridium's mesh network architecture — satellites relaying calls to each other rather than requiring direct ground station contact — makes it uniquely resilient compared to bent-pipe satellite systems that fail without ground infrastructure nearby.

41925.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, part of a constellation that emerged from one of the most dramatic bankruptcy-to-redemption stories in commercial space history. Original Iridium investors lost billions when the company collapsed in 1999 just a year after launch; two decades later, Iridium NEXT represents a fully modernized, profitable, and strategically important satellite network serving military, maritime, and aviation customers worldwide.

41926.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation's 86.4°-inclined polar orbit, ensuring genuinely global coverage including the extreme polar latitudes most competing satellite networks cannot reach. This satellite contributes to a mesh network design that lets Iridium maintain connectivity even during major ground infrastructure outages, since calls can route satellite-to-satellite around any affected region.

41937.d: The fourth SBIRS missile warning satellite, positioned to provide redundant coverage of the most likely missile threat corridors. SBIRS has been operational during North Korean ICBM tests, Iranian missile strikes on US bases in Iraq, and numerous other launch events — providing US commanders with crucial seconds to minutes of warning that change the calculus of every military confrontation.

41942.d: Spain's advanced all-electric satellite for the Americas and Europe, pioneering all-electric propulsion for a major commercial GEO satellite. All-electric satellites use ion engines for orbit raising instead of chemical rockets — taking months to reach GEO instead of weeks but saving thousands of kilograms of propellant mass that translates into more payload capacity or lower launch costs. Hispasat 36W-1 demonstrated that all-electric GEO satellites are commercially viable, accelerating their adoption across the industry.

41944.d: A Telkom Indonesia satellite providing C-band and Ku-band services for Indonesia and Southeast Asia. Telkom-3S replaced the Telkom-3 satellite that failed to reach orbit in 2012 — a painful loss that left Indonesian telecommunications operators scrambling for alternative capacity. The replacement satellite restored Indonesia's commercial communications capacity and expanded the Indonesian operator's services across Southeast Asia.

41945.d: Intelsat's first Epic high-throughput satellite, combining traditional Ku-band wide-beam coverage with high-throughput Ka-band spot beams in a single spacecraft. Epic represents Intelsat's answer to ViaSat and HughesNet — bringing GEO broadband throughput competitive with LEO constellations while maintaining the wide coverage and low latency stability of GEO orbit. IS-32E covers the Americas and the Atlantic with 60 Gbps of Ka-band capacity.

41948.d: One of India's high-cadence Cartosat-2 series satellites providing continuous sub-meter mapping imagery. The series demonstrates India's mature space manufacturing capability — producing complex optical imaging satellites on an annual schedule with high reliability, supporting both civilian infrastructure and defense mapping requirements.

42063.d: Sentinel-2B flies offset 180 degrees from Sentinel-2A — together achieving global land coverage every 5 days. Five days is fast enough to catch a spreading forest fire, a flooding river, or a glacier calving an iceberg. The combined archive is the world's most-downloaded free satellite dataset, used by hundreds of thousands of researchers and applications daily.

42070.d: An EchoStar Ku-band satellite at 45°W providing broadcasting and enterprise services for the Brazilian and Latin American market. EchoStar-23 represents EchoStar's expansion beyond its North American DISH base into international commercial satellite services. Its South Atlantic position provides strong coverage of Brazil — the most important Latin American satellite market — from a position optimized for the country's geographic extent.

42075.d: The ninth WGS satellite with enhanced polar coverage extending into the Arctic Circle. WGS-9 was the first funded entirely by a coalition of international partners — Australia, Canada, Denmark, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, and New Zealand collectively funded and own capacity on the satellite. A genuine allied investment in shared military communications infrastructure.

42432.d: An SES satellite at 67°W serving Latin America, launched on SpaceX's first Falcon 9 to use a previously flown first stage — a milestone that marked the dawn of the reusable rocket era in commercial satellite launches. SES was the customer who gave SpaceX the commercial contract to validate that a reused orbital booster was technically and commercially viable, a decision that transformed the economics of the entire space launch industry.
42432.t: Latin American Communications (Historic Reused Rocket Launch)

42662.d: China's first commercial Ka-band high-throughput satellite, officially designated Shijian-13 in its experimental phase before becoming operational ChinaSat-16. It was China's technology validation for next-generation broadband satellite technology — the first Chinese satellite to use electric propulsion for orbit raising, reducing launch mass significantly. It demonstrated Ka-band broadband delivery at performance levels previously only achievable by Western commercial operators.

42691.d: South Korea's Ka-band high-throughput satellite, providing broadband internet across Korea and Southeast Asia. KT SAT's KoreaSat-7 introduced high-throughput Ka-band broadband service for Korean domestic customers and expanded into Southeast Asian markets — Indonesia, the Philippines, and Vietnam — where satellite broadband fills critical gaps in national connectivity infrastructure. Korea's sophisticated communications technology sector drives the development of increasingly capable satellite platforms.

42692.d: Brazil's first dedicated military communications satellite — the Geostationary Defense and Strategic Communications Satellite. SGDC provides encrypted X-band military communications for the Brazilian armed forces and Ka-band broadband internet for Brazil's national broadband connectivity program. Brazil combined military and civilian missions to reduce costs while achieving defense requirements for sovereign communications independent of commercial providers.

42695.d: India's gift to its South Asian neighbors — the South Asia Satellite launched as a diplomatic initiative providing satellite connectivity to India's neighboring nations. GSAT-9 provides Ku-band services to Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, the Maldives, Nepal, and Sri Lanka as a free Indian offering. The satellite supports broadcasting, weather services, broadband connectivity, and telemedicine across some of South Asia's smallest and least-connected nations — diplomatic power expressed in satellite form.

42698.d: The fourth Global Xpress satellite, expanding capacity in high-demand regions. Inmarsat-5 F4 added coverage redundancy and capacity to support growing in-flight broadband demand as more airlines equipped their fleets with satellite Wi-Fi. The exponential growth of passenger expectations for connectivity — driven by smartphone adoption and streaming services — made each successive Global Xpress satellite necessary sooner than Inmarsat had originally planned.

42709.d: An SES hybrid satellite at 129°W providing Ku-band wide-beam coverage of North America alongside Ka-band high-throughput spot beams for aviation broadband. SES-15's Ka-band beams are specifically designed for in-flight passenger wifi across North American airspace — covering the flight routes between the continental US, Canada, Mexico, the Caribbean, and Hawaii. Airlines serving North American routes use SES-15 to provide the in-flight connectivity that passengers increasingly expect on every flight.

42738.d: The second Michibiki quasi-zenith satellite, providing GPS augmentation across the Asia-Oceania region. Michibiki-2 carries centimeter-level augmentation signals that correct GPS errors in real time — enabling the precision agriculture, autonomous construction equipment, and self-driving vehicles that Japan is deploying across its territory. The Michibiki L6 signal provides free sub-centimeter positioning corrections that would otherwise cost premium subscription fees on commercial correction networks.

42740.d: The successor to ViaSat-1, with nearly 300 Gbps capacity and coverage extending from the continental US to Mexico, Central America, and northern South America. ViaSat-2 also pioneered aviation broadband — providing fast satellite internet to commercial aircraft over North America and the Atlantic through JetBlue, United, and other airlines. ViaSat-2 made in-flight Wi-Fi fast enough to stream video reliably, permanently changing passenger expectations of commercial aviation.

42741.d: Eutelsat's all-electric satellite at 172°E serving the Asia-Pacific maritime market — the first satellite in the world specifically designed to target the maritime broadband market from its initial conception. Its Ka-band throughput is focused on major Asia-Pacific shipping routes, providing the bandwidth needed for modern vessels to transmit engine telemetry, cargo management data, electronic chart updates, and crew welfare internet simultaneously. Eutelsat-172B shows how satellite operators are building satellites around specific market verticals rather than general-purpose coverage.

42747.d: India's first Ka-band high-throughput satellite, launched as the first mission of India's heavy-lift GSLV Mk III rocket. GSAT-19 demonstrated both Ka-band satellite technology and India's new large rocket in a single mission — two firsts for India's space program. It carries propulsion experiments and space environment instrumentation alongside its communication payload, providing valuable data for future Indian satellite and launch vehicle design.

42749.d: A high-throughput satellite providing mobile broadband across Europe and North Africa — primarily targeting in-flight passenger WiFi for European airlines. EchoStar-21's Ka-band spot beams deliver enough throughput to provide meaningful broadband to passengers on hundreds of aircraft simultaneously crossing European airspace. As passenger expectations for in-flight WiFi have risen to match terrestrial connectivity, satellites like EchoStar-21 have become critical infrastructure for European aviation competitiveness.

42758.d: China's first X-ray astronomy satellite — Huiyan means 'Insight' or 'Sharp Eyes' — scans the sky for X-ray sources from black holes, neutron stars, and gamma-ray bursts. In 2017, just three months after launch, it detected the electromagnetic counterpart to a gravitational wave event (GW170817), the collision of two neutron stars 130 million light-years away. This observation marked China's arrival at the frontier of multi-messenger astronomy.

42767.d: Continuing India's steady cadence of high-resolution Earth observation, Cartosat-2E ensures no gap in India's national mapping program. The series demonstrates how a consistent satellite production pipeline — rather than occasional large missions — builds the most valuable long-term Earth observation archives.

42801.d: Bulgaria's first satellite and the Balkans' first direct-to-home broadcasting satellite. BulgariaSat-1 provides Ku-band direct-to-home television for Bulgaria and the broader Balkan region — distributing Bulgarian, Serbian, Romanian, and Greek television channels to homes across Southeastern Europe. The satellite represents Bulgaria's entry into satellite operations and provides coverage for the 1.5 million strong Bulgarian diaspora across Western Europe who can receive Bulgarian channels via small satellite dishes.

42803.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites launched in a later 2017-2018 batch, continuing the systematic replacement of Iridium's original constellation. Each Iridium NEXT satellite also hosts secondary payloads beyond core telephony — including GPS radio occultation weather sensors that feed into NOAA's commercial weather data program, extracting extra scientific value from a constellation built primarily for telecommunications.

42804.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, providing global mesh-network satellite communications for maritime, aviation, government, and military customers. The system's inter-satellite laser and radio links allow it to relay data between satellites in orbit without ever touching a ground station — a capability few other commercial constellations offer even today.

42805.d: One of the 66 Iridium NEXT satellites, replacing an original constellation whose polished antennas produced the famous 'Iridium flares' that amateur astronomers once tracked and predicted for decades. Their disappearance with Iridium NEXT's redesigned antennas marked the quiet end of one of the more beloved unplanned phenomena in modern satellite history.

42806.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, hosting Aireon's real-time global aircraft surveillance payload alongside core satellite telephony services. Aireon's ADS-B tracking data is now used by air navigation service providers worldwide to safely reduce aircraft separation distances over oceans, directly saving airlines fuel and time on transoceanic routes.

42807.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, part of a constellation that replaced its 1990s predecessor through eight dedicated SpaceX Falcon 9 launches between 2017 and 2019. Iridium's continuous polar and oceanic coverage makes it the standard choice for military communications, maritime distress systems, and polar scientific expeditions that other satellite networks simply cannot serve.

42808.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT mesh-network constellation, providing satellite phone and data services with a design architecture unlike almost any competing system — calls and data can hop between multiple satellites in orbit before reaching a ground station, giving Iridium resilience against regional ground infrastructure failures that would disable other networks entirely.

42809.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, launched to replace the original Iridium constellation's aging first-generation satellites. Iridium's history is a case study in space industry economics: a $5 billion investment that went bankrupt within a year, sold for pennies on the dollar, and eventually rebuilt into a critical global communications utility now essential to shipping, aviation, and defense.

42810.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, providing global mobile satellite communications with genuine pole-to-pole coverage. This satellite's 86.4° orbital inclination is a defining Iridium design choice — high enough to cover the entire globe including polar regions that lower-inclination satellite constellations cannot reach.

42811.d: One of the 66 operational Iridium NEXT satellites, part of a system that also hosts secondary scientific payloads including GPS radio occultation sensors feeding NOAA's commercial weather program. Building extra science payloads into a telecommunications constellation is an efficient use of launch mass that few satellite operators have replicated at this scale.

42812.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, providing the mesh-network satellite telephony that has made Iridium the standard choice for military units, cargo ships, and polar researchers who need guaranteed connectivity anywhere on Earth. Its redesigned antenna array eliminated the bright flares that made the original 1990s Iridium satellites famous among skywatchers.

42814.d: A Greek-led communications satellite serving southeastern Europe and extending coverage to South Asia. Hellas-Sat 3 was built in partnership with Israel Aerospace Industries and jointly owned by Greek and Saudi interests — a commercial collaboration that brought diverse investment to expand Greek satellite capacity. It serves broadcasting, broadband, and maritime communications across the Mediterranean and Middle East.

42815.d: An Indian C-band and extended C-band satellite with a unique additional mission — hosting a COSPAS-SARSAT search and rescue transponder. GSAT-17 relays emergency distress signals from personal locator beacons, emergency position-indicating radio beacons (EPIRBs), and emergency locator transmitters across India and the surrounding oceans. When an Indian fishing vessel or trekker in the Himalayas activates a distress beacon, GSAT-17 is often the satellite that receives and relays that emergency call to rescue coordination centers.

42818.d: Intelsat's second Epic high-throughput satellite, providing combined wide-beam Ku-band and high-throughput Ka-band services over the Americas and the Atlantic. IS-35E is positioned to serve the critical US-Europe corridor — the most heavily trafficked transoceanic satellite route in the world. The Epic platform's simultaneous delivery of traditional broadcast services and modern broadband internet from the same spacecraft is the model for how GEO operators compete with LEO constellations.

42829.d: Built by students at TU Berlin, TechnoSat tests miniaturized satellite components under actual space flight conditions. Its passive attitude control system and experimental sensors have been validated on orbit and feed directly into Germany's next generation of small satellite systems — university satellites doing the hardware qualification work that makes future missions reliable.

42831.d: Named for its laptop-like form factor, this German small satellite was built by students at the University of Stuttgart. It carries a multi-spectral Earth imager and tests new on-board computing systems for the harsh radiation environment of low Earth orbit. Flying Laptop represents Germany's university tradition of producing flight-heritage technology that feeds directly into commercial and scientific missions.

42907.d: A classified Russian military satellite in geostationary orbit. Russia routinely launches highly capable military satellites under the Cosmos designation, which provides a level of ambiguity about their missions. COSMOS 2520's orbital position is monitored by independent tracking organizations, but its specific capabilities remain undisclosed. Russia's classified GEO presence mirrors the US approach of maintaining unacknowledged national security payloads in the strategic geostationary belt.

42915.d: The most recent addition to NASA's space communications backbone. TDRS-13 provides continuous relay capability for human spaceflight and robotic science missions from a fixed geostationary position. As the ISS approaches retirement, TDRS will become critical for supporting the commercial space stations planned to replace it, and TDRS-13 will be among the newest assets supporting that transition.

42917.d: The geostationary component of Japan's Michibiki constellation — parked permanently at 127°E over the Pacific. QZS-3 provides constant GPS augmentation signals over Japan without the orbital motion of the quasi-zenith satellites, providing redundancy and ensuring that Japan always has at least one Michibiki satellite nearly overhead. It also carries experimental Ka-band inter-satellite links and a Search and Rescue payload to relay distress signals from maritime emergencies.

42920.d: Taiwan's first independently developed Earth observation satellite, deliberately built with 70% domestic content to grow Taiwan's space engineering capability. Its true value was in the engineers it trained — who built Taiwan's next-generation FORMOSAT-7 constellation, establishing Taiwan as a serious space technology nation.

42934.d: Hispasat's most powerful Americas satellite, incorporating high-throughput Ka-band payload alongside traditional Ku-band capacity. Amazonas-5 provides the backbone for Brazilian satellite internet services targeting the country's rural connectivity gap — 30 million Brazilians lack any broadband access, most in the Amazon region. The satellite enables Brazil's government programs aiming to connect every school and health post across the country's vast interior.

42942.d: AsiaSat's newest and most powerful satellite, providing enhanced C-band and Ku-band capacity across the Asia-Pacific. AsiaSat-9 strengthens the Hong Kong-based operator's position in the competitive Asian broadcasting market as it faces increasing competition from Chinese operators and global giants like SES and Intelsat. Its high-power transponders serve both traditional broadcasting distribution and emerging broadband services across a region stretching from Japan to the Indian Ocean.

42945.d: The lead satellite of China's first Yaogan-30 ELINT triplet — the opening salvo of China's most prolific naval surveillance satellite program. Launched September 29, 2017 on a Long March 2C, Yaogan-30 A and its two companions operate in a 600 km, 35° inclined orbit that provides coverage of the Pacific and Indian Oceans — the operating areas of US Navy carrier strike groups that China's anti-ship ballistic missiles are designed to target. The triplet formation allows three satellites to compare the time-of-arrival of radio signals from the same emitter and triangulate its precise location — geolocating ships by their radar and communications emissions even when optically invisible under cloud cover or darkness. Western analysts consider the Yaogan-30 series the Chinese equivalent of the US Naval Ocean Surveillance System (NOSS), part of the kill chain that would cue Dong Feng 21 and Dong Feng 26 anti-ship ballistic missiles.

42946.d: Second satellite of the Yaogan-30 group 02 triplet, launched November 24, 2017 into a plane positioned 119° west of the original group 01 formation. This second Yaogan-30 triplet marked the beginning of the program's rapid expansion — rather than building one satellite formation and stopping, China began systematically adding new orbital planes, each populated by an identical three-satellite triangulation group, to build denser and more frequent naval surveillance coverage across the Pacific.

42947.d: Third satellite completing the Yaogan-30 group 02 triplet. With two full triplets now operational barely two months apart, China demonstrated it could replicate the Yaogan-30 naval signals triangulation formation on a genuinely industrial launch cadence — a pattern of rapid, repeated deployment that would define the entire program for the next seven years.

42950.d: Intelsat's third Epic satellite providing high-throughput Ka-band broadband and Ku-band broadcasting over the Atlantic, covering Europe, the Middle East, and the Americas simultaneously. IS-37E's coverage spans more than any previous Epic satellite — from the Americas through the Atlantic to Europe and Africa. The Epic fleet represents Intelsat's multi-billion dollar bet that high-throughput GEO satellites can compete with Starlink and LEO broadband by offering more capacity, wider coverage, and lower latency than low-orbit constellations.

42951.d: Japan's newest direct-to-home broadcasting satellite at 110°E, providing the enhanced power and capacity needed for Japan's nationwide 4K Ultra HD broadcasting service. BSAT-4A delivers 4K content from NHK and commercial broadcasters to home viewers using consumer satellite receivers — part of Japan's nationally coordinated digital broadcasting upgrade that aims to make 4K the standard for Japanese home television by 2025.

42955.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites launched in a 2018 batch, continuing the systematic eight-launch replacement of Iridium's original constellation. Each satellite in the network can relay calls and data to its neighbors via inter-satellite links, creating a genuinely global mesh network unlike almost any other commercial satellite system in operation today.

42956.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, one of the network's earliest-numbered satellites in the current fleet. Iridium's story remains one of the space industry's most dramatic reversals — a bankrupt 1990s venture transformed into essential infrastructure now trusted by the US military, commercial shipping, polar science, and global aviation surveillance.

42957.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, providing global mobile satellite communications and hosting the Aireon aircraft surveillance payload. Since Aireon went operational, air traffic controllers over the North Atlantic — previously limited to procedural separation with no real-time radar — can now track aircraft with radar-equivalent precision, a genuine aviation safety breakthrough enabled by this satellite constellation.

42958.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, one of 66 satellites providing pole-to-pole global coverage through an inter-satellite mesh network. This design, unusual among commercial satellite systems, allows Iridium to maintain service continuity even in regions where the nearest ground station might be thousands of kilometers away.

42959.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, replacing the original 1990s Iridium constellation whose satellites were beginning to fail faster than the company could keep up with replacements. The full Iridium NEXT deployment, completed via eight SpaceX Falcon 9 launches by 2019, secured the network's operational future for another 15+ years.

42960.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, contributing to a genuinely global mesh network offering satellite phone and data connectivity with no coverage gaps anywhere on Earth, including the poles. This universal coverage — impossible to replicate with lower-inclination or regionally focused constellations — remains Iridium's defining commercial advantage.

42961.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, part of a network whose predecessor generation created the famous 'Iridium flare' phenomenon — brief, brilliant flashes of reflected sunlight bright enough to see in broad daylight, once predictable to the second by amateur astronomy software. Iridium NEXT's redesigned antennas quietly ended this era when the last flare-capable original satellite deorbited.

42962.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, providing satellite communications for maritime, aviation, government, and consumer customers through a network architecture built around inter-satellite mesh relay rather than dependence on nearby ground stations. This satellite is one of 66 identical spacecraft maintaining that continuous global mesh.

42963.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, hosting both core satellite telephony services and the Aireon real-time global air traffic surveillance payload. Aireon has transformed oceanic and polar aviation safety, replacing decades-old procedural separation standards with genuine radar-equivalent aircraft tracking capability delivered from space.

42964.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, one of 66 satellites completing the replacement of Iridium's original 1990s fleet. The full transition, executed across eight SpaceX Falcon 9 launches between 2017 and 2019, represents one of the largest and most operationally complex satellite constellation replacement campaigns any commercial company has ever completed.

42965.d: The fourth Michibiki satellite, completing Japan's initial quasi-zenith constellation. With four satellites, Japan achieved 24-hour continuous high-elevation coverage — ensuring that at any moment, Japanese users have a Michibiki satellite nearly overhead that can provide sub-centimeter positioning corrections. This enabled Japan to begin deploying centimeter-accurate autonomous agricultural machinery across its rice paddies, reducing labor requirements in Japan's aging farming sector.

42967.d: A joint SES-EchoStar satellite at 105°W serving the US market with Ku-band broadcasting and C-band distribution. SES-11 / EchoStar-105 exemplifies the trend of satellite operators combining forces on a single spacecraft to share costs and launch risk. The satellite carries SES's cable distribution capacity alongside EchoStar's direct broadcast television payload — two commercial competitors sharing hardware while serving different customers.

42969.d: Europe's atmospheric chemistry sentinel maps air pollution globally every single day at 3.5 km resolution. Its TROPOMI instrument measures nitrogen dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ozone, methane, and carbon monoxide simultaneously. Within hours of a factory explosion or wildfire, Sentinel-5P shows the toxic plume drifting across continents — pollution made visible from orbit in near real-time.

42984.d: South Korea's military communications satellite, providing secure Ku-band connectivity for Korean armed forces including the army, navy, air force, and Marine Corps. KoreaSat-5A combines commercial services with military-reserved capacity, providing Korea's defense establishment with protected satellite communications — essential for a military facing the most intensely militarized border in the world and operating under constant threat of North Korean electronic warfare.
42984.a: KT SAT / Republic of Korea Military

42987.d: SkySat-C11, part of Planet's mature operational constellation. By this point Planet had transformed from a startup to the world's largest commercial Earth observation operator — capturing more images of Earth per day than all previous commercial operators combined.

42988.d: The tenth SkySat satellite. Planet's open data approach for lower-resolution imagery, combined with its commercial SkySat service, established a model for Earth observation that has been copied by dozens of competitors worldwide — democratizing space-based imagery in ways previously unimaginable.

42989.d: SkySat-C9 is part of the constellation that made Planet Labs the most prolific satellite operator in history. Planet's strategy — many cheap satellites instead of few expensive ones — proved that Moore's Law could be applied to Earth observation, making space-based imagery cheaper and more accessible every year.

42990.d: SkySat-C8 contributed to Planet achieving the milestone of daily global coverage at 72 cm resolution, making the company the world's first commercial operator to offer that capability. This marked the moment satellite imagery transitioned from a specialty product to a broadly accessible commercial service.

42991.d: Part of Planet's rapid 2017 expansion. The SkySat satellites offer both still imagery and 90-second video clips — operators choose the mode most useful for their application. For tracking moving vessels, vehicles, or crowds, video from orbit provides information no number of still images can replicate.

42992.d: SkySat-C6, part of a six-satellite batch that dramatically accelerated Planet's coverage. With 12+ SkySats in orbit, Planet achieved near-global daily coverage at 72 cm — the threshold where its constellation became a genuine alternative to traditional government reconnaissance programs for many applications.

43010.d: FY-3D introduced China's first space-based hyperspectral greenhouse gas monitor, measuring CO2 and methane from orbit. Its atmospheric soundings feed China's rapidly improving global weather models — now matching European and American models in accuracy over China and neighboring regions, a remarkable advance achieved in under a decade.

43013.d: The first Joint Polar Satellite System satellite collects more data per orbit than any previous NOAA weather satellite. Its VIIRS instrument maps global land surface, sea ice, ocean color, and fire locations every day — the complete surface picture that feeds the 7-day forecasts you check on your phone.

43022.d: A Jilin-1 satellite from the early expansion phase of China's largest commercial Earth observation constellation, providing high-resolution imaging with a 3.3-day revisit cycle. As the fourth-numbered satellite in a program that would eventually exceed 130 spacecraft, Jilin-1 04 represents the constellation's formative period — before the mass-production techniques that later allowed China to launch dozens of Jilin-1 satellites in a single mission.

43023.d: Part of the early Jilin-1 constellation build-out, launched in 2017 as China's commercial remote sensing capability continued expanding beyond its four-satellite founding mission. Each new Jilin-1 satellite incrementally improved the constellation's global revisit frequency — the core metric that determines how useful a commercial imaging constellation is for time-sensitive applications like disaster response, agricultural monitoring, and change detection.

43024.d: One of the early operational Jilin-1 satellites launched in 2017, contributing to the constellation's initial expansion beyond its founding four-satellite mission. By this point Chang Guang had established the basic production and launch cadence that would eventually scale into the mass-deployment model responsible for over 130 Jilin-1 satellites reaching orbit by the mid-2020s.

43028.d: Lead satellite of the Yaogan-30 group 03 triplet, launched December 25, 2017 — the third Yaogan-30 formation in exactly three months, positioned in a plane 120° east of the original group 01. This launch cadence, a new triplet roughly every month through the winter of 2017-2018, revealed the scale of China's ambition for the program well before most Western analysts had assessed its true naval surveillance significance.

43029.d: Second satellite of the Yaogan-30 group 03 triplet, part of China's third naval ELINT triangulation formation launched within a single quarter. Three simultaneous triplets by the end of 2017 meant nine total Yaogan-30 satellites tracking Pacific naval emissions from three separate, evenly spaced orbital planes.

43030.d: Third satellite completing the Yaogan-30 group 03 triplet, launched on Christmas Day 2017. With this triplet operational, China had tripled its Yaogan-30 naval surveillance density in just three months — a launch tempo that would only accelerate as the program matured through the following years.

43039.d: Algeria's first national communications satellite, a milestone for North African space capabilities. AlComSat-1 provides direct-to-home TV broadcasting, broadband internet, and government communications across Algeria — a country the size of Western Europe where terrestrial infrastructure struggles to reach remote Saharan regions. The satellite was built by China Great Wall Industry Corporation and represents Algeria's growing space partnership with China.

43070.d: One of the later Iridium NEXT satellites launched in the 2018-2019 completion phase of the constellation's replacement campaign, providing global mesh-network satellite communications. Iridium remains the only commercial satellite operator offering genuinely uninterrupted pole-to-pole coverage, a defining technical advantage over regionally optimized competitors.

43071.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, one of the final satellites to complete the eight-launch replacement campaign that modernized Iridium's aging original fleet. This satellite continues supporting the mesh network architecture that lets Iridium relay communications satellite-to-satellite across the globe without requiring dense ground station infrastructure.

43072.d: One of the Iridium NEXT satellites, part of a constellation whose original 1990s predecessor became famous for producing brilliant, predictable 'Iridium flares' as sunlight glinted off its antenna panels. Iridium NEXT's redesigned antennas eliminated the effect — a small loss for skywatchers, but part of the broader engineering improvements that made the new constellation more power-efficient and reliable.

43073.d: Part of the Iridium NEXT constellation, one of the last satellites launched to complete the full 66-satellite replacement of Iridium's original network. With this deployment complete by 2019, Iridium secured a modernized, mesh-networked, globally continuous satellite communications system expected to serve customers well into the 2030s.

43081.d: Lead satellite of the Yaogan-30 group 04 triplet, launched January 25, 2018 — the fourth Yaogan-30 formation in four months, an almost monthly cadence unprecedented for any Chinese classified satellite program to that point. By early 2018, China operated twelve Yaogan-30 satellites across four orbital planes, a naval surveillance density that dramatically compressed the time between successive triangulation passes over any given stretch of Pacific ocean.

43082.d: Second satellite of the Yaogan-30 group 04 triplet, part of China's fourth naval ELINT formation launched within five months of the program's 2017 debut. The rapid succession of triplet launches through this period reflected a deliberate, well-resourced program rather than a series of incremental, cautiously-paced tests.

43083.d: Third satellite completing the Yaogan-30 group 04 triplet, launched January 2018. After this fourth deployment, China paused Yaogan-30 launches for over a year — the program's rapid four-triplet debut giving way to a more measured, multi-year cadence for subsequent groups.

43099.d: Part of China's SuperView-1 (Gaojing-1) commercial high-resolution imaging constellation, operated by China Siwei Surveying and Mapping Technology. SuperView-1 satellites deliver 0.5-meter panchromatic resolution — among the sharpest commercially available imagery from any Chinese operator — competing directly with Western operators like Planet's SkySat and Maxar's WorldView in the global commercial satellite imagery market. SuperView-1 03 joined the constellation in 2018 as part of China's broader push to establish commercial credibility in the international Earth observation data market.
43099.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging

43111.d: Cartosat-2F represents India's reliable high-resolution imaging workhorse. The series' quality and reliability helped establish ISRO's credibility as a world-class Earth observation manufacturer — contributing to India's growing portfolio of satellite technology exports to developing nations seeking affordable Earth observation capability.

43159.d: Part of China's expanding Jilin-1 commercial imaging constellation, launched in 2018 as Chang Guang continued building toward its stated goal of 138 satellites by 2025 — a target the company later expanded to 300. Jilin-1 07 contributes to the constellation's core mission of providing sub-meter resolution imagery with increasingly frequent global revisit, competing directly with Western commercial operators like Planet and Maxar.

43160.d: The eighth satellite in China's first commercial Earth observation constellation, operated by Chang Guang Satellite Technology (CGSTL) — a spin-off from the Chinese Academy of Sciences' Changchun Institute of Optics. Jilin-1-08 is part of the 'Shipin' (Smart Video) sub-series, capturing 4K HD video of the Earth's surface at approximately 1.2 meter resolution from 535 km. Jilin-1 drew global attention when it released footage of a US Air Force F-22 Raptor in flight — then drew controversy in March 2026 when it published detailed post-strike damage imagery of US military bases in the Middle East during the Iran conflict, at a time when Planet Labs and Maxar had restricted their imagery at the US government's request. The constellation's 'military-civil fusion' DNA means commercial data and national security are not separate considerations in this program.

43162.d: The third SBIRS satellite, adding capacity to the missile warning constellation.
