49003.d: Second satellite in the Kuanfu wide-swath imaging sub-series, providing 50 cm resolution across a 136 km footprint — a specification that trades some resolution for dramatically expanded coverage compared to Jilin-1's high-resolution Gaofen line. Kuanfu-01B's wide-area context imaging complements the narrower, sharper Gaofen-03D satellites, giving Chang Guang customers a choice between broad regional surveys and pinpoint detailed imagery depending on the application.

49008.d: FY-3E made history as the world's first operational polar weather satellite in a dawn-dusk orbit — crossing poles during sunrise and sunset when atmospheric changes are most rapid. ECMWF in Europe calls its unique sounding data 'transformational' for their forecast models. China pioneered an orbital slot no other nation had dared attempt.

49011.d: The fifth first-generation Tianlian satellite, extending China's orbital communications relay network. Tianlian-1-05 provides essential connectivity for the Tiangong Space Station crew — relaying voice communications, experimental data, medical monitoring, and high-definition video between astronauts and China's mission control in Beijing. The satellite also supports China's deep space missions and robotic scientific payloads in low Earth orbit.

49026.d: Lead satellite of the Yaogan-30 group 11 triplet, launched 2023 — the program's eleventh generation and, as of this deployment, its most recent numbered expansion before China's newer Yaogan-35 and Yaogan-36 satellite families began appearing in similar low-altitude orbits, suggesting Yaogan-30's role may gradually transition to these successor programs.

49028.d: Second satellite of the Yaogan-30 group 11 triplet, part of China's eleventh and most recent naval ELINT surveillance formation. Thirty-three satellites across eleven Yaogan-30 triplets, deployed over six years since September 2017, represent one of the most sustained and successful Chinese military satellite constellation buildouts of the past decade.

49029.d: Third satellite completing the Yaogan-30 group 11 triplet, launched 2023. As China's newer Yaogan-35 and Yaogan-36 mixed-sensor satellite families began appearing at similar orbital altitudes shortly after, group 11 may represent the Yaogan-30 program approaching a natural handoff point to its next-generation naval surveillance successors.

49049.d: The fourth satellite in China's Tianhui-1 ('Sky Drawing') stereo-topographic mapping series, launched July 2021 from Jiuquan aboard a Long March 2D. Operated by the PLA's Navigation and Survey Bureau, Tianhui-1 satellites carry a three-line array panchromatic camera producing 5-meter resolution stereo imagery used to generate precise 3D terrain models of China and surrounding regions — digital elevation data essential for everything from artillery targeting calculations to civilian infrastructure planning. By 2021, the Tianhui-1 program had been building China's independent topographic dataset for a full decade.
49049.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

49055.d: The newest Embratel Star One satellite, providing next-generation Ka-band high-throughput broadband across Brazil and Latin America. Star One D2 targets Brazil's exploding demand for satellite internet driven by the country's digital inclusion programs aiming to connect schools, health clinics, and government buildings in remote Amazon and interior regions where terrestrial broadband is decades away.

49056.d: The world's first fully software-defined commercial satellite — one that can be completely reprogrammed while in orbit. Every aspect of Eutelsat Quantum's operation can be reconfigured from the ground: its eight beams can be reshaped and redirected to any coverage area, its frequency allocations can be changed, its power distribution adjusted, and it can even detect and automatically counter jamming attempts by switching frequencies in real time. US defense contractor Peraton holds the majority of its capacity, using it for military communications and surveillance missions that change too frequently for a fixed-payload satellite to accommodate. Built by Airbus with ESA support, Quantum represents the future of satellite design — a universal platform that can be any satellite its operators need it to be.

49062.d: A recent Chinese military communications satellite providing enhanced capacity to the PLA's secure communications network. China's military satellite communications architecture has evolved substantially — from basic voice relay to high-throughput digital networks capable of supporting unmanned systems, intelligence data relay, and the full bandwidth demands of modern military operations.

49070.d: The twin to Pleiades Neo 3, flying offset by 180 degrees for twice-daily revisit. Together the pair provides 30 cm imagery of any target on Earth within 24 hours — a capability that until recently required classified military satellite access. Commercial space has permanently changed who can see what.

49071.d: Lead satellite of the second Tianhui-2 formation pair, launched in 2021 to expand China's radar interferometry mapping capability. With two operational Tianhui-2 pairs in orbit, the PLA's Navigation and Survey Bureau gained the ability to task multiple simultaneous all-weather elevation mapping missions — doubling the throughput of what had been, with the first pair alone, a capacity-constrained program for updating China's strategic terrain databases.
49071.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

49072.d: The companion to Tianhui 2-02A, completing the second Tianhui-2 interferometric SAR mapping formation. Flying in precise formation with its partner, this satellite pair enables China to continuously refresh its 3D elevation models of strategically significant terrain — border regions, contested territories, and areas of military interest — without waiting for clear weather windows that optical satellites require.
49072.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

49115.d: The seventh TJS satellite, continuing China's classified geostationary military program. China has launched more classified GEO satellites than any other nation in recent years, reflecting the PLA's view of space as an operational warfighting domain rather than solely a support environment. TJS-7's mission remains officially undisclosed, but its rapid succession in a growing series suggests operational military communications are the primary purpose.

49125.d: A Chinese direct-to-home broadcasting satellite providing Ku-band TV distribution across China. ChinaSat-9B delivers Chinese state and commercial television channels to homes in China's rural and remote regions, including Tibet, Xinjiang, and Inner Mongolia — areas where satellite TV is often the only access to national television programming and a key tool for national cultural cohesion in linguistically and ethnically diverse territories.

49256.d: Part of the Jilin-1 Gaofen-02 sub-series, one of several satellites in this generation contributing to China's commercial high-resolution imaging capacity. As Chang Guang's constellation grew through the early 2020s, the Gaofen-02 line's imagery increasingly fed both civilian commercial applications and, per most Western analyst assessments, China's military-civil fusion intelligence architecture.

49260.d: The latest Landsat maintains Earth's most valuable land surface archive — 50 continuous years of consistent imagery of every continent. Scientists have used the combined record to track loss of 40% of Earth's temperate forests, growth of 37 of the 40 largest cities, and retreat of virtually every glacier on the planet. Arguably the most scientifically productive Earth observation program in history.

49330.d: The most operationally significant Chinese GEO technology demonstration satellite. In 2022, Shijian-21 successfully docked with a dead Chinese satellite in graveyard orbit and relocated it to a different parking slot — demonstrating China's ability to maneuver non-cooperative objects in space. This capability — grasping, moving, and releasing another satellite — is directly applicable to removing adversary satellites from their operational orbits. It was observed by US Space Force and widely discussed as a milestone in Chinese anti-satellite capability development.
49330.t: Technology Demo / Satellite Servicing / Anti-Satellite Capable

49332.d: SES's most advanced satellite for the Americas, combining Ka-band high-throughput broadband with a flexible digital payload that can be partially reconfigured from the ground. SES-17 provides over 200 Gbps of Ka-band capacity for aeronautical, maritime, and enterprise broadband across the Americas and the Atlantic. Its digital payload allows SES to redirect capacity between beams in response to shifting demand — a precursor to the fully software-defined satellite model exemplified by Eutelsat Quantum.

49333.d: The newest generation of French military communications satellites, providing dramatic improvements in capacity, security, and anti-jam performance. Syracuse-4A uses quantum key distribution-compatible encryption and advanced waveforms designed to defeat adversary electronic warfare. France's investment in next-generation military space capabilities reflects the growing importance of the space domain in European security policy.
49333.a: French Ministry of Defence / Thales Alenia

49336.d: Japan's first quasi-zenith satellite replacement — part of the Michibiki (Guidance) constellation that augments GPS with Japan-specific signals. Quasi-zenith satellites follow a figure-8 orbit that keeps them nearly overhead Japan for most of the day, solving a problem unique to Japan's urban geography: tall buildings in Tokyo and Osaka block GPS signals at low elevation angles. Michibiki's high-elevation signals cut through urban canyons where GPS fails, enabling centimeter-level navigation for autonomous vehicles across Japanese cities.

49338.d: A later member of the Jilin-1 Gaofen-02 sub-series, contributing to the constellation's continued expansion into the early 2020s. By this point Jilin-1 had already established itself as China's largest and most operationally significant commercial Earth observation program, with imagery products marketed globally through HEAD Aerospace's international distribution network.

49383.d: Lead satellite of the second Yaogan-32 pair, launched in 2021 to expand China's paired radar reconnaissance capability first established with the 2019 Yaogan-32 formation. The consistent two-satellite grouping across both Yaogan-32 generations continues to suggest an interferometric SAR mission distinct from the country's ELINT triplet naval surveillance program.

49384.d: Companion satellite completing the second Yaogan-32 pair, launched in 2021. Two generations of Yaogan-32 pairs, each flying in close formation rather than the wider triangulation geometry of ELINT triplets, reinforces the assessment that this sub-series serves a synthetic aperture radar interferometry role within China's broader reconnaissance satellite architecture.

49390.d: One of the Yaogan-35 (01) group satellites, launched in 2021 as part of a distinct new Chinese reconnaissance satellite family operating at the lower-altitude, 35°-inclination orbital regime pioneered by the Yaogan-30 program. Yaogan-35 groups combine optical, SAR, and possibly ELINT sensors in mixed formations rather than the single-sensor-type triplets of earlier Yaogan generations, reflecting an evolution toward multi-role reconnaissance groupings.

49393.d: Part of the Yaogan-35 (01) satellite group, sharing the 35°-inclination orbital regime established by the Yaogan-30 ELINT program but representing a distinct, more recent generation of Chinese reconnaissance satellites. Yaogan-35 groups are assessed to combine multiple sensor types within a single formation, a more integrated approach than the single-purpose triplets that characterized earlier Yaogan naval surveillance generations.

49505.d: A Chinese military or dual-use communications satellite operating in geostationary orbit. The '1D' designation in the Zhongxing series indicates a classified or government-priority payload. China's satellite communications architecture deliberately blends civil and military functions, making it difficult for outside analysts to definitively characterize individual satellites as purely civil or purely military.

49817.d: A US Space Force technology demonstration satellite hosted on a geostationary platform as part of the Space Technology Program. STPSAT-6 tests advanced communications technologies, space environment sensors, and experimental payloads in the harsh GEO radiation environment. The STPSAT program provides a flight-test pathway for emerging technologies before they're incorporated into operational military satellites.

49954.d: X-ray polarimetry — measuring how X-rays from cosmic sources are oriented — was theorized as a powerful tool for 60 years but never successfully done from orbit until IXPE. Launched in 2021, NASA's Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer measures not just where X-rays come from but how they're polarized, revealing the geometry of magnetic fields around black holes, neutron stars, and supernova remnants. Its first results showed that the Crab Nebula's pulsar jet is far more ordered and structured than any model had predicted.

49961.d: Lead satellite of the fifth Shijian-6 generation pair, launched in 2021 — over 15 years after the program's first satellites reached orbit. The Shijian-6 series' remarkable longevity as an active research line reflects how fundamental space radiation data remains to Chinese satellite engineering even as the country's broader space program has grown vastly more sophisticated and ambitious.

49962.d: Companion to Shijian-6 05A, completing the newest Shijian-6 generation pair. Fifteen years and five satellite generations after the program began, Shijian-6 continues to be China's primary vehicle for direct, in-situ measurement of the radiation environment that every other Chinese satellite must be engineered to survive.

50001.d: Russia's latest Express satellite, continuing the modernization of the country's commercial satellite fleet under difficult conditions created by Western technology restrictions. Express AMU-7 covers Russia's most densely populated regions with Ku-band TV distribution and Ka-band broadband, supporting the Russian government's goal of extending satellite internet access to rural populations across the country.

50002.d: One of Russia's newest commercial communications satellites, part of the modernization of Russia's aging geostationary commercial fleet. Express AMU-3 provides Ku and Ka-band services for Russian customers, replacing capacity from earlier satellites approaching end of life. Russia has faced significant challenges procuring satellite components under sanctions, making domestic satellite manufacturing capability increasingly strategic.

50005.d: The second Tianlian-2 satellite, completing the initial next-generation pair. Tianlian 2-02 significantly expands coverage to ensure Tiangong crew have nearly continuous data relay contact regardless of the station's orbital position. China's growing human spaceflight program — planning longer missions and more frequent crew rotations — demands the communication infrastructure that Tianlian-2 provides.

50212.d: Turkey's Ka-band high-throughput satellite, the most powerful Turkish communications satellite ever built. Turksat-5B provides broadband internet services across Turkey with a capacity exceeding all previous Turkish satellites combined, targeting Turkey's ambitious national broadband connectivity goals and competing with terrestrial fiber in underserved areas. Turkey's Turksat is positioning itself as a provider of satellite services to Central Asian and Middle Eastern nations along historical Silk Road connectivity corridors.

50319.d: The first of Inmarsat's sixth-generation satellites — a massive 9-ton spacecraft combining L-band safety services with Ka-band broadband in a single platform. Inmarsat-6 represents the future of maritime communications: a single satellite providing both the regulatory-mandated GMDSS emergency communication and gigabit-class commercial broadband, making it the most capable maritime communications satellite ever built. Its L-band payload maintains continuity of international maritime safety services while its Ka-band payload transforms what ships can do with connectivity.

50321.d: First of a pair of Chinese technology verification satellites in geostationary orbit. The Shiyan ('Experiment') designation covers an enormously wide range of actual missions — from genuine academic research payloads to operational military technology tests — making it one of China's most versatile classification covers. Shiyan-12 01 and 02 were launched together, suggesting coordinated capability testing requiring two platforms operating in concert from geostationary altitude.

50322.d: The companion to Shiyan-12 01, flying in coordination for technology verification purposes. The Shiyan-12 pair represents China's systematic approach to GEO technology validation — launching paired spacecraft to test capabilities requiring two platforms simultaneously. China's proliferating experimental GEO satellite programs (TJS, SJ, Shiyan) collectively form one of the largest experimental geostationary satellite operations in history, advancing capabilities that feed into future commercial and military systems.

50572.d: The first satellite of China's Tianhui-4 mapping series — 'Tianhui' (天绘) translating as 'Sky Drawing.' Developed by Aerospace Dongfanghong Satellite Co. under CASC and operated by the PLA's Navigation and Survey Bureau, Tianhui-4A carries stereo cameras for generating three-dimensional terrain maps of China and neighboring regions. The Tianhui series produces the digital elevation models that underpin everything from military terrain analysis to civil infrastructure planning — the same 3D surface data that autonomous vehicles, precision weapons, and city planners all depend upon. The PLA's investment in independent topographic mapping from orbit means China does not depend on commercial or foreign elevation data for any territory it considers strategically significant.
50572.a: CASC / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

50573.d: The second Tianhui-4 satellite, launched into a near-polar 89° orbit distinct from the sun-synchronous orbits typical of most Chinese mapping satellites. Little is publicly confirmed about Tianhui-4's specific mission — Gunter's Space Page and other tracking sources describe it only as 'likely an earth observation satellite of unknown purpose operated by the PLA.' The near-polar orbit suggests global rather than regional coverage priorities, a departure from the China-focused mapping emphasis of earlier Tianhui generations.

50574.d: Another TJS satellite observed performing anomalous maneuvers and releasing objects in geostationary orbit. TJS-9 attracted significant attention from US Space Force and independent analysts who tracked its proximity operations — approaching other satellites and releasing sub-satellite objects. These activities are consistent with China testing counter-satellite inspection, electronic intelligence, or jamming capabilities in the geostationary belt.

51280.d: A classified US government satellite in geostationary orbit launched in 2021. Its mission is unacknowledged. Independent space tracking organizations monitor its orbit, which is consistent with national security communications or intelligence collection payloads routinely placed in GEO by the NRO and US Space Force. The classified nature reflects the sensitivity of capabilities that peer adversaries would find most valuable to understand and counter.

51444.d: The second COSMO-SkyMed Second Generation satellite completes Italy's upgraded radar constellation, achieving sub-6-hour revisit over any Mediterranean target. Italy now operates one of the world's most capable Earth observation systems — serving both military intelligence and civilian environmental monitoring from the same two-satellite constellation.

51850.d: The newest GOES-West satellite provides the most detailed surveillance of the Pacific Ocean and western North America ever achieved from geostationary orbit. It simultaneously watches for California wildfires, tracks Pacific typhoons toward Hawaii, monitors Alaskan volcanoes, and detects Rocky Mountain lightning — all in real time from 35,800 km up.

51950.d: A cataloged fragment or sub-component released during a 2022 rocket launch, tracked as 'Object E' from that mission's payload group. Objects like this — pieces of payload adapters, deployment hardware, or small debris shed during separation events — make up a significant share of everything the US Space Surveillance Network tracks in orbit, even though most carry no further function once released.

52084.d: Yaogan-34 (02), the second satellite bearing the Yaogan-34 designation, launched in 2022 into the same 63.4° high-altitude orbital regime as its predecessor. Two solo Yaogan-34 deployments at an ELINT-characteristic inclination, rather than the triplet formations typical of that orbital band, continues to suggest China may be evaluating alternative naval surveillance architectures beyond the established three-satellite triangulation model.

52255.d: A Chinese C-band communications satellite extending ChinaSat's commercial coverage. Zhongxing-6D serves a combination of Chinese broadcaster requirements, corporate network customers, and government services across the Asia-Pacific. China's commercial satellite operators have benefited from government support that allows them to offer competitive pricing in international markets.

52320.d: The first satellite of China's next-generation SuperView Neo constellation, launched 2022 with improved resolution and revisit capability over the original SuperView-1 series. SuperView Neo-1 targets sub-0.5-meter resolution imagery, positioning China Siwei to compete more directly with the newest generation of Western commercial imaging satellites like Pléiades Neo and Planet's Pelican constellation — the global commercial imagery market has become genuinely competitive across US, European, and Chinese operators.
52320.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

52322.d: Second satellite of the SuperView Neo-1 pair, launched alongside SuperView Neo-1 01 to establish twin-satellite coverage for China's next-generation commercial imaging constellation. The paired deployment allows more frequent revisit of any given target — a competitive necessity as commercial Earth observation increasingly competes on speed and freshness of imagery rather than resolution alone.
52322.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

52443.d: Third satellite in the original Kuanfu wide-swath sub-series, launched May 5, 2022 alongside a large batch of Gaofen-03D satellites on the same Long March 2D mission. Kuanfu-01C rounds out the first-generation wide-field imaging fleet before Chang Guang introduced the significantly lighter and more capable Kuanfu-02 generation, which cut satellite mass from 1,200 kg down to 230 kg while improving resolution to 0.5 meters.

52444.d: One of the mass-produced Jilin-1 Gaofen-03D satellites, launched May 5, 2022 as part of a batch that put multiple Gaofen-03D units into orbit on a single Long March 2D. The Gaofen-03D line represents Chang Guang's mature manufacturing philosophy: 75 cm panchromatic resolution at a mass low enough to fit nine satellites on one rocket, distributing them across different orbital planes to compress the constellation's global revisit time without a proportional cost increase.
52444.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

52445.d: Part of the same May 2022 mass-production batch of Jilin-1 Gaofen-03D satellites, contributing another node to China's densifying commercial imaging constellation. Each Gaofen-03D added to the fleet incrementally reduces the average time between images of any given point on Earth — the metric that ultimately determines a commercial imaging constellation's usefulness for time-sensitive monitoring applications.
52445.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

52446.d: One of nine Jilin-1 Gaofen-03D satellites launched together on May 5, 2022 — a single-rocket deployment that exemplifies Chang Guang's shift toward batch production and mass launch. By spreading nine identical satellites across multiple orbital planes in one mission, CGSTL achieved a step-change improvement in constellation revisit frequency that would have taken years to accumulate through individual satellite launches.
52446.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

52448.d: Part of the May 2022 Jilin-1 Gaofen-03D batch deployment, contributing to China's largest commercial satellite constellation. The scale of this single mission — nine satellites deployed simultaneously — reflected China's growing confidence in mass-producing capable imaging satellites at costs that would have seemed impossible to Western operators just years earlier, fundamentally changing the economics of the global commercial Earth observation market.
52448.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

52754.d: The third satellite of Cluster 5. Each successive HawkEye 360 cluster incorporates software-defined radio improvements that expand frequency coverage and improve sensitivity — detecting weaker signals and covering new frequency bands used by emerging communication systems.

52756.d: Second satellite of Cluster 5. HawkEye 360's growing constellation attracted significant US intelligence community interest — the ability to geolocate adversary radar systems, communications links, and GPS jammers from commercial satellites that are politically less sensitive than government intelligence assets.

52757.d: First satellite of HawkEye 360 Cluster 5. By Cluster 5, the revisit time was short enough to track vessels moving at sea — following a ship's position through multiple cluster overflights to build its track over time, even without any AIS signal.

52817.d: Egypt's newest broadcasting satellite, providing additional Ku-band capacity at 7°W and extending coverage into Sub-Saharan Africa. NileSat-301 supports the continuing explosion of Arabic satellite television channels and the transition to HD and 4K broadcasting across Egypt and the Arab world. Egypt's NileSat has become a commercial satellite operator of regional significance, providing services to governments and broadcasters across the Arab world and Africa.

52903.d: An Indian commercial communications satellite entirely leased to Tata Play (formerly Tata Sky) for direct-to-home television distribution across India. CMS-02 represents a new commercial model for ISRO — leasing complete satellite capacity to a private operator rather than managing commercial services directly. Tata Play uses it to deliver its premium HD and 4K television channels to millions of Indian subscribers.

52904.d: Malaysia's newest and highest-throughput satellite, providing Ka-band broadband alongside traditional broadcasting services. MEASAT 3D reflects Malaysia's ambition to address Southeast Asia's massive unmet broadband demand — connecting the rural populations of Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines who lack terrestrial internet access. Malaysia's satellite sector aims to become a regional broadband hub as Southeast Asian digital economies continue to accelerate.

52907.d: Lead satellite of the Yaogan-35 group 02 formation, launched 2022 as China's newer mixed-sensor reconnaissance family continued expanding at the same 35°-inclined orbital regime long used by the Yaogan-30 ELINT program. Yaogan-35 groups are assessed to combine optical, SAR, and signals intelligence capabilities within a single formation — a more integrated, multi-role approach than the single-sensor-type triplets that defined earlier Yaogan generations.

52908.d: Second satellite of the Yaogan-35 group 02 formation, part of China's evolving mixed-sensor reconnaissance architecture. As Yaogan-35 groups proliferated through 2022 and beyond, they began to look increasingly like a deliberate successor to the aging Yaogan-30 ELINT program, combining that system's naval surveillance role with additional optical or radar imaging capability in the same satellite family.

52912.d: China's third Gaofen-12 SAR satellite densifies the radar surveillance constellation. The rapid deployment pace — multiple per year — demonstrates China's industrial capacity to mass-produce capable radar reconnaissance satellites, changing the global balance of space-based surveillance capability.

52933.d: An SES C-band satellite at 135°W providing US West Coast cable distribution in the cleared upper C-band spectrum. SES-22 anchors SES's West Coast C-band distribution capability, ensuring that cable programming reaches headends across California, Oregon, Washington, and Alaska on the transition frequencies that don't conflict with 5G deployment. California alone has the largest cable television subscriber base of any US state.
52933.t: US West Coast Cable Distribution (C-band 5G Clearing)

52940.d: The Wide Field of View satellite — one of the few classified US military GEO satellites with a partially acknowledged purpose. WFOV is assessed as a space domain awareness payload, using wide-angle optical sensors to monitor other satellites in the GEO belt. As adversary anti-satellite capabilities have grown, the US Space Force has invested heavily in surveillance of the geostationary arc itself, watching for threatening maneuvers by foreign spacecraft.

53100.d: The third second-generation Tianlian satellite, working toward full global data relay coverage. Tianlian 2-03 covers the Atlantic and South American sectors, filling geographic gaps in the first-generation constellation. China's space station program is now permanently crewed, making continuous communications not optional but operationally essential — a crew medical emergency or critical system fault requires immediate contact with ground teams.

53105.d: The sequel to LARES, made from niobium alloy and orbiting in a specially chosen inclination to cancel out gravitational perturbations that complicate frame-dragging measurements. When combined with LARES data, it will test Einstein's frame-dragging prediction to within 0.2% — a remarkable precision for a gravitational experiment, and the inaugural payload of ESA's Vega-C rocket.

53109.d: GreenCube (IO-117), a 3U CubeSat built by Sapienza University of Rome's S5Lab in partnership with ENEA and the University of Naples Federico II, launched July 13, 2022 on the maiden flight of Europe's Vega-C rocket. GreenCube carries a genuinely novel experiment: a pressurized, autonomous biological laboratory growing microgreens (Brassicaceae seeds) in orbit — the farthest-ever plant cultivation experiment from Earth at launch, monitoring air composition, humidity, lighting, and plant growth via onboard cameras. Deployed into an unusually high medium-Earth orbit around 6,000 km, GreenCube also became the first amateur radio satellite ever to operate from MEO, its 70cm-band digipeater giving ham radio operators worldwide passes lasting up to 90 minutes with contact ranges spanning entire continents. Its amateur radio payload was later handed to AMSAT Italia for continued community use after the primary science mission concluded.
53109.t: Italian University CubeSat (MEO Plant Biology + Amateur Radio)

53128.d: First satellite of China's SuperView Neo-2 constellation, launched later in 2022 to further expand China Siwei's commercial imaging capacity. Each successive SuperView generation has pushed resolution and revisit performance closer to the leading edge of what's commercially available anywhere in the world — part of China's broader strategy of building sovereign commercial capability across every category of space technology rather than depending on any single foreign supplier.
53128.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

53130.d: Companion satellite to SuperView Neo-2 01, completing the second Neo-generation pair launched in 2022. With four SuperView Neo satellites now in orbit across two pairs, China Siwei's constellation achieves meaningfully faster revisit rates over any global target — a direct commercial response to the increasingly rapid revisit demands of customers in agriculture, insurance, defense, and infrastructure monitoring worldwide.
53130.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

53318.d: Part of the Yaogan-35 group 03 formation, launched 2023 to continue expanding China's mixed-sensor reconnaissance family. By this third Yaogan-35 group, the program had established a clear pattern of pairing with the roughly contemporaneous Yaogan-36 satellites, suggesting the two designations may represent complementary sensor types within a single, coordinated reconnaissance architecture.

53320.d: Part of the Yaogan-35 group 03 formation, contributing to China's expanding mixed-sensor reconnaissance capability at the 35°-inclined orbital regime. The rapid succession of Yaogan-35 groups through 2022-2024 reflects a launch cadence matching or exceeding the peak years of the older Yaogan-30 program it appears to be gradually superseding.

53355.d: The sixth and final planned SBIRS satellite, completing the global missile warning constellation. SBIRS GEO-6 brings redundancy and enhanced coverage to the system as the US faces a proliferating ballistic missile threat from multiple peer adversaries. Together the SBIRS constellation provides 24/7 coverage of every ballistic missile launch on Earth, making it impossible for any nation to conduct a surprise nuclear strike without immediate American awareness.

53445.d: A Jilin-1 Gaofen-03D satellite launched November 16, 2022 on a Ceres-1 rocket alongside four Gaofen-03D51-54 satellites — part of China's expanding roster of commercial small launch vehicles supporting the Jilin-1 constellation's rapid growth. The diversification of launch providers beyond the traditional Long March family reflects China's maturing commercial space launch ecosystem, with multiple private and state-linked companies now competing for smallsat launch contracts.
53445.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

53448.d: One of the Jilin-1 Gaofen-03D satellites contributing to the constellation's rapid late-2022 expansion phase. By this point Chang Guang was routinely launching batches of six to nine identical Gaofen-03D units per mission, a production and deployment cadence that pushed the total Jilin-1 constellation toward the 100-satellite milestone the company would achieve in mid-2023.
53448.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

53522.d: Lead satellite of the Yaogan-35 group 04 formation, launched 2023 as China continued its rapid build-out of next-generation mixed-sensor reconnaissance satellites. Four Yaogan-35 groups within roughly a year of the program's debut suggests China intends this family to reach a scale comparable to or exceeding the eleven-generation Yaogan-30 program it likely succeeds.

53524.d: Part of the Yaogan-35 group 04 formation, contributing to China's accelerating deployment of mixed-sensor reconnaissance satellites. The pace of Yaogan-35 group additions through 2023 — several formations within a single year — mirrors the aggressive early buildout tempo China applied to the original Yaogan-30 program in 2017-2018.

53526.d: Part of the Yaogan-35 group 04 formation, one of the satellites completing China's fourth mixed-sensor reconnaissance group of this newer generation. As Yaogan-35 groups multiply, they progressively add both naval ELINT-style triangulation coverage and additional imaging capability across the same low-altitude orbital band the older Yaogan-30 fleet has occupied since 2017.

53586.d: Part of a much later generation of Chuangxin satellites, CX-16A launched in 2022 into a distinctly different low-inclination orbit (29°) from the sun-synchronous orbits used by earlier Chuangxin missions — suggesting a shift toward equatorial coverage applications, possibly related to communications relay or technology testing relevant to China's expanding presence at lower latitudes. By CX-16, the program had evolved considerably from its 2003 origins as a basic data-relay technology test.

53698.d: Yaogan-33 (02), launched in 2022 as part of a modernized successor class to China's original Jianbing-5 synthetic aperture radar reconnaissance satellites. The first Yaogan-33 actually failed to reach orbit in a May 2019 launch failure; this satellite represents the program's continued replacement effort, flying in the same orbital regime as the classic JB-5 SAR satellites but with an updated design visible in released illustrations.

53760.d: Lead satellite of the Yaogan-35 group 05 formation, launched 2023 to further expand China's mixed-sensor reconnaissance architecture. Five Yaogan-35 groups within roughly eighteen months represented a launch tempo that, combined with the parallel Yaogan-36 program, gave China one of the fastest-growing reconnaissance satellite fleets of any nation in the mid-2020s.

53761.d: Part of the Yaogan-35 group 05 formation, contributing to China's continuously expanding mixed-sensor reconnaissance capability. The combined scale of Yaogan-35 and its companion Yaogan-36 program by this point exceeded fifty satellites across both families — a reconnaissance buildout rate that Western defense analysts increasingly cited as evidence of accelerating Chinese military space investment.

53763.d: Part of the Yaogan-35 group 05 formation, one of the satellites in China's fifth mixed-sensor reconnaissance group. As this newer generation matured through 2023, it increasingly appeared to be absorbing the ELINT triangulation mission of the older Yaogan-30 program while adding complementary optical and radar sensing that the earlier, more narrowly focused triplets never carried.

53765.d: Eutelsat's Very High Throughput Satellite — with over 500 Gbps of Ka-band capacity, the highest-throughput geostationary satellite ever operated in Europe. Konnect VHTS uses hundreds of spot beams across Europe and Africa to deliver residential broadband at speeds of up to 500 Mbps — faster than most European fiber connections. Launched in 2022, it represents Eutelsat's most direct response to Starlink's European expansion, offering comparable speeds to some LEO services from the reliability and coverage consistency of geostationary orbit.
53765.t: European / African Very High Throughput Broadband

53813.d: A Chinese high-throughput satellite extending China's government communications capabilities. Zhongxing-1E provides secure and priority communications capacity for Chinese government agencies, state enterprises, and infrastructure monitoring applications across China's territory and overseas Chinese interests — the digital nervous system of China's expanding global economic and political footprint.

53876.d: The companion satellite to CX-16A, launched into the same low-inclination orbit to test coordinated small-satellite operations. Flying two Chuangxin satellites together at 29° inclination — a geometry favoring equatorial and low-latitude coverage — represents a notable departure from the polar sun-synchronous orbits of the earlier Chuangxin generation, hinting at applications beyond the original program's data-relay origins.

53877.d: The third Yunhai-1 satellite, launched October 2022 to continue China's atmospheric and space environment monitoring program. Yunhai-1 03 carries an infrared Earth horizon sensor and scanning radiometer for atmospheric temperature measurement — instruments whose data feeds both civilian weather forecasting and, by most Western intelligence assessments, PLA operational weather planning. The program's genuine purpose sits in the deliberately blurred space between the two.

53943.d: Lead satellite of the first Yaogan-36 formation, launched 2022 as a companion program to the roughly contemporaneous Yaogan-35 satellite family. Analysts assess Yaogan-36 groups as providing synthetic aperture radar imaging for high-resolution military reconnaissance — complementing whatever sensor combination the parallel Yaogan-35 program carries — giving China all-weather, day-night imaging capability alongside its established naval ELINT surveillance architecture.
53943.t: Chinese Military SAR Imaging (Assessed Triplet Constellation)

53947.d: Part of the first Yaogan-36 formation, one of the satellites establishing China's newest SAR reconnaissance program. Flying as a coordinated group with Yaogan-36's sister satellites provides faster revisit time over any target than a single satellite could achieve alone — potentially imaging the same ship or military installation multiple times per orbital pass.
53947.t: Chinese Military SAR Imaging (Assessed Triplet Constellation)

53960.d: An SES C-band satellite at 101°W providing US cable distribution capacity as part of the C-band 5G clearing transition. Like SES-18 and SES-19, SES-20 was funded by FCC relocation payments to replace legacy satellites operating in the lower C-band. Together the new SES C-band satellites ensure that US cable television — which depends on C-band satellite distribution for national programming delivery — continues without disruption as 5G networks expand into formerly satellite-occupied frequencies.

53961.d: The companion to SES-20, a C-band satellite at 95°W completing SES's transition to upper C-band frequencies for US cable television distribution. The four new SES C-band satellites (SES-18, 19, 20, 21) represent a complete fleet replacement driven by regulatory policy rather than market demand — one of the most unusual satellite fleet investment programs in history, funded by wireless carriers through FCC transition payments.

54026.d: A Galaxy satellite at 121°W with C-band and Ku-band capacity serving US West Coast markets. Galaxy-33 provides the Pacific Rim's connection point to US satellite distribution, with C-band for cable headend distribution and Ku-band for enterprise and broadcast uplinks along the US West Coast. The 121°W position offers optimal coverage of California, Oregon, and Washington — the US states most dependent on Pacific communications.
54026.t: US West Coast C-band / Ku-band Distribution

54027.d: A Galaxy satellite at 99°W serving US cable television distribution. Launched alongside Galaxy-33, the pair reflects SES's fleet modernization strategy. Galaxy-34's 99°W position is central to US cable distribution geography, equidistant from both coasts and providing the most uniform signal strength across the contiguous United States — the reason 99°W has been a prime US cable satellite position since the 1980s.

54033.d: Angola's second attempt at a national communications satellite — the first, AngoSat-1, suffered a power system failure and was lost in 2018. AngoSat-2, built with Russian cooperation, provides C and Ku-band communications services across Angola and the broader African continent. Angola's persistence in pursuing satellite capability despite the AngoSat-1 failure reflects the continent's acute need for satellite connectivity — terrestrial infrastructure serving vast sub-Saharan African territories remains chronically insufficient.

54042.d: Lead satellite of the second Yaogan-36 formation, launched 2024 to expand China's SAR reconnaissance capability alongside the parallel Yaogan-35 mixed-sensor program. The rapid proliferation of both Yaogan-35 and Yaogan-36 groups within a two-year window mirrors the aggressive early buildout pace China applied to the original Yaogan-30 program in 2017-2018, suggesting a deliberate strategic priority on replacing and expanding beyond that aging constellation.
54042.t: Chinese Military SAR Imaging (Assessed Triplet Constellation)

54043.d: Part of the second Yaogan-36 formation, contributing to China's expanding SAR reconnaissance architecture. Combined, the Yaogan-35 and Yaogan-36 programs represent the newest generation of a Chinese military space surveillance strategy that has evolved from single-sensor ELINT triplets (Yaogan-9 through 31) toward integrated, multi-sensor reconnaissance groups providing naval targeting, all-weather imaging, and signals intelligence from the same coordinated satellite families.
54043.t: Chinese Military SAR Imaging (Assessed Triplet Constellation)

54045.d: Third satellite of the second Yaogan-36 SAR formation, completing the group launched in 2024. Yaogan-36 satellites are assessed to provide synthetic aperture radar imaging in coordination with the parallel Yaogan-35 mixed-sensor program, giving the PLA all-weather, day-night imaging capability layered on top of the naval ELINT surveillance mission inherited from the older Yaogan-30 constellation.
54045.t: Chinese Military SAR Imaging (Assessed Triplet Constellation)

54048.d: Eutelsat's newest Hot Bird satellite at 13°E, replacing aging Hot Bird capacity with vastly higher power and additional transponders. Hot Bird 13F delivers the next generation of European DTH broadcasting — enough power to support compact 35 cm mini-dishes, Ultra HD channels that require three times the bandwidth of HD, and interactive services that require high-capacity return channels. The 13°E position remains the most valuable single geostationary slot in Europe.

54219.d: A hosted payload on a commercial GEO satellite testing advanced technologies for the US Space Force. The Long Duration Propulsion Experiment program validates next-generation propulsion and satellite bus technologies in the operational GEO environment — reducing technical risk for future military GEO satellites by demonstrating new capabilities on commercial platforms rather than purpose-built government spacecraft.

54225.d: The second new-generation Hot Bird satellite, launching alongside Hot Bird 13F to double the capacity at Europe's most important broadcasting position. Hot Bird 13G and 13F together replace three older Eutelsat satellites with two more powerful ones — a net capacity increase of over 50% at the 13°E position while reducing the number of spacecraft operating there. The pair ensures that Hot Bird remains Europe's dominant DTH neighborhood against competition from streaming and competing satellite positions.

54230.d: A Chinese communications satellite providing C and Ku-band services across China and the Asia-Pacific. Zhongxing-19 serves the expanding Chinese commercial satellite communications market, which faces competition from global operators but benefits from government preference policies that ensure state-owned enterprises like ChinaSat maintain their position in China's domestic satellite services market.

54234.d: When NOAA-21 joined NOAA-20 in orbit, their combined data improved 48-hour temperature forecasts over the entire Northern Hemisphere — the equivalent of adding an entirely new global observing network. Its data is in every major weather model running right now. When you see a 5-day forecast, NOAA-21 helped build it.

54235.d: The first satellite of China's third-generation Yunhai program, launched in 2022 to Taiyuan Satellite Launch Center rather than Jiuquan — a departure from the earlier Yunhai-1 and Yunhai-2 launch pattern. Yunhai-3 continues the GNSS radio occultation atmospheric sensing mission of Yunhai-2 but represents a technology refresh, incorporating design improvements from four years of operational experience with the earlier constellation. As of 2024, only two Yunhai-3 satellites had reached orbit, suggesting a more measured deployment pace than the immediate six-satellite Yunhai-2 rollout.
54235.t: Chinese GNSS Radio Occultation Weather Satellite (3rd Gen)

54243.d: A Galaxy C-band satellite at 91°W serving US cable television distribution. Galaxy-31 is part of SES's fleet renewal program — replacing older C-band satellites as they reach end of life, ensuring continuity of the C-band distribution infrastructure that the US cable industry has relied upon for 40 years. Despite streaming's rise, cable operators still use satellite C-band for the 'linear' channels that carry live sports, news, and scheduled programming that audiences haven't migrated to on-demand.

54244.d: A Galaxy C-band satellite at 87°W providing cable distribution across the eastern United States. Galaxy-32 and Galaxy-31 were launched together as a pair, demonstrating SES's commitment to maintaining C-band infrastructure at multiple US orbital slots simultaneously. The eastern US position covers the densest cable markets — the Northeast megalopolis, Florida, and the Mid-Atlantic states — where cable television penetration remains highest and satellite distribution revenue is most concentrated.
54244.t: US East Coast C-band Cable Distribution

54249.d: Yaogan-34 (03), the third satellite bearing this designation, launched into the same 63.4° high-altitude orbital regime as its two solo predecessors. Unlike China's ELINT triplet programs, Yaogan-34 satellites have consistently deployed alone rather than in three-satellite formations — an unusual pattern for this orbital band that continues to draw attention from analysts assessing whether China is testing a single-satellite alternative to its established triangulation-based naval surveillance model.

54251.d: A Jilin-1 Gaofen-03D satellite launched in the November 2022 Ceres-1 batch mission, one of the earlier-numbered units in Chang Guang's largest single imaging sub-series. The Gaofen-03D program's rapid numerical growth — from single digits to over 60 satellites within a few years — illustrates the sheer velocity of China's commercial Earth observation build-out during this period.
54251.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

54252.d: Part of a five-satellite Gaofen-03D batch launched November 16, 2022 on a Ceres-1 rocket from Jiuquan. As the Gaofen-03D numbering approached and passed 50 units, the sub-series alone had become larger than most nations' entire national Earth observation programs — a single company's single satellite line exceeding what most space agencies operate in total.
54252.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

54253.d: Part of the same November 2022 five-satellite Gaofen-03D batch, contributing to the constellation's push toward the 100-satellite milestone that Chang Guang celebrated in June 2023. Each additional Gaofen-03D unit incrementally reduces the constellation's global revisit gap — the company's stated goal being coverage of any global location once every 10 minutes with the full constellation operational.
54253.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

54254.d: One of the higher-numbered Gaofen-03D satellites from the November 2022 batch launch, part of Jilin-1's rapid constellation expansion. By the time this satellite reached orbit, CGSTL had reported 117 satellites in its combined constellation as of early 2025, with 31 launched in 2024 alone — a launch cadence that few commercial satellite operators anywhere in the world could match.
54254.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

54255.d: Part of the November 2022 Gaofen-03D batch, one of five identical satellites deployed together to expand China's densifying commercial imaging network. The rapid numerical progression of the Gaofen-03D series — reaching 50+ within roughly three years of the sub-series' introduction — reflects Chang Guang's transition from a promising startup to an industrial-scale satellite manufacturer.
54255.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

54259.d: Eutelsat's high-throughput satellite at 10°E providing Ka-band broadband across Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Eutelsat-10B carries 35 Gbps of Ka-band capacity specifically targeting maritime and aeronautical broadband markets — the high-value users who need reliable, fast internet at sea and in the air. Airlines flying over Europe and the Mediterranean and ships transiting from the Atlantic to the Indian Ocean all pass through Eutelsat-10B's coverage footprint.

54690.d: A Jilin-1 Gaofen-03D satellite from a December 2022 batch launch, part of the constellation's continuous expansion through late 2022 into 2023. Chang Guang's aggressive launch cadence during this period — multiple Gaofen-03D batches within weeks of each other — reflected the company's confidence in its manufacturing pipeline and its ambition to reach the 138-satellite target (later expanded to 300) well ahead of the original 2025 schedule.
54690.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

54692.d: Part of a December 9, 2022 launch that also carried Jilin-1 Gaofen-03D44-49 and the Jilin-1 Pingtai-01A01 platform satellite — one of the densest single-mission Jilin-1 deployments to date, flying on the Jielong 3 (Smart Dragon 3) rocket. The variety of vehicles now launching Jilin-1 payloads — Long March, Ceres-1, Jielong 3 — demonstrates China's increasingly diverse commercial launch ecosystem supporting this single constellation's rapid growth.
54692.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

54694.d: One of eight Gaofen-03D satellites and one Pingtai satellite launched together December 9, 2022 aboard the Jielong 3 rocket — a batch that continued Chang Guang's relentless late-2022 constellation build-out. This mission's mixed payload, combining imaging satellites with an experimental platform satellite, reflects the increasingly sophisticated mission planning behind what began as a simple four-satellite technology demonstration in 2015.
54694.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

54695.d: Part of the December 9, 2022 Jielong 3 launch batch, one of the final Gaofen-03D satellites deployed that year as Chang Guang closed out an extraordinarily productive twelve months of constellation expansion. By year-end 2022, the combined effect of multiple large batch launches had transformed Jilin-1 from a promising commercial program into unambiguously the largest Chinese commercial satellite constellation and one of the largest in the world.
54695.t: Chinese Commercial High-Resolution Imaging (Gaofen-03D Mass Production)

54741.d: A Galaxy satellite providing C-band distribution at 95°W — the heart of the US cable television orbital arc. Galaxy-35 joins its siblings at 95°W to ensure this prime position has ample spare capacity if any individual satellite fails. The redundancy of multiple Galaxy satellites at the same position reflects the critical importance of cable television distribution — a service interruption that cuts off cable programming nationwide would be economically and reputationally catastrophic for the broadcasters and cable operators depending on it.

54742.d: A Galaxy satellite at 95°W adding capacity to America's most important cable distribution orbital position. Galaxy-36 demonstrates that despite streaming's growth, the satellite C-band cable distribution business remains robust enough to justify new satellite investment. Sports rights fees — which support live sports as cable television's most irreplaceable content — ensure that C-band distribution infrastructure remains economically viable as long as live sports drive cable subscriptions.

54743.d: Europe's generational leap in geostationary meteorology. MTG-I1's Lightning Imager is the first ever on a European weather satellite, detecting every lightning flash across Europe, Africa, and the Atlantic simultaneously. Meteorologists say it extends severe weather warning lead times by 30 minutes — 30 minutes that can mean the difference between shelter and exposure in a violent storm.

54746.d: Lead satellite of the fourth Yaogan-36 imaging triplet, launched late 2022. The Yaogan-36 series represents a distinct capability from the ELINT-focused Yaogan-30 series — analysts assess Yaogan-36 as a synthetic aperture radar imaging constellation for high-resolution military reconnaissance, providing all-weather, day-night imagery of ground and maritime targets. Flying as a triplet provides three-fold faster revisit time over any target compared to a single satellite — potentially imaging the same ship or military installation three times per orbital pass. The rapid proliferation of Yaogan-36 groups (four triplets in quick succession) mirrors the constellation-building pace of the Yaogan-30 ELINT series and reflects the PLA's urgency in achieving comprehensive, persistent space-based military situational awareness.
54746.t: Chinese Military SAR Imaging (Assessed Triplet Constellation)

54748.d: Second satellite of the fourth Yaogan-36 SAR formation, one of the newer groups in China's rapidly expanding radar reconnaissance program. By this fourth generation, Yaogan-36 had established a launch cadence matching the aggressive early years of the older Yaogan-30 ELINT program, suggesting a deliberate strategic priority on building out this newer, more capable successor constellation.
54748.t: Chinese Military SAR Imaging (Assessed Triplet Constellation)

54750.d: Third satellite completing the fourth Yaogan-36 SAR formation. Combined with the parallel Yaogan-35 mixed-sensor program, the two satellite families together represent the most capable and rapidly growing Chinese reconnaissance architecture of the mid-2020s, blending radar imaging, optical sensing, and signals intelligence across coordinated satellite groups.
54750.t: Chinese Military SAR Imaging (Assessed Triplet Constellation)

54754.d: The Surface Water and Ocean Topography satellite maps 120 km wide swaths of every lake, river, and ocean simultaneously — the first time scientists can observe how water moves across the entire surface of Earth. For the first time, flood forecasting models can see where the water is going, not just where it has been. A fundamental change in how we manage water for billions of people.

54755.d: SES mPOWER-A F1, the first satellite of SES's next-generation O3B mPOWER medium-Earth-orbit broadband constellation, launched December 2022. Where the original O3B satellites proved that MEO broadband could rival fiber-like latency, mPOWER dramatically scales up the concept — each satellite carries thousands of fully digital, individually steerable beams that can be reshaped and redirected from the ground in real time, letting SES redirect bandwidth to wherever demand spikes: a cruise ship, a disaster zone, a temporary military deployment. It represents SES's answer to the competitive pressure from Starlink and other LEO broadband constellations.
54755.t: Medium Earth Orbit Broadband (Next-Gen O3B mPOWER)

55049.d: One of ICEYE's mid-generation SAR satellites, part of the constellation's steady 2022-era expansion as the company pushed past 20 satellites in orbit. By this point ICEYE offered dwell mode imaging — staring at a fixed location for up to 25 seconds to achieve extremely fine azimuth resolution with significantly reduced speckle noise, a capability valuable for detailed structural monitoring of individual buildings, ships, or infrastructure.

55131.d: China's Shijian-23 technology demonstration satellite, continuing the series of large GEO experiments that test advanced propulsion, communications, and satellite bus technologies. The SJ series has become China's proving ground for next-generation GEO capabilities — the satellites that demonstrate technologies eventually incorporated into China's operational military and commercial space platforms.

55239.d: APStar's newest commercial satellite, extending the operator's Ka-band coverage for maritime and aviation connectivity. APStar-6E reflects the growing demand for high-speed satellite internet from Chinese commercial shipping and aviation industries. China's Belt and Maritime Silk Road shipping interests across the Indian Ocean, Pacific, and beyond create specific demand for reliable connectivity along routes where coverage from Western operators may be limited.

55244.d: A Chinese military satellite from early 2023, part of the continuously expanding Yaogan reconnaissance fleet that now encompasses optical, synthetic aperture radar, electronic intelligence, and video imaging variants. Yaogan-37 carries the broader strategic significance of the complete Yaogan program: a comprehensive, multi-sensor space-based intelligence system that gives the PLA near-continuous awareness of adversary military activities across the Indo-Pacific. Individual satellite designations in the Yaogan series are often deliberately opaque — it is not always clear from the name alone whether a given Yaogan satellite is optical, SAR, or ELINT — part of China's deliberate policy of maintaining ambiguity about specific military satellite capabilities.

55263.d: A classified US government satellite in geostationary orbit launched in 2023. Orbital tracking reveals its GEO position but not its mission, which remains officially unacknowledged. The growing pace of classified US GEO satellite launches reflects the increasing importance of the geostationary orbital regime as a domain of strategic competition between spacefaring nations.

55264.d: Another Long Duration Propulsion Experiment satellite, continuing the US Space Force's program of testing advanced technologies hosted on commercial GEO platforms. This approach dramatically reduces the cost and schedule of technology validation — a commercial satellite carries experimental military payloads as secondary passengers, providing real operational environment testing at a fraction of dedicated satellite costs.

55324.d: The second satellite of Cluster 6. The Ukraine conflict demonstrated the military value of space-based RF geolocation — locating radars, jammers, and communication networks from orbit without risking aircraft or personnel — accelerating government demand for commercial RF intelligence products.

55326.d: The third satellite of Cluster 6, representing the first commercial system capable of near-global, near-continuous radio frequency monitoring from orbit — a capability that has opened new markets in maritime security, spectrum management, and signals intelligence.

55327.d: First satellite of HawkEye 360 Cluster 6. As the constellation approaches full deployment, global revisit times are measured in hours rather than days — transforming from an occasional snapshot of radio emissions to a near-continuous monitoring system for global radio frequency activity.

55506.d: Russia's fourth Elektro-L geostationary weather satellite, expanding coverage and redundancy for Russian meteorological services. Positioned at a different longitude from its siblings, Elektro-L 4 gives Russian meteorologists simultaneous multi-angle coverage of the same weather systems — improving forecast accuracy for a country where weather extremes from Arctic blizzards to steppe heat waves affect hundreds of millions of people across the world's largest national territory.

55508.d: Hispasat's next-generation Americas satellite, featuring a fully flexible high-throughput payload that can allocate capacity dynamically across coverage zones. Amazonas Nexus supports the continued expansion of Brazilian satellite broadband — a market that has grown dramatically as the pandemic forced remote work and education into rural areas previously served only by voice satellite. Its flexible payload adjusts coverage and power allocation in real time to match demand patterns that shift seasonally and daily.

55686.d: A Chinese high-throughput communications satellite providing Ka-band broadband across China. Zhongxing-26 addresses the rapidly growing demand for satellite broadband in China's underserved western and rural regions. China's government has invested heavily in satellite internet connectivity as part of its digital infrastructure programs, and Zhongxing-26 is part of that national connectivity strategy.

55836.d: Lead satellite of a sixth-generation Tianhui pair, launched in 2023 as China's mapping satellite program continued its steady generational cadence. As successive Tianhui generations proliferate, the PLA has built one of the most comprehensive and continuously updated topographic intelligence databases of any military in the world — an asset whose value only compounds as more data accumulates over more of the Earth's surface, refreshed more frequently, in more weather conditions.
55836.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

55839.d: Companion satellite to Tianhui 6A, completing the sixth Tianhui formation-flying pair. The relentless cadence of new Tianhui generations — six major iterations in roughly a decade — demonstrates a level of sustained institutional commitment that few nations apply to military topographic mapping, a capability area that rarely generates headlines but underpins nearly every other military operation from missile guidance to logistics planning.
55839.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

55841.d: Russia's most controversial satellite — and the most closely watched object in the geostationary belt. Luch-5X, also known as Olymp-K 2, has made a series of unexplained close approaches to commercial and government satellites operated by other nations, maneuvering to within a few kilometers of targets including Intelsat satellites, European military spacecraft, and US government satellites. Russia has not explained the purpose of these maneuvers. Independent analysts assess Olymp-K 2 as a signals intelligence or electronic warfare satellite testing proximity operations capabilities against foreign spacecraft — the most concerning behavior exhibited by any satellite in GEO.

55912.d: The second Gaofen-13 geostationary optical imaging satellite, doubling China's persistent surveillance capability from GEO. Together with Gaofen-4 and Gaofen-13 01, China has built a small but growing constellation of geostationary optical satellites that can maintain continuous visual observation of strategic regions — a capability no other nation possesses at operational scale. China's ability to persistently monitor the Taiwan Strait, South China Sea, and Indian border regions from GEO provides a persistent intelligence advantage.

55970.d: An SES C-band satellite at 138°W serving the US cable distribution market as part of the C-band clearing program. The US FCC required satellite operators to clear the lower C-band frequencies for 5G wireless use, requiring new satellites that operate only in the upper C-band. SES-18 was specifically built for this C-band clearing transition, ensuring that US cable television distribution could continue serving the cable industry while the lower frequencies were repurposed for 5G networks.

55971.d: An SES satellite at 103°W built for the US C-band spectrum clearing program. SES-19 and SES-18 were designed and launched specifically so that SES could vacate the lower C-band frequencies used by its existing satellites, freeing that spectrum for 5G wireless. The US government paid SES and Intelsat billions of dollars in accelerated relocation payments — effectively funding new satellites so that the 5G wireless industry could access spectrum previously occupied by satellite television.

55973.d: One of China's Tianmu commercial weather satellites using GPS radio occultation to probe the atmosphere. Each GPS signal bent through the atmosphere becomes a precise temperature and humidity profile — a cosmic thermometer with no cloud cover limitation. The constellation's goal: atmospheric profiles every 90 minutes globally, ten times more frequent than any existing system.

55974.d: Tianmu #4, building China's commercial GPS occultation weather network. The constellation already outnumbers all government-operated radio occultation satellites combined, reshaping who controls global weather data. A commercial entity quietly displacing government monopolies on atmospheric intelligence.

55975.d: Tianmu #5 continues China's commercial weather data revolution. Radio occultation profiles each GPS bending through the atmosphere from surface to 60 km, through any clouds, in complete darkness. The most all-weather atmospheric measurement technique ever invented — and China is deploying it at commercial scale.

55976.d: Tianmu #6 demonstrates China's pivot from state-operated space infrastructure toward a commercial data economy. Tianmu data is sold to national meteorological services globally — including countries without their own weather satellites — giving China influence over forecasting infrastructure in dozens of nations.

56174.d: Intelsat's most advanced Epic high-throughput satellite yet, covering the Americas and Atlantic with both Ka-band high-throughput spot beams and a hosted Space Force payload. IS-40E represents Intelsat's convergence of commercial broadband and government communications — the spacecraft simultaneously provides commercial Ka-band internet services and hosts a classified US government payload, a revenue model that helps offset satellite costs. The Epic Ka capacity serves commercial maritime, aeronautical, and enterprise broadband across the Americas.
56174.t: Americas Epic High-Throughput + Hosted Government Payload

56178.d: İMECE, Turkey's first fully domestically developed high-resolution Earth observation satellite, launched April 15, 2023 on a SpaceX Falcon 9 as part of the Transporter-7 rideshare mission. Built entirely in-house by TÜBİTAK Space Technologies Research Institute — including its electro-optical camera, flight computer, star trackers, reaction wheels, and electric propulsion system — İMECE delivers sub-meter panchromatic imagery from a 680 km sun-synchronous orbit, imaging a swath 16.7 km wide and up to 1,000 km long in a single pass at 320 Mbps downlink speed. President Erdoğan called it proof Turkey could design, build, and test an advanced imaging satellite without foreign dependency; the country has since begun work on IMECE-2 and IMECE-3, a next-generation cluster planned to work together in orbit from 2027.
56178.t: Turkish Domestic High-Resolution Earth Observation Satellite

56191.d: First satellite of HawkEye 360 Cluster 7. The seventh cluster incorporates upgraded receivers capable of characterizing signals in greater detail — moving from simply locating emitters to identifying them by their technical characteristics, a jump from 'there's a signal here' to 'this is a specific radar type.'

56193.d: Second satellite of Cluster 7. By this stage HawkEye 360 had signed partnerships with multiple national coast guards and maritime authorities — providing commercial satellite RF geolocation as an operational service rather than an occasional data product. Commercial space intelligence is becoming routine infrastructure.

56200.d: The third satellite of Cluster 7, completing the latest addition to HawkEye 360's constellation. From three Pathfinder satellites in 2018 to 21+ operational spacecraft by 2023 — HawkEye 360 demonstrated the commercial viability of space-based RF geolocation and established itself as the global leader in a brand-new commercial intelligence category.

56232.d: China's first precipitation-focused polar satellite carries a dual-frequency precipitation radar — the first such instrument on any Chinese spacecraft. Combined with a microwave imager, it measures rainfall rates and raindrop size distribution globally, transforming China's typhoon rainfall tracking and its contribution to international precipitation monitoring.

56370.d: The first of Viasat's third-generation constellation — designed to deliver 1 Tbps of capacity over the Americas, 10 times ViaSat-2's throughput. After launch in 2023, a reflector deployment anomaly prevented ViaSat-3 Americas from achieving its intended capacity, providing only a fraction of planned throughput. The partial failure was one of the costliest satellite anomalies in commercial history — a humbling reminder that even proven operators can face crippling technical failures at the frontier of satellite technology.

56371.d: Northrop Grumman's second mission extension vehicle in geostationary orbit, operating as the latest commercial satellite servicing spacecraft. Following the success of MEV-1 and MEV-2, Arcturus represents the next generation of satellite life extension — potentially capable of serving multiple clients sequentially. The emergence of a commercial satellite servicing industry is transforming GEO economics: operators can now plan for satellite life extension rather than replacement, significantly reducing the cost per year of GEO communications service.

56372.d: A commercial geostationary satellite providing communications services. GS-1 is part of the continuing diversification of GEO operators beyond the traditional large commercial satellite companies — smaller, specialized operators increasingly finding niches in the GEO arc serving specific geographic markets, frequency bands, or customer types that larger constellations don't address efficiently.

56564.d: A newer BeiDou-3 geostationary satellite providing additional capacity and redundancy for China's global navigation system. As BeiDou has gained international adoption — now used across much of Asia, Africa, and along the Belt and Road — the GEO component's Short Message Service has found new applications in maritime safety, disaster response, and government communications across dozens of nations.

56757.d: Arabsat's newest satellite, positioned at 26°E — the 'Hot Bird of the Arab world' — where more than 500 channels are broadcast to over 100 million viewers. Arabsat-7B provides the next-generation capacity to support 4K and future 8K broadcasting as Arab audiences upgrade to higher-definition programming. The 26°E orbital slot has become the most-watched satellite position in the Arab world, rivaling Europe's Astra 19.2°E as a content concentration point.

56759.d: The first second-generation NavIC satellite, incorporating significantly upgraded atomic clocks and a new L1 signal compatible with GPS and Galileo. NVS-01 enables dual-frequency reception — comparing L1 and L5 signals to cancel ionospheric errors that limit single-frequency GPS accuracy to 3-5 meters. With dual-frequency NavIC, India's navigation system achieves sub-meter accuracy autonomously, without augmentation from ground networks.

56963.d: Part of ICEYE's continued constellation growth through 2023, as the company's satellite count climbed toward the 30s and 40s ahead of its dramatic 2025 acceleration. ICEYE-X25 contributed to the company's core value proposition: persistent, weather-independent monitoring for customers who cannot afford to wait for cloud cover to clear — insurers assessing flood damage, militaries tracking troop movements, and governments monitoring their coastlines around the clock.

57045.d: Indonesia's largest and most powerful communications satellite — a national broadband project providing Ka-band internet to over 150,000 public service points across Indonesia's remote islands. SATRIA (Satellite Indonesia Raya) specifically targets schools, health clinics, government offices, and military outposts in areas unreachable by fiber or 4G — the digitally excluded population of the world's fourth most populous nation. Indonesia's government invested in SATRIA as infrastructure equivalent to building roads through its island geography.

57166.d: Russia's third Meteor-M No.2 expands Arctic polar weather coverage. Each new Meteor-M improves Russia's capacity to track sea ice conditions along the Northern Sea Route — which Russia is developing as a commercially viable alternative to the Suez Canal for European-Asian shipping as Arctic ice retreats.

57213.d: Germany's experimental communications satellite named after the physicist who first proved the existence of electromagnetic waves. Heinrich Hertz carries experimental payloads testing Q/V band communications (frequencies above 40 GHz) and optical laser links — technologies needed for future next-generation satellite communications. Germany's DLR and several commercial partners use it to validate technologies that will appear in operational satellites a decade from now, filling the gap between laboratory and full-scale mission that technology demonstration satellites uniquely address.

57214.d: The companion to Syracuse-4A, completing France's next-generation military communications pair. Together they provide France with the most capable European-independent military satellite communications system, supporting the full range of French defense activities from nuclear deterrence to special operations. France and Germany are the only European nations operating fully independent strategic military satellite constellations.
57214.a: French Ministry of Defence / Thales Alenia

57391.d: Spire Global's LEMUR-2 DEVERILL-M-T is a 3U CubeSat in the LEMUR-2 constellation simultaneously tracking weather, ships, and aircraft from a single compact spacecraft. The '-M-T' suffix indicates a modified variant with enhanced radio frequency capabilities beyond the baseline LEMUR-2 design. Spire names its satellites with anarchic creativity that scales with volume — when you operate hundreds of satellites, you need naming that doesn't run out. DEVERILL-M-T's GPS radio occultation soundings profile the atmosphere from surface to stratosphere, feeding weather forecast models that improve accuracy over oceans where no ground instruments exist and where most of Earth's major storm systems are born.

57399.d: Tianmu #7 enters a new orbital plane, increasing the constellation's global revisit frequency. The target: atmospheric profiles every 90 minutes — making weather data as current as a live traffic feed. When achieved, it would be the most frequent global atmospheric measurement in human history.

57400.d: Tianmu #8, quietly becoming one of the most significant new inputs to global weather prediction this decade — rivaling multi-billion-dollar traditional weather satellites at a fraction of the cost. The economics of atmospheric sounding changed permanently.

57401.d: Tianmu #9 reflects China's extraordinary commercial space acceleration. Five years ago, commercial weather satellites were purely American and European. The Tianmu constellation's rapid deployment shows how quickly China's private space companies built capability once they gained government and venture capital backing.

57402.d: Tianmu #10. Radio occultation has become the single most valuable new data source for weather forecasting this decade — improving short-range forecasts more than any other advance since geostationary weather satellites appeared in the 1970s.

57479.d: The most powerful commercial communications satellite ever built when launched in 2023 — a 9-metric-ton behemoth with 500 Gbps of total capacity, more than double the throughput of the next most capable commercial satellite at the time. Jupiter 3 provides internet access to rural North and South America through Hughes Network Systems' HughesNet service, using hundreds of Ka-band spot beams to concentrate power where customers are. Its 18 kW solar arrays generate more power than any previous commercial satellite. Jupiter 3 shifts broadband from a subscription service to a commodity for millions of unserved rural Americans, Canadians, and Latin Americans.
