57490.d: FY-3F complements China's polar weather fleet in the afternoon orbital slot, building toward a full Fengyun-3 constellation independent of US or European data. Upgraded ozone monitoring and stratospheric sounding extend China's atmospheric science capabilities across the full vertical atmosphere.

57493.d: SES's latest Galaxy satellite, continuing the fleet that has distributed American television for 40 years. Galaxy-37 carries both C-band for traditional cable distribution and Ka-band for broadband — reflecting the cable industry's own evolution from pure content delivery to integrated communications services. As cable operators bundle internet and television, Galaxy satellites must serve both roles simultaneously.

57624.d: A Chinese land detection satellite in geostationary orbit, officially designed for land and geological monitoring. From GEO, a land detection satellite can continuously observe agricultural conditions, desertification trends, and natural disaster development across a fixed geographic region — capabilities useful for both environmental monitoring and persistent agricultural intelligence that drives food security policy.

57630.d: HEAD-3E, part of the HEAD Aerospace commercial small satellite constellation operated out of Hong Kong and mainland China, combining Earth imaging with AIS ship-tracking receivers in a single compact spacecraft. HEAD's satellites serve customers needing both visual confirmation of maritime activity and the underlying vessel transponder data — a combination increasingly valuable for maritime insurance, illegal fishing detection, and sanctions enforcement monitoring.

57654.d: The fourth Gaofen-12 SAR satellite. China's proliferating radar constellation has eliminated single-satellite vulnerabilities and established persistent all-weather coverage of strategic global targets. Space-based radar intelligence, once exclusive to the United States, is now a Chinese operational reality.

57696.d: The first satellite of Chang Guang's next-generation Kuanfu-02 wide-swath series, launched August 25, 2023 on a Ceres-1 rocket. Kuanfu-02A represents a dramatic mass reduction from the original 1,200 kg Kuanfu-01 generation down to just 230 kg, while simultaneously improving resolution to 0.5 meters across a 150 km swath — smaller, cheaper, and sharper all at once, the same iterative improvement pattern that has characterized every generation of the Jilin-1 constellation since 2015.

57756.d: One of the September 2023 batch of SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer satellites, contributing to a demonstration constellation that would go on to prove laser intersatellite communication, ground data relay, and — significantly — direct connection between a commercial partner satellite and an SDA ground terminal aboard an aircraft in flight. WILDFIRE 4's operational data helped validate the architecture before the much larger Tranche 1 (154 satellites) began deployment in 2025.
57756.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57757.d: BB4, part of the US Space Development Agency's Tranche 0 demonstration constellation launched September 2023 — the same 27-satellite proof-of-concept deployment that established the Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture's core design: dozens of small, cheap, mesh-networked satellites in low Earth orbit rather than a handful of large, expensive, easily-targeted ones. Tranche 0 validated laser inter-satellite communication and rapid data relay concepts that now underpin the much larger Tranche 1, 2, and 3 constellations following it.

57758.d: Part of the SDA's Tranche 0 Tracking Layer constellation, WILDFIRE 3 carries the wide-field-of-view infrared sensor that scans large swaths of Earth for the characteristic heat signature of a missile launch. The Tranche 0 tracking satellites work alongside the parallel Transport Layer — the SDA's low-latency laser-linked communications mesh — to demonstrate an integrated system where missile detection data can be relayed to combatant commanders and interceptor systems within seconds rather than minutes.
57758.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57761.d: Part of the SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer constellation's eight-satellite deployment, contributing infrared missile detection coverage as part of the Pentagon's broader pivot toward proliferated, resilient space architectures. The Tranche 0 tracking satellites tested the fire-control quality tracking data generation that will eventually support the Golden Dome missile defense concept under study by the Space Force.
57761.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57763.d: One of the SDA's Tranche 0 Tracking Layer satellites, deployed as part of the second and larger of two 2023 launches that established the demonstration constellation's initial capability. WILDFIRE 6 and its seven siblings validated wide-field-of-view infrared missile detection from low Earth orbit — a capability previously demonstrated only from geostationary altitude by systems like SBIRS, now proven viable from a dramatically cheaper and more numerous constellation architecture.
57763.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57764.d: One of eight Tracking Layer satellites in the US Space Development Agency's Tranche 0 constellation, launched September 2, 2023 on a Falcon 9 from Vandenberg. Despite the name's coincidental resemblance to wildfire-monitoring satellites, WILDFIRE 1 is a military missile-warning spacecraft — part of the SDA's proliferated architecture demonstrating that hundreds of small, cheap, resilient satellites in low orbit can perform the same infrared missile detection mission that traditionally required a handful of large, expensive, vulnerable geostationary satellites like SBIRS. WILDFIRE 1 carries a wide-field-of-view infrared sensor designed to detect the heat signature of missile launches, including the harder-to-track hypersonic glide vehicles that traditional missile warning satellites struggle to follow.
57764.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57765.d: Part of the SDA's eight-satellite Tranche 0 Tracking Layer, deployed to validate the concept of distributed, resilient missile warning from low Earth orbit ahead of full operational deployment. As of December 2025, the SDA announced $3.5 billion in Tranche 3 Tracking Layer contracts for 72 additional satellites — a scale increase that traces its origins directly back to demonstration missions like WILDFIRE 9.
57765.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57766.d: The final satellite of the September 2023 SDA Tracking Layer batch, completing the eight-satellite demonstration group. Together with the four L3Harris-built HBTSS satellites launched separately by the Missile Defense Agency, the WILDFIRE constellation formed the complete Tranche 0 Tracking Layer — a proof of concept for hybrid missile warning, tracking, and defense architecture that the SDA now describes as central to US homeland and theater missile defense strategy going forward.
57766.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57767.d: Second of the SDA's WILDFIRE-designated Tracking Layer satellites, part of Tranche 0's demonstration that low-Earth-orbit missile tracking is both feasible and dramatically more resilient than the traditional geostationary approach. A constellation of dozens of small tracking satellites is far harder for an adversary to disable with anti-satellite weapons than a handful of large, expensive, easily-targeted satellites — the core strategic logic behind the SDA's entire proliferated architecture.
57767.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57768.d: One of the September 2023 Tranche 0 Tracking Layer satellites, part of the demonstration constellation that proved out low-latency laser communication between satellites, ground stations, and — in later testing — an SDA terminal installed on an aircraft in flight. WILDFIRE 8's operational lessons directly informed the design of the much larger Tranche 1 and Tranche 2 Tracking Layer constellations now under construction.
57768.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57769.d: Part of the SDA's proliferated missile warning demonstration constellation, launched alongside its WILDFIRE siblings in September 2023. The complete Tranche 0 constellation reached 27 satellites total — 19 optically-linked data transport spacecraft and 8 wide-field-of-view tracking satellites like this one — establishing the baseline architecture the SDA expects to scale into a constellation of over 1,000 satellites by 2026.
57769.t: Military Missile Tracking (SDA Tranche 0 Tracking Layer)

57797.d: Yaogan-33 (03), continuing China's modernized SAR reconnaissance satellite line that traces back to the original Jianbing-5 all-weather radar imaging satellites. Each successive Yaogan-33 launch adds capacity to a program specifically valued for penetrating the near-constant cloud cover over China's southern provinces — Tibet, Sichuan, Yunnan, Guangxi, and Hainan — where optical satellites are frequently blind.

57830.d: Lead satellite of the first Yaogan-40 formation, launched into an 86° near-polar orbit — a departure from the 35°- and 63.4°-inclined regimes used by the earlier Yaogan-25, 30, and 31 ELINT families. The shift to polar orbit gives Yaogan-40 truly global rather than regionally-focused coverage, reflecting the PLA Navy's expanding operational reach beyond the Western Pacific into the Indian Ocean and potentially further afield.
57830.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (Polar Orbit, Next-Gen)

57832.d: Second satellite of the first Yaogan-40 formation, part of China's newest-generation polar-orbiting naval ELINT surveillance system. Yaogan-40's near-polar inclination lets it pass over every ocean on Earth within hours, a significant capability upgrade from the geographically limited Pacific-focused coverage of the earlier Yaogan-25 and Yaogan-30 triplet families.
57832.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (Polar Orbit, Next-Gen)

57833.d: The third member of China's Yaogan-40 01 ELINT triplet, a newer-generation naval surveillance satellite launched in 2023. The Yaogan-40 series appears to continue the Yaogan-30 ELINT mission but in a polar orbit providing global rather than regional coverage — an upgrade that reflects China's expanding naval operations beyond the western Pacific. As the PLA Navy deploys carrier strike groups into the Indian Ocean, the South Atlantic, and potentially further afield, the need for a global ocean surveillance satellite constellation rather than a regionally focused one becomes strategically necessary. Yaogan-40's polar orbit provides coverage of every ocean on Earth within hours.

57958.d: Yaogan-33 (04), the newest satellite in China's modernized Jianbing-5 successor SAR class as of its launch. The program's radar imaging capability was originally funded entirely by the PLA specifically to see through the weather that blinds optical reconnaissance over China's cloud-prone southern border regions — terrain where visual intelligence gathering has always been unreliable.

58073.d: The fourth Yunhai-1 satellite, launched October 2023 as China's 47th orbital mission of that year — a marker of just how routine satellite launches had become for China by the mid-2020s. Yunhai-1 04 continues the series' atmospheric, marine, and space environment sensing mission from its predecessors, part of a Chinese satellite weather program that Western analysts continue to assess carries significant undisclosed military utility alongside its stated civilian purposes.

58199.d: Lead satellite of a fifth-generation Tianhui pair, launched in 2023 to continue the PLA's evolving stereo mapping and terrain intelligence program. Each successive Tianhui generation has incorporated design lessons from its predecessors — the program's decade-plus continuous development reflects sustained PLA investment in maintaining an independent, sovereign topographic mapping capability that doesn't rely on any foreign or commercial elevation data source for strategically sensitive terrain.
58199.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

58201.d: Companion satellite to Tianhui 5A, forming the latest generation formation-flying mapping pair in China's continuously evolving Tianhui program. Nearly two decades after the first Tianhui satellite reached orbit in 2010, the program has matured from single-satellite optical stereo imaging into sophisticated multi-satellite radar interferometry — a trajectory that mirrors the broader arc of Chinese military space capability development across almost every satellite category.
58201.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

58204.d: The tenth TJS satellite, representing the continued and accelerating pace of China's classified GEO military program. With more than 20 TJS satellites now in orbit, the series has grown from experimental designations to what analysts consider one of the world's largest classified military geostationary constellations. China's continued investment in TJS capacity reflects the PLA Strategic Support Force's prioritization of space-based military capabilities.

58253.d: One of China's newest commercial C-band satellites, extending its coverage into new markets. Zhongxing-6E reflects China's continued investment in C-band commercial capacity at a time when many Western operators are transitioning toward Ka-band high-throughput systems — recognizing that C-band remains essential for reliability in tropical markets and for professional video distribution applications that favor its characteristics.

58271.d: The 16th SuperDove in Planet Labs' Flock 4Q batch, launched November 11, 2023 on SpaceX's Transporter-9 rideshare mission alongside the Pelican-1 high-resolution technology demonstrator. Flock 4Q 16 operates the PSB.SD 8-band sensor at 3-meter resolution — one of 36 identical SuperDoves from this single launch. The simultaneous deployment of 36 imaging satellites in one rocket shot is something that would have seemed impossible to satellite engineers a decade ago, yet it's now routine for Planet. Each Transporter rideshare adds another layer of coverage and redundancy to the constellation that collectively generates more Earth imagery per day than all other commercial imaging operators combined.

58273.d: Part of Planet Labs' 36-satellite Flock 4Q batch from the November 2023 Transporter-9 mission, contributing to the company's daily-revisit Earth imaging constellation. Each SuperDove in this batch carries an identical 47-megapixel, 8-band sensor — the standardized design that allows Planet to operate hundreds of satellites as a single coherent imaging system rather than a collection of individually managed spacecraft.

58280.d: Part of Planet Labs' Flock 4Q batch, one of the final satellites in the 36-spacecraft deployment from Transporter-9. By late 2023, Planet's SuperDove constellation had matured into a routine, production-phase operation — each new Flock replenishing aging satellites and incrementally expanding capacity rather than representing a novel technological leap, the hallmark of a genuinely operational, mature satellite constellation.

58282.d: One of the higher-numbered SuperDoves in Planet Labs' Flock 4Q batch, part of the 36-satellite deployment that added meaningful capacity to Planet's daily global monitoring constellation. Flock 4Q-27's imagery feeds Planet's agricultural, environmental, and defense-adjacent commercial customers, part of a data pipeline generating terabytes of Earth imagery every single day.

58288.d: One of ICEYE's 2023-era SAR satellites, contributing to a constellation that by year-end 2023 had grown to roughly 27 spacecraft. ICEYE-X31's imagery serves customers ranging from insurance companies quantifying storm damage to defense agencies monitoring contested regions — all benefiting from SAR's fundamental advantage: it doesn't care whether it's cloudy, stormy, or the middle of the night.

58294.d: Part of ICEYE's expanding constellation in the run-up to the company's 2025 growth surge, which would eventually add 22 satellites in a single year. ICEYE-X34's contribution to the fleet helped establish the operational cadence and reliability record that gave investors confidence to fund the company's subsequent rapid scale-up, including dedicated national security missions for Germany, Poland, Greece, and Ukraine.

58302.d: One of ICEYE's later 2023 SAR satellites, contributing to a constellation growing toward the scale that would soon make it the world's largest commercial SAR operator. By this point ICEYE had launched over 30 satellites since 2018, offering resolutions as fine as 25 cm and scene sizes up to 120,000 km² — a dramatic leap from the 10-meter resolution of the company's original 2018 proof-of-concept mission.

58318.d: One of 36 SuperDoves in Planet Labs' Flock 4Q batch, launched November 11, 2023 on SpaceX's Transporter-9 rideshare mission alongside the Pelican-1 high-resolution technology demonstrator. Flock 4Q-7 carries Planet's 8-band PSB.SD imaging sensor at 3-meter resolution — one node among 200+ active SuperDoves that together image nearly all of Earth's landmass every single day.

58320.d: One of 36 SuperDoves in Planet Labs' Flock 4Q batch, launched November 11, 2023 on SpaceX's Transporter-9 rideshare alongside the Pelican-1 technology demonstrator. Flock 4Q-2 carries the 8-band PSB.SD sensor at 3-meter resolution, one node in Planet's daily-revisit global imaging constellation.

58322.d: One of the Flock 4Q SuperDoves launched on Transporter-9, contributing to Planet's constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global coverage. Flock 4Q-9's imagery is indistinguishable in capability from its siblings — the value of the Planet constellation lies not in any individual satellite but in the aggregate daily-revisit coverage the full fleet provides together.

58327.d: Part of Planet Labs' 36-satellite Flock 4Q deployment from November 2023, one of dozens of identically equipped SuperDoves that together image nearly all of Earth's landmass every single day. Flock 4Q-4 feeds the same continuously updated global dataset used across agriculture, forestry, insurance, and defense-adjacent monitoring applications.

58328.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4Q batch, one of the SuperDoves that expanded Planet's constellation capacity in late 2023. By this stage in Planet's operational maturity, launching three dozen identical satellites in a single rideshare mission had become routine — a stark contrast to the company's earliest days, when a single Dove satellite represented a major engineering milestone.

58425.d: First of a three-satellite Chuangxin-20 formation launched together in 2023, part of China's continuing evolution of its small-satellite technology program. Flying at a distinctive 50° inclination shared by all three CX-20 satellites, this formation tests coordinated multi-spacecraft operations — a technical building block for future distributed sensing or communications relay missions that require several satellites working in concert rather than a single monolithic spacecraft.

58426.d: Second satellite of the Chuangxin-20 three-satellite formation, flying alongside CX-20A and CX-20C in the same 50° inclined orbit. Multi-satellite formations like this one allow China to test techniques — inter-satellite ranging, coordinated maneuvering, distributed sensing — that a single spacecraft physically cannot demonstrate, building toward future operational constellations that depend on multiple satellites acting as one coordinated system.

58427.d: The third satellite completing the Chuangxin-20 formation-flying trio, launched together with CX-20A and CX-20B in 2023. Three-satellite formations provide a minimum viable testbed for triangulation-based techniques — the same basic principle behind China's Yaogan naval surveillance triplets, but here applied in an experimental technology-proving context rather than an operational military mission.

58505.d: A Chuangxin-series satellite launched in 2023 into a distinctive 86.5° polar orbit, continuing the Chinese Academy of Sciences' decades-long small-satellite technology demonstration program that began with the original CX-1 store-and-forward mission in 2003. Two decades and nineteen numbered missions later, the Chuangxin program remains China's quiet proving ground for satellite technologies that eventually filter into much larger operational constellations.

58557.d: Lead satellite of the fifth Yaogan-39 triplet, part of a newer Chinese reconnaissance family operating in the same 35°-inclined orbital regime long used by the Yaogan-30 naval ELINT program. As with the parallel Yaogan-35 and Yaogan-36 families, Yaogan-39 groups are assessed to extend China's naval surveillance and reconnaissance architecture with updated sensor technology beyond what the aging Yaogan-30 constellation was originally designed to provide.

58558.d: Second satellite of the fifth Yaogan-39 triplet, continuing China's rapid expansion of next-generation reconnaissance satellite families sharing the 35°-inclined low Earth orbit regime. The proliferation of parallel Yaogan-35, 36, and 39 programs within a few years of each other reflects an unusually broad and well-resourced simultaneous investment across multiple successor architectures to the original Yaogan-30 ELINT constellation.

58559.d: Third satellite completing the fifth Yaogan-39 triplet. Five Yaogan-39 groups within roughly a year of the family's debut — alongside parallel expansion of Yaogan-35 and Yaogan-36 — reflects the scale of China's mid-2020s investment in replacing its aging naval ELINT infrastructure with multiple newer, more capable reconnaissance satellite families operating simultaneously.

58582.d: An unusual entry in China's Yaogan Earth observation series — operating in geostationary orbit rather than the low-altitude orbits typical of the Yaogan surveillance constellation. A geostationary Yaogan satellite provides persistent coverage of a fixed region rather than the global sweeping coverage of LEO systems, suggesting a specialized intelligence collection role targeting a specific geographic area with continuous, unbroken observation capability.

58584.d: The second Arktika-M completes Russia's highly elliptical Arctic monitoring pair — the first system in history providing continuous geostationary-quality coverage of the North Pole 24 hours a day. As the melting Arctic generates increasingly unpredictable weather affecting the entire Northern Hemisphere, this capability is becoming indispensable.

58645.d: Tianmu #11, one of four launched together accelerating build-out of China's commercial atmospheric sounding network. The ability to mass-produce and batch-launch radio occultation satellites is changing the economics of weather data globally — and China is leading the change.

58646.d: Tianmu #12 deployed alongside three sisters in a rideshare launch. Each compact satellite provides atmospheric soundings that most improve forecasts over the world's least-observed ocean regions, where model errors accumulate fastest and storm track forecasts diverge the most.

58647.d: Tianmu #13 adds tropical coverage where it matters most. GPS occultation through moist tropical air reveals moisture structures that determine whether a hurricane intensifies into a monster or weakens into a rainmaker — the exact data that forecasters have lacked for decades.

58648.d: Tianmu #14. China's rapid commercial weather satellite deployment mirrors its infrastructure strategy in other sectors — build fast, build redundantly, achieve independence from foreign data providers. Weather data sovereignty is becoming as strategically important as energy independence.

58660.d: Tianmu #19 fills gaps in tropical plane coverage. GPS occultation through warm, moisture-laden tropical air reveals the humidity structures that determine whether a tropical disturbance becomes a catastrophic hurricane or fizzles into a rainstorm — the key data forecasters have lacked for decades.

58661.d: Tianmu #20. A geopolitical question is forming: will weather agencies in Europe, Japan, and the US build competing commercial ecosystems, or become data customers of Chinese networks? The answer will shape global meteorology infrastructure for a generation.

58662.d: Tianmu #21. Each satellite measures hundreds of atmospheric profiles per day — complete vertical slices of temperature, pressure, and humidity from the ground to the stratosphere. Across the full Tianmu constellation, it represents one of the most prolific atmospheric measurement programs in history.

58663.d: The twenty-second Tianmu satellite. For the first time, a non-Western commercial entity is positioning itself as a primary weather data supplier to meteorological services globally — including in Africa and Southeast Asia where coverage has historically been critically poor.

58698.d: Sweden's high-throughput military and government communications satellite, developed by Ovzon to provide mobile connectivity for military users and government agencies. Ovzon-3 targets a specific niche: encrypted, high-speed broadband for mobile terminals used by military units in the field, intelligence agencies, and emergency services that need reliable connectivity without large ground infrastructure. Built to survive the interference-rich electromagnetic environment of military operations.

58700.d: Tianmu #15 in a new orbital plane, dramatically increasing global revisit frequency — moving from a useful supplement toward a primary atmospheric sounding system for numerical weather prediction models worldwide.

58701.d: Tianmu #16, particularly valuable for weather models over the Southern Ocean — where conventional observations are almost nonexistent and forecast errors accumulate faster than anywhere else on Earth.

58702.d: Tianmu #17, approaching the temporal resolution that makes weather data actionable in near-real-time — the equivalent of going from one daily weather balloon launch to continuous atmospheric monitoring at thousands of locations simultaneously.

58703.d: Tianmu #18. The constellation now produces more GPS occultation soundings per day than any single national agency's system — a remarkable achievement for a commercial entity that barely existed five years before.

58957.d: RAPTOR1, a small technology demonstration or Earth observation satellite launched in 2024 as part of a rideshare mission. Satellites like this typically validate new sensor, propulsion, or communications hardware in the actual orbital environment before that technology is incorporated into larger, more capable operational missions.

58990.d: India's most advanced meteorological satellite combines 19-channel atmospheric sounding and 6-channel imaging in one spacecraft. It positions India to track monsoon dynamics and Bay of Bengal cyclones with accuracy comparable to Japan's Himawari — critical where monsoon forecasting directly affects food production for 1.4 billion people.

58995.d: Indonesia's Merah Putih-2 ('Red and White', the colors of the Indonesian flag) provides Ku and C-band communications across Southeast Asia and beyond. Indonesia's continued investment in national satellite capacity reflects a strategic commitment to connecting an archipelago nation through sovereign infrastructure. As Indonesia's digital economy grows — from e-commerce to digital government services — reliable national satellite connectivity becomes critical infrastructure for maintaining cohesion across thousands of islands.

59020.d: One of the more recent TJS satellites, part of an accelerating Chinese classified GEO program. The TJS series has continued launching at a pace of several per year despite international attention on the series's activities. China's persistent investment in classified GEO satellites demonstrates that the geostationary belt has become a domain of active military competition, mirroring US investments in AEHF, SBIRS, and space domain awareness satellites.

59051.d: Russia's fourth Meteor-M No.2 expands polar coverage as Soviet-era spacecraft retire. Russia's 11-time-zone territory requires domestic weather satellite capability for reasons as much political as scientific — no other nation's forecasts will reliably cover Russian interests as well as Russian satellites.

59065.d: Iran's domestically built Earth observation satellite, developed despite international sanctions restricting access to many space technologies. Pars-1 demonstrates Iran's determination to achieve space-based observation capability independent of foreign supply chains — a milestone in Iran's technology sovereignty program.

59069.d: The first satellite in China's Hulianwan ('Internet of Things') commercial GEO constellation — one of the first dedicated IoT satellites designed for geostationary orbit. Unlike LEO IoT constellations (Starlink, Iridium), a GEO IoT satellite covers a fixed region continuously from a single spacecraft. Hulianwan Gaogui-01 targets China's vast agricultural and industrial monitoring market, connecting remote sensors across Chinese territory that lack terrestrial connectivity.

59072.d: Marafon-D-GVM, part of a Russian satellite messaging or IoT data relay technology demonstration launched in 2024. Satellites in this class typically test store-and-forward data relay for remote sensor networks, similar in concept to Russia's established Gonets-M messaging constellation, extending Russia's push for sovereign satellite communications infrastructure independent of Western commercial providers.

59100.d: Part of ICEYE's continued 2024 constellation growth, one of dozens of satellites the company launched that year en route to its later dramatic 2025 acceleration. ICEYE-X38 serves the company's dedicated government and defense contracts — including missions for Poland's military, Ukraine's battlefield surveillance program funded by Germany's Ministry of Defence, and Greece's national space program — alongside its commercial insurance and maritime customers.

59102.d: One of ICEYE's 2024 SAR satellites, contributing to a Finnish-founded constellation that has become the world's largest commercial synthetic aperture radar operator. ICEYE-X37 flies at a 97.8° sun-synchronous inclination shared by most of the company's satellites, ensuring consistent lighting-independent imaging geometry across every pass — a radar advantage over optical satellites that still depends on orbital consistency for repeatable, comparable imagery over time.

59103.d: Part of ICEYE's growing SAR constellation, launched in 2024 as the company continued its rapid expansion trajectory ahead of the even more intense 22-satellite deployment pace it would achieve in 2025 alone. ICEYE-X36's radar imagery serves customers across insurance, defense, maritime domain awareness, and disaster response — all sectors that benefit from all-weather monitoring capability that optical satellites simply cannot provide.

59280.d: Lead satellite of China's second six-satellite Yunhai-2 batch, launched March 2024 to expand the GNSS radio occultation atmospheric sensing constellation established by the original 2018 Yunhai-2 sextet. This second batch follows the identical 50° inclination pattern of its predecessors, doubling China's radio occultation sampling density for weather prediction, ionospheric research, and — per Western assessment — PLA operational meteorological planning.
59280.t: Chinese GNSS Radio Occultation Weather Satellite (2nd Batch)

59281.d: Second of the six Yunhai-2 (02) satellites launched together in March 2024, contributing to a doubled radio occultation constellation now capable of significantly higher-resolution atmospheric sampling than the original 2018 batch alone could provide. With twelve total Yunhai-2 satellites now assessed to be in the WMO's tracked constellation, China's atmospheric sensing capability from radio occultation rivals that of dedicated commercial operators like Spire Global.
59281.t: Chinese GNSS Radio Occultation Weather Satellite (2nd Batch)

59282.d: Third of the six-satellite Yunhai-2 (02) constellation launched in March 2024. Each additional occultation satellite improves the temporal and spatial density of atmospheric soundings — the vertical temperature and humidity profiles that feed both civilian weather forecasting models and, per Western intelligence assessment, Chinese military weather planning for missile trajectory calculations and operational readiness.
59282.t: Chinese GNSS Radio Occultation Weather Satellite (2nd Batch)

59283.d: Fourth of the second Yunhai-2 batch, launched together with five sister satellites in March 2024 aboard a single Long March 2D. The repeated six-satellite deployment strategy — first used in 2018, now repeated in 2024 — reflects China's preference for establishing complete constellation coverage in a single launch rather than incrementally building capacity satellite by satellite over years.
59283.t: Chinese GNSS Radio Occultation Weather Satellite (2nd Batch)

59284.d: Fifth satellite of the March 2024 Yunhai-2 (02) sextet, expanding China's radio occultation weather sensing network to twelve total satellites across two coordinated batches. The atmospheric data these satellites collect — while officially framed as civilian disaster prevention and scientific research — is assessed by Western analysts to substantially serve PLA operational weather intelligence requirements as well.
59284.t: Chinese GNSS Radio Occultation Weather Satellite (2nd Batch)

59285.d: The sixth and final satellite of the March 2024 Yunhai-2 batch, completing the second six-satellite deployment of China's radio occultation constellation. With this launch, the combined Yunhai-2 program reached its full designed twelve-satellite capacity — a constellation the WMO's space observation database now formally tracks as a meaningful contributor to global atmospheric and space weather monitoring, dual-use military origins notwithstanding.
59285.t: Chinese GNSS Radio Occultation Weather Satellite (2nd Batch)

59290.d: First of a pair of 6U CubeSats in The Aerospace Corporation's AeroCube-16 mission, hosting a suite of advanced materials experiments in low Earth orbit. AeroCube-16A's mission is to expose new material samples to the harsh combination of vacuum, thermal cycling, atomic oxygen erosion, and radiation that defines the space environment — data used to validate ground-based testing methods and inform the material choices for future spacecraft designs across the entire Aerospace Corporation portfolio.

59291.d: Companion satellite to AeroCube-16A, flying together to conduct a comparative materials science experiment in orbit. By exposing similar or differing material samples to space simultaneously on two separate spacecraft, researchers can better isolate variables affecting material degradation — a subtle but scientifically valuable advantage of paired-satellite missions over single-spacecraft experiments.

59343.d: The second satellite in China's third-generation Yunhai atmospheric sensing program, launched in 2024 from Taiyuan Satellite Launch Center. Yunhai-3 continues the GNSS radio occultation mission pioneered by Yunhai-2 but incorporates design refinements from years of operational experience — China's pattern of iteratively improving each successive generation of a satellite program rather than freezing a design and running it unchanged for decades.
59343.t: Chinese GNSS Radio Occultation Weather Satellite (3rd Gen)

59346.d: Eutelsat's latest satellite at the 36°E Russian DTH position, replacing and expanding on Eutelsat-36B's capacity as the Russian satellite television market continues growing. The 36°E neighborhood is one of the world's highest-density DTH satellite positions — more Russian homes receive satellite television from this single slot than from all other satellite positions combined. Eutelsat-36D ensures that Russia's massive satellite TV market continues to be served from European-operated infrastructure.

59371.d: Russia's latest resource monitoring satellite provides hyperspectral and high-resolution imagery across Russia's 17 million square kilometers. Russia's territory — containing the world's largest forest reserve, major mineral deposits, and the changing Arctic — makes space-based observation essential for national resource management at a scale no other monitoring method can match.

59395.d: Yaogan-42 (01), launched as a single satellite into the 35°-inclined orbital regime shared with China's naval ELINT triplet families, but deployed alone rather than as part of a three-satellite formation. This solo deployment pattern, also seen with Yaogan-34, suggests China continues experimenting with single-satellite alternatives to its established triangulation-based surveillance architecture in this orbital band.

59510.d: First satellite of China Siwei's third-generation SuperView Neo constellation, launched in 2024 to introduce further design improvements over the Neo-1 and Neo-2 lines. Each successive Neo generation has incorporated lessons from its predecessors — a familiar pattern across nearly every Chinese commercial and military satellite program, where iterative refinement rather than infrequent generational leaps drives capability growth.
59510.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (3rd Gen)

59557.d: Yaogan-42 (02), the second satellite in this designation, continuing the program's pattern of individual rather than triplet deployment. Whether Yaogan-42 represents a genuinely different single-satellite sensing approach or simply a smaller-scale technology testbed within China's broader reconnaissance satellite ecosystem remains an open question for Western analysts tracking the program.

59625.d: The first satellite in Airbus's LEGION hyperspectral constellation brings science-grade hyperspectral imaging to a commercial daily-revisit service. Measuring 400+ spectral bands per pixel, LEGION identifies pollution events, maps mineral resources, and detects crop diseases before visible symptoms appear — generating orders of magnitude more value per pixel than standard color imagery.

59626.d: The second LEGION hyperspectral satellite builds the commercial constellation Airbus positions as the successor to traditional multispectral Earth observation. By detecting hundreds of spectral signatures invisible to standard cameras, LEGION can verify environmental compliance, authenticate agricultural claims, and map resource distribution across entire continents.

59915.d: Pakistan's newest communications satellite, significantly expanding the country's geostationary capacity. PakSat-MM1 provides enhanced broadband and broadcasting services for Pakistan's 220 million population — one of the world's largest underserved satellite markets. Pakistan's investment in new satellite capacity reflects the government's digital Pakistan initiative and the growing demand for satellite connectivity in rural and mountainous regions beyond the reach of terrestrial networks.

60086.d: The newest Astra satellite at 19.2°E, ensuring Europe's most important DTH position continues to evolve with next-generation capabilities. Astra-1P adds capacity for 4K Ultra HD channels and interactive services that require wider bandwidth than HD broadcasting. The 19.2°E position's dominance in European satellite television — sustained across three decades and five generations of Astra satellites — reflects the remarkable staying power of the direct broadcast satellite model despite streaming's rise.

60133.d: The fourth and final GOES-R series satellite, GOES-19 completed America's next-generation geostationary fleet as GOES-East in 2024. Its Advanced Baseline Imager has redefined Western Hemisphere weather forecasting. When you see a satellite image of an Atlantic hurricane on the news, it came from this spacecraft or its siblings.

60179.d: A recent Chinese communications satellite providing Ka-band high-throughput broadband across China and neighboring countries. Zhongxing-3A is part of China's push to deploy next-generation broadband satellite capacity domestically, reducing reliance on foreign high-throughput satellite services and building China's position as a satellite internet provider across its Belt and Road partner countries.

60182.d: Japan's ALOS-4 carries the most powerful L-band SAR ever flown — 3 times the power of ALOS-2 with 1-meter capability. L-band radar penetrates forest canopies to measure the trees beneath — enabling forest carbon stock measurement from orbit that will underpin the next generation of verified carbon credit markets worth hundreds of billions of dollars.

60211.d: Part of a later expansion of China's Tianhui-5 stereo mapping generation, launched in 2024 to add further capacity to the PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau's terrain intelligence constellation. As successive Tianhui-5 satellites reach orbit, they collectively increase the frequency at which China can refresh its topographic and elevation databases for strategically significant terrain — a capability that compounds in value the more satellites contribute to it.
60211.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

60212.d: The fourth satellite in China's Tianhui-5 mapping generation, expanding a constellation that by this point had grown well beyond the original two-satellite formation-flying pairs of earlier Tianhui generations. The expansion from paired to larger group deployments across the Tianhui-5 and Tianhui-6 generations reflects the PLA's growing appetite for more frequent terrain data refresh across a wider footprint of strategically relevant territory.
60212.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

60233.d: Turkey's first domestically designed and built communications satellite — a milestone comparable to India's ARSAT or Argentina's ARSAT programs. Turksat-6A was built by Turkish Aerospace Industries (TAI) using primarily domestic technology, fulfilling Turkey's ambition to become an independent satellite manufacturing nation. The satellite provides Ku-band capacity for Turkish communications services and represents decades of investment in building indigenous satellite engineering capability.

60243.d: Also called 'Méditerranée,' this shoebox-sized CubeSat was the sole French university payload aboard Ariane 6's historic maiden flight on July 9, 2024. Built by roughly 300 students over more than a decade at the University of Montpellier's Space Center, ROBUSTA-3A watches GNSS signals from Europe's own Galileo constellation reflecting off the Mediterranean Sea's surface, measuring atmospheric water vapor accumulation to help Météo-France better predict the violent 'Cévenol episodes' — sudden, catastrophic Mediterranean rainstorms that have caused deadly flash floods across southern France. As a secondary mission, it also tests how a French-made computer memory chip — one that had already flown to the Moon aboard India's Chandrayaan-3 lander — holds up against the radiation of low Earth orbit.
60243.t: French University CubeSat (Ariane 6 Maiden Flight Payload)

60327.d: The second Hulianwan GEO IoT satellite, expanding China's smart connectivity network. Hulianwan-02 provides M2M data collection across Chinese farms, mines, pipelines, and utility infrastructure — bringing real-time monitoring to remote Chinese infrastructure previously beyond the reach of affordable connectivity. China's enormous unmonitored infrastructure creates massive demand for satellite IoT services.

60419.d: Capella-13 (Acadia), one of Capella Space's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites, providing all-weather, day-and-night imagery down to 50 cm resolution. Capella's SAR constellation can image through clouds, smoke, and complete darkness — capabilities the company markets heavily to defense, insurance, and disaster response customers who cannot wait for clear skies. Acadia-class satellites represent Capella's most advanced imaging generation, competing directly with ICEYE in the rapidly growing commercial radar market.

60452.d: LEGION #3 builds daily hyperspectral coverage. Each satellite builds time-series data revealing change — crop growth curves predicting yield months before harvest, industrial emission patterns identifying violations, and ecosystem health trends driven by invisible atmospheric changes.

60453.d: The fourth LEGION satellite approaches daily global hyperspectral coverage. As a commercial product, it is creating entirely new markets for space-based chemical and biological intelligence about Earth's surface — seeing the world's chemistry from orbit for the first time at global scale.

60458.d: One of nine satellites launched together in August 2024 under the Yaogan-43 designation — an unusual program described by trackers as test satellites for future Chinese military low-Earth-orbit constellations, reportedly built by several different manufacturers and clustered under a single cover designation. The nine-satellite batch appears designed to validate constellation architecture concepts rather than serve as an operational reconnaissance system in its own right.

60459.d: Part of the nine-satellite Yaogan-43 group 01 batch launched August 16, 2024 on a Long March 4B from Xichang. The purpose of individual satellites within this diverse, multi-manufacturer group remains undisclosed, consistent with China's broader pattern of using the Yaogan designation as a flexible umbrella for reconnaissance and constellation technology that the government prefers not to detail publicly.

60460.d: One of the nine Yaogan-43 group 01 satellites, part of what trackers assess as an experimental testbed for future Chinese military satellite constellation designs. The involvement of multiple manufacturers within a single named batch is unusual for Chinese military satellite programs, which typically standardize on one primary contractor per satellite family.

60461.d: Part of the Yaogan-43 group 01 nine-satellite deployment, contributing to China's evaluation of distributed small-satellite architectures for future military low Earth orbit applications. The batch's diverse manufacturer origins hint that China may be comparison-testing competing satellite bus designs from different contractors within the same orbital deployment.

60462.d: One of nine Yaogan-43 satellites launched together in August 2024, part of an unusual multi-manufacturer batch whose specific individual missions have not been publicly disclosed. The scale of this single deployment — nine distinct satellites under one designation — suggests China is testing a broader distributed constellation concept rather than a conventional single-purpose reconnaissance mission.

60463.d: Part of the Yaogan-43 group 01 batch, contributing to China's testbed evaluation of future military LEO constellation technologies. Analysts have noted the group's composition — multiple manufacturers, unclear individual purposes — is consistent with an architecture demonstration mission rather than an operational reconnaissance deployment.

60464.d: One of the nine satellites in the first Yaogan-43 batch, launched from Xichang in August 2024. The Yaogan-43 program's unusual structure — many satellites, multiple builders, undisclosed purposes — makes it one of the more genuinely ambiguous recent additions to China's already opaque military satellite ecosystem.

60465.d: Part of the Yaogan-43 group 01 nine-satellite deployment, one of China's more unusual recent military satellite launches given its scale and multi-manufacturer composition. The batch likely serves as a proving ground for constellation management, deployment, and inter-satellite coordination techniques relevant to China's broader ambitions for distributed military space architectures.

60466.d: The final satellite of the nine-spacecraft Yaogan-43 group 01 batch, completing China's largest single-launch military satellite testbed deployment to that point. This experimental nine-satellite architecture set the stage for the smaller follow-on Yaogan-43 batches launched weeks later, suggesting an iterative testing program refining constellation design choices between successive groups.

60471.d: One of Spire Global's LEMUR-2 constellation — compact 3U CubeSats that do three remarkable things simultaneously from a single spacecraft the size of a shoebox. Each LEMUR-2 collects GPS radio occultation data to probe atmospheric temperature and humidity globally, listens to AIS transponders from ships at sea to track maritime traffic, and monitors ADS-B signals from aircraft. The name TOMATOKECHUP continues Spire's tradition of giving constellation satellites names that are memorable precisely because they're absurd. With hundreds of LEMUR satellites in orbit, Spire has built one of the world's most prolific atmospheric sounding networks — and this is one node in that planetary instrument.

60480.d: One of 36 SuperDoves in Flock 4BE, launched August 2024 alongside Tanager-1. Flock 4BE-18 carries the standard 8-band PSB.SD imaging payload contributing to Planet's constellation of 200+ satellites providing near-daily coverage of Earth's entire landmass.

60481.d: Part of Planet Labs' Flock 4BE batch, contributing 8-band 3-meter imagery to a constellation whose value lies entirely in its aggregate daily coverage. Flock 4BE-7 flies alongside 35 identical siblings launched on the same August 2024 Transporter-11 mission.

60486.d: The final satellite in Planet Labs' 36-spacecraft Flock 4BE deployment, completing the batch launched alongside Tanager-1 in August 2024. As the last-numbered satellite in this particular Flock, it rounds out Planet's continuous cycle of constellation replenishment that keeps the SuperDove fleet operating at peak daily-revisit capacity.

60487.d: One of the Flock 4BE SuperDoves, contributing to Planet's constellation of 200+ active imaging satellites. Flock 4BE-25 shares its August 2024 Transporter-11 launch with Tanager-1, part of Planet's evolution from pure medium-resolution monitoring toward a more diversified sensing portfolio.

60488.d: One of Planet Labs' Flock 4BE SuperDoves, launched August 2024 alongside Tanager-1 on Transporter-11. This satellite contributes to Planet's daily-revisit constellation, part of the company's evolution toward its first profitable year while continuing to expand global monitoring capacity.

60489.d: One of the Flock 4BE SuperDoves, contributing 3-meter 8-band imagery to Planet's continuously refreshed global dataset. This satellite launched alongside Planet's first hyperspectral instrument, Tanager-1, on the same August 2024 Transporter-11 mission.

60490.d: One of Planet Labs' Flock 4BE SuperDoves, launched on the same Transporter-11 mission that carried the landmark Tanager-1 hyperspectral satellite. This spacecraft contributes to Planet's core daily-revisit imaging product, the backbone of the company's commercial and government data business.

60492.d: The 17th SuperDove satellite in Planet Labs' Flock 4BE batch, launched on SpaceX's Transporter-11 rideshare mission in August 2024 alongside Planet's first hyperspectral satellite Tanager-1. Flock 4BE 17 carries Planet's PSB.SD sensor — an 8-band, 47-megapixel imager producing 3-meter resolution imagery across a 32.5 × 19.6 km swath. As one node in a constellation of 200+ active SuperDoves, it contributes to Planet's ability to image nearly every point on Earth's landmass every single day. The individual satellite name is anonymous; it's the constellation that matters — 200 shoebox-sized cameras circling the planet, together producing a continuous daily photograph of the entire world.

60494.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4BE deployment, one node among 200+ active SuperDoves providing Planet's signature near-daily global imaging service. Each satellite is functionally identical to its siblings, distinguished only by orbital slot within the constellation.

60495.d: One of the last-numbered satellites in Planet Labs' Flock 4BE batch, contributing to a constellation of 200+ active SuperDoves imaging nearly all of Earth's landmass every single day. This satellite completes the coverage pattern established by its 35 August 2024 launch siblings.

60497.d: Part of the Flock 4BE batch, one of the final satellites in this 36-spacecraft deployment contributing to Planet's global daily imaging service. This satellite's imagery, like all Flock 4BE SuperDoves, feeds Planet's continuously updated commercial and government dataset products.

60501.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4BE deployment, contributing to Planet's constellation-wide daily revisit of Earth's landmass. Each SuperDove in this batch carries an identical PSB.SD sensor, the design standardization that allows Planet to operate its satellites as a single coherent system rather than individually managed spacecraft.

60505.d: Part of the Flock 4BE batch, contributing 8-band 3-meter resolution imagery to Planet's constellation. This satellite is one of 36 identical SuperDoves launched together in August 2024, part of Planet's ongoing constellation replenishment and capacity expansion strategy.

60509.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4BE deployment, contributing incremental coverage to Planet's daily global imaging constellation. Like its 35 siblings from the same launch, this satellite operates the identical 8-band, 47-megapixel sensor design that defines the modern SuperDove generation.

60511.d: Part of Planet Labs' Flock 4BE batch, one of 36 SuperDoves co-launched with the Tanager-1 hyperspectral satellite in August 2024 — illustrating Planet's evolving two-tier strategy of daily medium-resolution monitoring from the SuperDove fleet complemented by specialized hyperspectral depth from newer instruments.

60512.d: One of Planet Labs' Flock 4BE SuperDoves from the August 2024 Transporter-11 mission, part of the constellation that helped drive Planet toward its first profitable year as a company by expanding both coverage capacity and data product diversity.

60513.d: Part of Planet Labs' 36-satellite Flock 4BE deployment, one of many identically equipped SuperDoves providing daily 3-meter resolution global coverage. This satellite's data feeds Planet's continuously updated PlanetScope basemap, refreshed with new imagery from the full constellation every day.

60514.d: One of the SuperDoves in Flock 4BE, launched alongside Planet's Tanager-1 hyperspectral demonstrator in August 2024. This satellite is one of over 200 active SuperDoves that together provide the daily global imaging cadence that defines Planet's core commercial product.

60516.d: One of Planet Labs' Flock 4BE SuperDoves, launched August 2024 on Transporter-11. This satellite contributes to Planet's aggregate daily-revisit dataset — a continuously refreshed global photograph of Earth's surface used by researchers, governments, and businesses tracking everything from deforestation to crop yields to military movements.

60517.d: Part of the Flock 4BE batch, contributing to Planet's daily global imaging cadence. This satellite's imagery feeds the same continuously updated PlanetScope dataset used across agriculture, environmental monitoring, disaster response, and government intelligence applications worldwide.

60518.d: One of 36 SuperDoves in Planet Labs' Flock 4BE batch, launched on SpaceX Transporter-11 in August 2024 alongside Planet's first hyperspectral satellite, Tanager-1. Flock 4BE-1 carries the 47-megapixel, 8-band PSB.SD sensor producing 3-meter resolution imagery across a 32.5 × 19.6 km swath, one node in Planet's daily global monitoring constellation.

60519.d: One of the Flock 4BE SuperDoves, part of the August 2024 batch that expanded Planet's global daily-revisit imaging capacity alongside the company's first hyperspectral satellite. Flock 4BE-28 carries the standard PSB.SD 8-band sensor shared across the entire modern SuperDove fleet.

60539.d: One of ICEYE's SAR satellites launched in the transition period between the company's 2024 and 2025 expansion phases, contributing incrementally to a constellation whose value compounds with every additional satellite — more satellites mean shorter gaps between images of any given location, which is the entire commercial proposition behind ICEYE's business.

60546.d: One of ICEYE's SAR satellites from a later 2024 launch batch, contributing to the company's steadily improving global revisit performance. By this point ICEYE had established itself not just as a commercial imaging provider but as a strategic supplier of persistent surveillance capability to European governments navigating heightened security concerns following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

60548.d: One of ICEYE's SAR satellites from the company's intensive 2024 constellation expansion, part of the buildout toward the 40+ satellites that would be operational by early 2025. ICEYE-X33 contributes to the company's dense revisit coverage — the operational goal of imaging any point on Earth multiple times within a single day, regardless of weather or darkness, that distinguishes ICEYE's business model from traditional optical Earth observation operators.

60549.d: Part of ICEYE's ongoing constellation expansion in 2024, contributing radar imaging capacity that operates identically day or night, in clear skies or hurricane conditions. This weather-independence is ICEYE's core commercial differentiator — the moment a disaster strikes, when clouds and darkness typically blind optical satellites for days, ICEYE's radar fleet keeps working without interruption.

60559.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4BE batch, one of the SuperDoves that expanded Planet's imaging capacity alongside the historic Tanager-1 hyperspectral launch. Each Flock 4BE satellite is functionally identical, distinguished only by its specific orbital position within the constellation's coverage pattern.

60561.d: One of the Flock 4BE SuperDoves from the August 2024 Transporter-11 mission, contributing 3-meter resolution 8-band imagery to Planet's constellation of 200+ active satellites providing near-daily coverage of Earth's entire landmass.

60563.d: Part of Planet Labs' Flock 4BE batch, one of the final satellites deployed in this 36-spacecraft mission. By late 2024, Planet's SuperDove constellation had become a mature, production-phase operation — each new Flock replenishing capacity rather than representing any novel technological step.

60606.d: One of China's newest commercial communications satellites, continuing the expansion of the Zhongxing fleet. ChinaSat's commercial satellite expansion reflects China's ambition to become a major provider of satellite services across Asia, Africa, and Latin America — markets where Western operators have traditionally dominated but where Chinese pricing and political relationships are creating new competitive dynamics.

60945.d: Lead satellite of the six-satellite Yaogan-43 group 02 batch, launched September 3, 2024 — just weeks after the first nine-satellite group. The smaller six-satellite size of this second batch, compared to the first group's nine, suggests China may have been testing different constellation scales or configurations as part of the same broader LEO architecture demonstration program.

60946.d: Part of the Yaogan-43 group 02 six-satellite batch, contributing to China's rapid-succession testing of military LEO constellation concepts in late 2024. Two major Yaogan-43 batches within a single month reflected an unusually fast experimental cadence even by the standards of China's already prolific military satellite launch program.

60947.d: One of the Yaogan-43 group 02 satellites, part of China's ongoing evaluation of distributed small-satellite constellation architectures for future military applications. The program's rapid iteration between the first and second batches — different satellite counts, likely different configurations — suggests active technical refinement rather than a settled operational design.

60948.d: Part of the six-satellite Yaogan-43 group 02 batch, contributing to China's testbed program for next-generation military LEO constellations. As with the earlier nine-satellite group, the specific individual missions of Yaogan-43 group 02 satellites remain undisclosed by Chinese authorities.

60949.d: One of the Yaogan-43 group 02 satellites, launched in the second of what would become three distinct Yaogan-43 batch deployments through 2024 and 2025. The program's evolving batch sizes — nine, then six, then a final three-satellite group — trace an experimental design process converging toward a smaller, more standardized constellation unit.

60950.d: The final satellite of the six-spacecraft Yaogan-43 group 02 batch. Together with the earlier nine-satellite group, fifteen Yaogan-43 satellites had reached orbit within a single month in 2024, an unusually rapid testbed deployment cadence for a program whose ultimate operational purpose remains publicly unconfirmed.

60989.d: The third Sentinel-2 ensures continuity of 10-meter land monitoring well into the 2030s. With three satellites, the combined constellation provides global land coverage every 2-3 days — fast enough to track vegetation across a full growing season, detect new deforestation within days, and monitor every major agricultural region continuously.

61503.d: The third Hulianwan GEO IoT satellite, continuing China's development of geostationary machine-to-machine connectivity. From GEO, these satellites provide continuous coverage of a fixed region without the latency and coverage gaps of LEO IoT systems — making them well-suited for applications like agricultural monitoring, wildlife tracking, and remote infrastructure management across China's vast western territories.

61617.d: Lead satellite of the third and smallest Yaogan-43 group — a three-satellite batch that, unlike the program's earlier nine- and six-satellite deployments, matches the classic triplet formation used throughout China's established ELINT and reconnaissance satellite families. This convergence toward the familiar three-satellite unit suggests the Yaogan-43 testbed program may have concluded its architecture experimentation and settled on a standard operational configuration.

61618.d: Second satellite of the Yaogan-43 group 03 triplet, completing the program's transition from larger experimental batches (nine, then six satellites) down to the standard three-satellite formation that has defined Chinese naval and reconnaissance ELINT triplets since Yaogan-9 in 2010.

61619.d: Third satellite completing the Yaogan-43 group 03 triplet. Across its three deployment batches — 9, then 6, then 3 satellites — the Yaogan-43 program appears to have run a genuine design experiment before settling, in its final iteration, on the same triangulation-friendly three-satellite formation that has proven durable across nearly every other Chinese naval surveillance satellite family.

61733.d: Japan's third Defense Satellite Network satellite, providing secure X-band communications for the Japan Self-Defense Forces. Kirameki-3 ('Twinkle' in Japanese) supports Japanese maritime, ground, and air forces with protected communications for coalition operations with the US and for independent national defense missions. Japan's investment in dedicated military communications reflects the country's evolving security posture and reinterpretation of its pacifist constitution.

61735.d: Russia's ionospheric monitoring satellite measures electron density in the layer that reflects radio signals and enables over-the-horizon communications. Solar storms distort it, disrupting GPS, HF radio, and satellite links. Ionosfera-M provides measurements needed to forecast and mitigate disruptions to critical infrastructure from power grids to aviation.

61736.d: The companion to Ionosfera-M 1, together providing the first Russian two-satellite ionospheric monitoring system distinguishing spatial from temporal variations. Flying in complementary orbits, the pair builds the three-dimensional ionospheric picture critical for maintaining GLONASS accuracy during solar disturbances at solar maximum.

61910.d: A next-generation Korean commercial communications satellite extending KT SAT's regional coverage. Koreasat-6A provides Ku and Ka-band services for Korean broadcasting, enterprise networks, maritime communications, and aviation broadband. South Korea's high-technology maritime industry — one of the world's largest shipbuilding sectors — creates specific demand for maritime satellite communications that Koreasat-6A helps meet.

62006.d: Australia's newest commercial satellite, providing expanded Ka-band capacity for Optus's growing satellite services portfolio. Australia's satellite market continues to grow as NBN satellite internet drives infrastructure investment and commercial demand. Optus-X extends the operator's services and provides redundancy for Australia's critical satellite communications infrastructure in a country where satellite remains essential connectivity infrastructure for millions of rural residents.

62028.d: India's newest and highest-throughput commercial satellite, launched on SpaceX's Falcon 9 after ISRO's GSLV Mk III launch schedule conflicted with the satellite's delivery timeline. GSAT-N2 provides Ka-band broadband with 48 high-power spot beams covering the entire Indian subcontinent — enough throughput to support India's government Digital India broadband expansion programs and commercial satellite internet subscribers simultaneously. Its launch on an American rocket raised eyebrows but reflected ISRO's pragmatic approach to commercial timelines.

62079.d: Third satellite of the SuperView Neo-2 sub-constellation, launched in 2025 to expand China Siwei's second-generation Neo fleet beyond its original two-satellite pair. As both the Neo-1 and Neo-2 lines grow toward four satellites each, China Siwei is assembling an eight-satellite next-generation commercial imaging fleet — a substantial capability expansion from the company's earlier SuperView-1 generation.
62079.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

62080.d: Fourth satellite of the SuperView Neo-2 sub-constellation, completing this generation's four-satellite formation. Between the Neo-1 and Neo-2 lines, China Siwei's next-generation commercial constellation now provides materially faster global revisit than the original SuperView-1 satellites — part of China's broader strategy of establishing credible domestic alternatives to every category of Western commercial space capability.
62080.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

62256.d: The Coronagraph Spacecraft half of ESA's most audacious formation-flying experiment ever attempted. PROBA-3's two satellites — this one carrying the telescope, its partner the Occulter carrying a 1.4-meter disk — fly in a precisely choreographed dance 150 meters apart for six hours out of every 19.6-hour orbit, blocking the Sun's blinding disk to reveal the faint solar corona in a way no single spacecraft ever could. Together they form what is effectively one 150-meter-long virtual telescope, holding formation to millimeter precision and arcsecond pointing accuracy entirely autonomously, without any commands from the ground. Launched December 5, 2024, PROBA-3 has already produced over 50 artificial solar eclipses and up to 250 hours of unprecedented corona observation — a technique first attempted crudely during the 1975 Apollo-Soyuz mission and now perfected fifty years later.

62258.d: The Occulter Spacecraft — the other half of ESA's revolutionary formation-flying solar observatory. This 200 kg satellite carries a 1.4-meter occulting disk that blocks direct sunlight from reaching its partner spacecraft 150 meters away, artificially recreating the conditions of a total solar eclipse for up to six hours at a stretch — roughly 50 times longer than nature ever provides. A laser-based Fine Lateral and Longitudinal Sensor fires between the two spacecraft, measuring their relative position to within a millimeter, while a shadow position sensor uses an onboard algorithm reading light intensity around the coronagraph aperture to keep the Coronagraph spacecraft locked precisely inside the Occulter's shadow. It is, quite literally, one satellite's entire job to cast a shadow on another satellite — with extreme precision, for science.

62259.d: A newer SiriusXM satellite continuing the modernization of its broadcasting constellation. SiriusXM's satellite radio service reaches subscribers from Alaska to the Caribbean, maintaining uninterrupted programming during long road trips across North America — a connectivity experience that terrestrial radio cannot replicate. SXM-9 expands the system's capacity and provides additional orbital positions for improved coverage.

62261.d: The satellite that saved Europe's all-weather eye on Earth. When Sentinel-1B suffered a fatal power failure in December 2021, the Copernicus program lost half its C-band radar coverage overnight — leaving only Sentinel-1A to cover the entire planet alone for three years. Sentinel-1C, launched December 5, 2024 on the returning-to-flight Vega-C rocket, restored the full twin-satellite constellation and its critical 6-day global revisit cycle. Its radar sees through clouds, storms, and total darkness to track Arctic sea ice for shipping safety, detect oil spills, monitor ground subsidence beneath cities to within millimeters, and now — for the first time on any Sentinel-1 satellite — carries an AIS receiver that identifies and tracks ships by their transponder signals directly alongside the radar imagery of the vessels themselves.

62374.d: A recent TJS satellite continuing China's classified GEO military program. The series has grown beyond what any 'communication technology experiment' designation can justify — the number, pace, and observed behavior of TJS satellites indicate an operational military communications and/or intelligence collection program of considerable scale, rivaling the combined GEO military presence of the US and its allies.

62384.d: One of ICEYE's newest SAR satellites, launched into the same distinctive 45° inclination as ICEYE-X47 — part of a deliberate orbital plane diversification strategy that gives ICEYE customers better revisit performance over specific lower-latitude regions of commercial or strategic interest, complementing the company's core sun-synchronous polar constellation.

62389.d: Part of ICEYE's late-2025 deployment into a distinctive low 45° inclination orbit — a departure from the sun-synchronous orbits typical of most ICEYE satellites, suggesting a deliberate expansion into lower-latitude coverage optimized for equatorial and tropical regions where sun-synchronous coverage patterns are less operationally advantageous for specific customer requirements.

62455.d: The first of NuView's revolutionary commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites in geostationary orbit — an extraordinary technical ambition. Traditional SAR satellites operate from low Earth orbit to concentrate their radar energy; NuView is attempting to operate SAR from 35,800 km altitude, which requires enormously more powerful signals. If successful, NuView Alpha would provide continuous persistent SAR surveillance of a fixed target area rather than the periodic passes of LEO SAR — fundamentally changing what persistent intelligence collection looks like.

62456.d: A Philippine communications satellite — 'Agila' means 'Eagle' in Filipino. The Philippines has historically relied on foreign satellite operators to connect its 7,600 islands, and Agila represents the country's effort to develop sovereign satellite capability for Southeast Asia's second-largest archipelago nation. The satellite provides broadcasting and telecommunications capacity across the Philippine archipelago where satellite connectivity is often the most practical link between islands.

62457.d: The companion to NuView Alpha in the world's first commercial GEO SAR constellation attempt. NuView Bravo adds a second coverage node to what could become persistent all-weather surveillance from geostationary orbit — a capability that would make it impossible to conceal large-scale military movements, industrial activity, or environmental changes from constant radar observation. Whether the technology achieves its promise will determine whether GEO SAR becomes a major commercial market.

62483.d: Thuraya's newest satellite, incorporating IoT machine-to-machine services alongside traditional voice and broadband capabilities. Thuraya-4 reflects the evolution of mobile satellite services toward connected devices — sensors, tracking systems, and industrial monitors that need occasional small data bursts rather than continuous voice calls. The satellite serves Middle Eastern and African markets where demand for satellite IoT connectivity is growing as agriculture, logistics, and resource extraction industries modernize.

62485.d: One of China's latest Shijian technology demonstration satellites in geostationary orbit. China's rapid succession of SJ technology demonstrators reflects the pace at which Chinese space technology is advancing — each SJ satellite incorporating lessons and pushing capabilities further than its predecessor. The technologies validated in the SJ program become the foundation for China's next generation of operational military and civil satellites.

62613.d: Part of the Flock 4G batch, one of 36 SuperDoves launched on Transporter-12 as part of the constellation that helped Planet achieve its first profitable year as a company. Each satellite carries the standardized 47-megapixel, 8-band PSB.SD imaging payload.

62621.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves, contributing to Planet's daily-revisit constellation. This satellite launched alongside 35 siblings in January 2025, part of the constellation expansion that supported Planet's transition to profitability that same year.

62622.d: One of Planet Labs' Flock 4G SuperDoves, contributing to a constellation providing near-daily 3-meter resolution coverage of nearly all of Earth's landmass. This satellite operates alongside 35 identical siblings from the same January 2025 launch.

62624.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves from January 2025, contributing to Planet's constellation of 200+ active satellites. This spacecraft's daily imagery joins that of its siblings to build the continuously refreshed global photograph that defines Planet's core commercial product.

62625.d: Part of Planet Labs' Flock 4G batch, one of 36 satellites deployed on the January 2025 Transporter-12 rideshare mission. This spacecraft joins Planet's 200+ satellite SuperDove fleet providing daily global imaging at 3-meter resolution.

62629.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves launched January 2025, part of Planet's mature operational constellation that images nearly all of Earth's landmass every single day. This satellite's 8-band sensor produces imagery identical in specification to all other active SuperDoves.

62633.d: Part of Planet Labs' Flock 4G batch, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in January 2025. This spacecraft contributes incremental daily coverage to a constellation whose value compounds with every additional satellite in orbit.

62634.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4G deployment, contributing to Planet's daily-revisit global imaging service. This satellite is one of many identical SuperDoves that together image nearly all of Earth's landmass every single day.

62636.d: Part of Planet Labs' Flock 4G deployment, contributing daily 3-meter resolution imagery to the company's core commercial product. This satellite launched in January 2025 as part of the batch that helped push Planet toward its first profitable operating year.

62637.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4G deployment, contributing to Planet's daily global imaging constellation. This spacecraft operates the same standardized PSB.SD 8-band sensor found across the entire modern SuperDove fleet, producing 3-meter resolution imagery.

62639.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves launched on Transporter-12 in January 2025, feeding Planet's continuously updated global basemap product. Everything from crop condition monitoring to deforestation tracking to seasonal flood mapping draws on the aggregate daily coverage this satellite and its siblings provide together.

62641.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves, part of the January 2025 batch that expanded Planet's constellation ahead of the company's first profitable year. This satellite carries the identical 47-megapixel, 8-band imaging payload standard across the modern SuperDove generation.

62642.d: One of Planet Labs' Flock 4G SuperDoves, launched on Transporter-12 in January 2025. This spacecraft's imagery feeds Planet's continuously updated PlanetScope dataset, part of the company's core commercial and government data business.

62645.d: Part of the Flock 4G batch, one of Planet's newest SuperDoves as of the January 2025 Transporter-12 mission. This satellite contributes to a constellation of 200+ active spacecraft imaging nearly all of Earth's landmass every day.

62646.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves from Transporter-12, part of Planet's mature production-phase constellation. This satellite's data joins that of its siblings to build the continuously refreshed daily photograph of Earth that Planet sells to commercial and government customers worldwide.

62647.d: One of Planet Labs' Flock 4G SuperDoves, launched alongside 35 identical siblings in January 2025. This satellite's imagery feeds Planet's global commercial data products, part of the constellation that helped the company achieve profitability that same year.

62650.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves, part of the January 2025 Transporter-12 batch. This spacecraft joins Planet's mature SuperDove constellation, contributing daily coverage to the company's flagship PlanetScope imaging product.

62651.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves, part of a 36-satellite batch that expanded Planet's daily-revisit imaging capacity in early 2025. This spacecraft's 8-band sensor is identical to every other active SuperDove, the standardization that lets Planet operate its fleet as one coherent system.

62652.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves from January 2025, contributing to a constellation of 200+ active satellites providing near-daily 3-meter global coverage. Its imagery is functionally identical to its 35 launch siblings.

62659.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4G deployment, one of Planet's newest SuperDoves contributing to the constellation's daily global revisit capability. Flock 4G-4's imagery is functionally identical to its 35 launch siblings — the value proposition lies in the aggregate coverage, not any individual satellite.

62660.d: Part of Flock 4G, one of 36 identically equipped SuperDoves deployed together on Transporter-12. This satellite operates in Planet's mature production-phase constellation, where each new Flock replenishes aging satellites and maintains the fleet's daily-revisit capacity.

62661.d: Part of Planet Labs' Flock 4G batch from January 2025, contributing to a constellation of 200+ SuperDoves providing daily 3-meter global imaging. Alongside the Pelican high-resolution constellation, SuperDoves like this one form the medium-resolution backbone of Planet's two-tier Earth observation service.

62663.d: One of Planet Labs' Flock 4G SuperDoves, part of the January 2025 launch that helped push Planet's active satellite count past previous highs. This spacecraft contributes 8-band imagery to a data pipeline generating terabytes of fresh Earth imagery daily.
