62666.d: One of 36 SuperDoves in Planet Labs' Flock 4G batch, launched January 14, 2025 on SpaceX Transporter-12. This flock is part of the constellation underpinning Planet's transformation into the first profitable New Space company, announced in late 2025. Flock 4G-1 carries the 8-band PSB.SD sensor imaging Earth at 3-meter resolution across a 32.5 km swath.

62667.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4G deployment, one of Planet's newest SuperDoves as of early 2025. This spacecraft carries the standard PSB.SD sensor producing 3-meter, 8-band imagery across a 32.5 × 19.6 km swath per pass.

62670.d: Part of the 36-satellite Flock 4G deployment, contributing incremental global coverage to Planet's daily-revisit imaging service. This satellite's data feeds the same continuously updated PlanetScope dataset used across agriculture, defense, and environmental monitoring applications worldwide.

62675.d: The final-numbered satellite in Planet Labs' Flock 4G batch, completing the 36-spacecraft deployment from Transporter-12 in January 2025. This satellite rounds out the coverage pattern established by its launch siblings, part of the constellation that helped Planet achieve its first profitable operating year.

62678.d: One of Planet Labs' Flock 4G SuperDoves, part of Transporter-12's January 2025 rideshare mission. This satellite's imagery contributes to Planet's global daily photograph of Earth, used across agriculture, insurance, government, and environmental monitoring sectors.

62679.d: Part of the Flock 4G batch, one of 36 identically equipped satellites deployed together to expand Planet's global daily imaging capacity. This satellite operates alongside its siblings as part of Planet's mature, production-phase SuperDove constellation.

62680.d: One of the Flock 4G SuperDoves launched on Transporter-12, feeding Planet's continuously updated global basemap. This satellite is one of 36 identically equipped spacecraft that together compress the time between images of any given location on Earth.

62681.d: Part of the Flock 4G batch, contributing to Planet's constellation of 200+ active imaging satellites. Like its 35 launch siblings, this satellite operates the identical 8-band PSB.SD sensor standardized across Planet's entire modern SuperDove fleet.

62685.d: Part of Planet Labs' Flock 4G deployment, one of the later-numbered satellites in this 36-spacecraft batch. This satellite continues Planet's daily global imaging mission, part of the constellation supporting the company's transition to sustained profitability.

62698.d: Part of ICEYE's record-setting 2025 deployment year, contributing to a constellation whose rapid growth reflected both surging commercial demand and a wave of new government contracts as European nations sought sovereign radar surveillance capability independent of both American and Russian systems in an increasingly unstable security environment.

62700.d: One of ICEYE's SAR satellites from the company's explosive 2025 growth phase, which added 22 satellites in a single year and pushed the total constellation past 60 spacecraft. ICEYE-X41 contributes to what has become the densest commercial SAR revisit network in the world, with sub-daily coverage of most populated and strategically significant areas on Earth.

62705.d: One of ICEYE's later 2025 SAR satellites, contributing to a constellation that by year-end had grown large enough to offer meaningfully sub-daily revisit over most of the world's strategically and commercially significant regions — the culmination of a growth trajectory that began with a single 100 kg proof-of-concept satellite in 2018.

62707.d: Part of ICEYE's 2025 constellation surge, one of dozens of satellites the Finnish company deployed that year as it cemented its position as the world's largest commercial SAR operator by satellite count, well ahead of competitors still operating single-digit or low-double-digit radar constellations.

62725.d: A high-resolution optical reconnaissance satellite in Saudi Arabia's growing Earth observation constellation. The Kingdom's rapid expansion of domestic space capability reduces dependence on commercial imagery providers for monitoring its vast territory, oil infrastructure, and the regional security environment.

62756.d: USA 485, a classified National Reconnaissance Office satellite launched in early 2025 as part of the agency's rapid pivot toward a 'proliferated architecture' of many small, resilient satellites rather than a handful of large, expensive ones. The NRO deployed nearly 100 such satellites across 2024 and 2025 alone. USA 485 orbits at a 43° inclination optimized for revisit coverage over mid-latitude Eurasia and the Middle East, one node in a constellation whose individual satellites' specific roles — imagery, signals intelligence, or communications relay — the NRO does not publicly disclose.

62757.d: A classified National Reconnaissance Office satellite launched January 21, 2025 on a Falcon 9 from Cape Canaveral — one of the dozens of small NRO satellites deployed in 2024–2025 as part of the agency's 'proliferated architecture' strategy. The NRO deployed nearly 100 satellites in 2024 alone, deliberately shifting from a few large expensive spacecraft to many small resilient ones. USA 486 orbits at 43° inclination in low Earth orbit, a geometry that provides global coverage while optimizing revisit time over mid-latitude regions including most of Eurasia, the Middle East, and China's major military bases. The NRO does not describe its satellites' missions; the proliferated architecture satellites are believed to include imagery, signals intelligence, and communications relay variants built by SpaceX and Northrop Grumman.

62804.d: Part of China's rapidly expanding TJS classified GEO program. The frequency with which China is launching these satellites — sometimes multiple per year — indicates urgency in building out a capability the PLA considers operationally essential. The TJS constellation provides China with a robust, redundant, and presumably jam-resistant military communications network across the geostationary arc.

62852.d: Spain's next-generation military communications satellite, incorporating quantum-safe encryption, improved anti-jam capability, and significantly higher throughput than the original Spainsat. Spainsat NG I keeps Spain at the forefront of European military space capabilities — a strategic choice reflecting Spain's growing role in NATO and EU defense initiatives and its ambition to maintain sovereign communications for national security missions.

62876.d: A newer Michibiki satellite expanding Japan's quasi-zenith constellation. Japan plans to expand to seven Michibiki satellites to achieve redundant, always-available high-accuracy navigation across Japan and the Asia-Pacific region. Each additional satellite improves service availability and provides backup during maintenance of other constellation members — essential as Japan integrates Michibiki into critical infrastructure including aviation, maritime navigation, and emergency management systems.

63075.d: A Chinese replacement or supplementary communications satellite in the Zhongxing fleet. The 'R' suffix indicates a replenishment mission, ensuring continuity of service as older satellites approach end of life. China's systematic satellite replacement program demonstrates the commercial maturity of its satellite communications sector — managing a fleet across multiple generations rather than ad-hoc procurement.

63125.d: Third satellite of China Siwei's SuperView Neo-1 constellation, launched 2025 to further densify the company's next-generation commercial high-resolution imaging fleet. With three Neo-1 satellites now sharing the same orbital plane, China Siwei can offer customers meaningfully faster revisit times than the original two-satellite Neo-1 deployment alone provided — an incremental but commercially important improvement in a competitive global imagery market.
63125.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

63126.d: Fourth satellite of the SuperView Neo-1 sub-constellation, completing a four-satellite formation that provides China Siwei with substantially improved global coverage frequency compared to the original two-satellite Neo-1 pair. Each additional Neo satellite compounds the constellation's commercial value proposition against Western competitors like Planet's Pelican and Airbus's Pléiades Neo constellations.
63126.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

63157.d: One of the newest TJS satellites, reflecting the ongoing prioritization of GEO military communications within China's space program. China's pace of classified GEO launches has raised concerns in the US Space Force and among allied nations about the nature and intentions of the TJS program, prompting increased space domain awareness investment by Western nations to better understand what these satellites are actually doing in orbit.

63208.d: Second satellite of the SuperView Neo-3 generation, launched in 2025 to establish an operational pair for China Siwei's newest commercial imaging line. With Neo-1, Neo-2, and now Neo-3 all operational or expanding, China Siwei has built one of the most extensive next-generation commercial imaging portfolios of any single operator outside the United States and Europe.
63208.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (3rd Gen)

63220.d: Part of the newest generation in The Aerospace Corporation's quarter-century CubeSat technology demonstration program, following AeroCube-16's materials science work and building on decades of accumulated small-satellite engineering experience dating back to the 1999 MEPSI mission. Each new AeroCube mission continues Aerospace's role as an in-house proving ground for technologies too experimental or unproven for immediate use on operational national security satellites.

63222.d: Companion satellite to AeroCube-18A, continuing The Aerospace Corporation's tradition of flying paired or small-formation CubeSats to validate technologies that require more than one spacecraft to properly test — whether formation flying, comparative experiments, or distributed sensing techniques that inform the design of future operational defense and civil space systems.

63253.d: ICEYE-X48, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is ICEYE's Gen4 satellites now deliver resolution as fine as 16 cm — a leap from the 10-meter imagery the company's original 2018 satellite produced, achieved in barely six years of continuous design iteration. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

63255.d: ICEYE-X50, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is part of the record 22-satellite deployment year that pushed ICEYE's total constellation past 60 spacecraft by early 2025. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

63257.d: ICEYE-X51, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is contributing to ICEYE's core commercial promise: imagery that doesn't wait for clear skies, delivered to insurers, militaries, and disaster responders who cannot afford to wait days for cloud cover to lift. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

63258.d: ICEYE-X46, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is one of the satellites underpinning ICEYE's dedicated national security contracts, including missions supporting Ukraine's battlefield surveillance, Poland's MikroSAR program, and Greece's national space effort. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

63262.d: LEMUR 2 Marache-Fran, one of Spire Global's LEMUR-2 constellation satellites — compact 3U CubeSats that simultaneously track weather via GPS radio occultation, ships via AIS transponder signals, and aircraft via ADS-B, all from a single spacecraft the size of a shoebox. With hundreds of LEMUR satellites launched since 2013, Spire has built one of the world's most prolific commercial atmospheric sounding and maritime tracking networks.

63263.d: SOAP, a small technology demonstration CubeSat deployed as part of a large 2025 rideshare mission carrying dozens of small satellites to orbit simultaneously. Rideshare missions like this one have become the standard way universities, startups, and research institutions get compact experimental payloads to orbit — sharing a single rocket's excess capacity dramatically cuts the cost of testing new small-satellite technology compared to a dedicated launch.

63361.d: A newer Tianlian second-generation relay satellite expanding China's orbital communications coverage. Tianlian 2-04 supports not only the Tiangong Space Station but China's growing fleet of commercial and government Earth observation satellites, which increasingly require high-rate downlink channels that the Tianlian system provides independently of ground station networks.

63397.d: A recent TJS satellite continuing China's classified GEO military program at an accelerating pace. TJS-16 is one of multiple TJS launches in 2024 alone, demonstrating China's commitment to rapidly expanding its protected military communications architecture in geostationary orbit as global competition in the space domain intensifies.

63524.d: One of the most recent TJS satellites, part of what has become China's most rapidly growing classified GEO program. The TJS series has become a central concern for US Space Force planners attempting to characterize China's geostationary military presence — understanding what these satellites actually do is critical to understanding the military balance in space.

63662.d: One of the newest Chinese data relay satellites, continuing the expansion of China's orbital communications infrastructure toward a full global constellation. Tianlian 2-05 represents China's sustained investment in the critical but unglamorous infrastructure that makes everything else in its space program possible — connecting crewed spacecraft, science satellites, and exploration missions to the ground continuously, regardless of where they are in their orbit.

63772.d: ESA's most unusual Earth observation satellite fires 70 cm radar wavelengths long enough to penetrate dense forest canopies and measure woody biomass. Biomass will produce the first global map of forest carbon stocks — the missing variable holding the largest uncertainty in climate science. If forests store twice as much carbon as we think, everything about climate policy needs revisiting.

63918.d: Lead satellite of the second Yaogan-40 group, expanding China's polar-orbit naval surveillance constellation to a full three-satellite triplet formation — a shift from the two-satellite configuration of the first Yaogan-40 group. This triplet arrangement restores the triangulation geometry that has defined China's ELINT satellite methodology since the original Yaogan-9 formation in 2010, now applied at global polar coverage rather than regional Pacific coverage.
63918.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (Polar Orbit Triplet)

63919.d: Second satellite of the second Yaogan-40 triplet, part of China's expanding polar-orbit naval surveillance architecture. With this launch, Yaogan-40 adopted the same three-satellite triangulation formation used throughout China's decade-long ELINT satellite program, but at an orbital inclination providing genuinely global rather than Pacific-limited ocean surveillance.
63919.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (Polar Orbit Triplet)

63920.d: Third satellite completing the second Yaogan-40 triplet. The transition from Yaogan-40's initial pair configuration to a full triplet suggests China refined the program's design after evaluating the first group's performance, converging on the same triangulation geometry proven across a decade of Pacific-focused ELINT triplets but now extended to worldwide coverage.
63920.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (Polar Orbit Triplet)

63924.d: A newly launched TJS satellite extending China's classified GEO military constellation. The sheer number of TJS satellites — exceeding 20 by 2025 — and their observed proximity operations behavior represent a fundamental change in how China is approaching the geostationary domain: not merely as a communications medium but as an active operational environment where Chinese military capabilities must be continuously present.

64062.d: A Chinese communications satellite providing C and Ku-band capacity for government and commercial users. ChinaSat-3B serves China's growing satellite communications market, supporting government networks, enterprise data links, maritime and aviation connectivity, and regional broadcasting across the Asia-Pacific — a market where Chinese operators have been aggressively expanding at the expense of international competitors.

64199.d: Shijian-26, launched May 29, 2025 on a Long March 4B — a genuinely unusual satellite in China's already opaque Shijian technology demonstration series, jointly built by the Shanghai Academy of Spaceflight Technology's Dongfanghong satellite division, the Harbin Institute of Technology, and the Chinese Academy of Sciences' Changchun Institute of Optics. Both HIT and CIOMP's commercial offshoot Chang Guang Satellite Technology are under US sanctions for alleged military technology work and providing geospatial intelligence to the Houthis and the Wagner Group — a consortium pairing that analysts flagged as a 'noteworthy' signal of the satellite's likely dual-use military purpose despite its official 'land survey and environmental management' description.
64199.a: CAST / HIT / CAS Changchun Institute of Optics
64199.t: Chinese Experimental Satellite (Assessed Dual-Use Remote Sensing)

64290.d: One of SiriusXM's newest satellites, maintaining the robust multi-satellite architecture that delivers uninterrupted audio programming to tens of millions of subscribers. SXM-10's contribution to the constellation ensures that SiriusXM continues to provide a service uniquely suited to the mobile American lifestyle — entertainment that follows you from New York to Los Angeles without a single interruption or signal search.

64467.d: One of the most recent classified Russian military satellites in geostationary orbit. Launched in 2024 as Russia maintains pace with its military space modernization program, COSMOS 2589 represents Russia's continued investment in assured military communications in space — a capability seen as essential for command and control of Russian strategic forces and coordination of military operations across its global military presence.

64470.d: The latest ChinaSat-9 direct-to-home broadcasting satellite, providing next-generation HDTV and 4K content delivery across China. ChinaSat-9C supports China's national goal of transitioning its satellite TV broadcasting infrastructure to high-definition standards, reaching the hundreds of millions of Chinese viewers who receive television primarily via satellite dish rather than cable.

64570.d: ICEYE's 54th satellite, launched as part of the company's intense 2025 deployment campaign that put 22 satellites in orbit in a single year. ICEYE-X54 operates in X-band SAR and contributes to the company's ability to revisit any point on Earth within hours — a sub-daily monitoring cadence critical for tracking evolving disasters, monitoring disputed maritime zones, and detecting infrastructure changes as they happen rather than days later. ICEYE reached 62 satellites by December 2025 and has continued expanding in 2026, serving customers across the insurance, defense, maritime, and humanitarian sectors who need imagery that doesn't wait for weather to clear.

64572.d: ICEYE-X52, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is flying in ICEYE's standard sun-synchronous polar orbit, ensuring consistent lighting-independent imaging geometry across every single pass. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

64574.d: ICEYE-X56, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is part of the Finnish company's push toward sub-daily global revisit, the operational threshold that makes persistent monitoring — not just occasional snapshots — commercially possible. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

64578.d: ICEYE-X57, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is contributing dwell-mode imaging capability, staring at a fixed target for up to 25 seconds to sharpen resolution and reduce speckle noise for detailed structural monitoring. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

64581.d: ICEYE-X55, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is one of dozens of satellites that helped ICEYE become the world's largest operator of commercial synthetic aperture radar by satellite count. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

64584.d: ICEYE-X53, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is part of ICEYE's constellation supporting the 2025-2026 growth phase that saw the company's total fleet more than double in under two years. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

64694.d: Japan's next-generation greenhouse gas satellite combines CO2 and methane measurement with ocean wind sensing. It measures greenhouse gas concentrations precisely enough to verify whether national commitments under the Paris Agreement are being kept — a scientific fact-checker for climate diplomacy operating from orbit.

64723.d: Europe's first geostationary infrared sounder — a completely new capability for European meteorology. MTG-S1 doesn't image clouds; it measures temperature and humidity in 3D across Europe and Africa every hour in 3,600 spectral channels. Scientists compare it to replacing a 2D weather map with a 3D scan — the leap MRI brought to medicine, applied to the sky. Expected to extend severe storm warnings by 30 minutes.

64784.d: Israel's first dedicated satellite for the country's ultra-Orthodox Haredi community — providing specially curated television content without internet connectivity that meets strict religious content guidelines. Dror ('Freedom' in Hebrew) broadcasts kosher programming including news, religious content, and educational channels to an audience of over a million Israelis who observe strict media content standards. It occupies an unusual niche — perhaps the only satellite whose mission is defined by religious content governance rather than commercial or military requirements.

64876.d: Russia's third Ionosfera-M expands the constellation as solar maximum approaches with more frequent ionospheric disturbances. The multi-satellite network provides continuous monitoring needed to issue timely space weather warnings to Russia's power grid operators, aviation authorities, and military communications systems.

64877.d: The fourth Ionosfera-M completes Russia's basic operational ionospheric monitoring constellation. Four satellites at complementary inclinations can map the global ionosphere continuously — providing Russia the space weather infrastructure equivalent to what NOAA's Space Weather Prediction Center provides to the US and its allies.

64899.d: France's CNES MicroCarb measures atmospheric CO2 precisely enough to verify whether national emissions reductions reported under the Paris Agreement are actually occurring or merely on paper. 'Trust but verify' for climate policy, conducted from orbit by a satellite the size of a washing machine.

64900.d: One of four CO3D stereoscopic satellites forming France's global 3D mapping constellation. The system will produce a complete 3D model of Earth's entire land surface at 50 cm resolution — the definitive three-dimensional snapshot of Earth as it exists today, against which every future change will be measured.

64901.d: CO3D #4, part of the four-satellite stereoscopic system capturing simultaneous multi-angle imagery to reconstruct Earth's surface in 3D. Flying in precise formation, the constellation will complete a global mapping campaign in one year — replacing decades of patchwork terrain data with one coherent dataset.

64902.d: CO3D #2, part of the constellation that will be the first to image Earth's complete land surface in consistent high-resolution 3D. The dataset will serve as the baseline against which future elevation changes from climate change, human development, and geological processes will be measured for generations.

64903.d: The lead CO3D satellite, progenitor of a dataset that will be used for generations. CO3D-1's three-dimensional snapshot of Earth in the 2020s will eventually be compared to future surveys to document how the planet is changing — a benchmark for measuring Earth's transformation set in the current decade.

65055.d: Saudi Arabia's SAR reconnaissance satellite provides all-weather, day-night radar imaging of the Middle East. It extends Saudi sovereign surveillance to conditions where optical satellites are blind — through clouds, dust storms, and darkness — giving the Kingdom persistent situational awareness independent of any foreign satellite partner.

65159.d: Europe's next-generation polar weather satellite begins the transition to the most advanced operational weather observing system ever. Its Infrared Atmospheric Sounding Interferometer-Next Generation measures atmospheric temperature and humidity in 16,000 spectral channels — detecting molecular absorption signatures that reveal weather patterns before they develop into storms.

65301.d: BASIL, a small satellite deployed from a large 2025 rideshare mission carrying dozens of compact payloads simultaneously to orbit. The rideshare model — sharing a single rocket's excess payload capacity among many small, independent missions — has become the dominant way research institutions and early-stage space companies now reach orbit at a fraction of the cost of a dedicated launch.

65316.d: Planet Labs' Pelican-4 — one of the fourth-generation imaging satellites representing Planet's sharpest eyes in orbit. The Pelican constellation is Planet's answer to Maxar and Airbus: a 30 cm resolution system capable of revisiting any location up to 30 times per day with a 32-satellite constellation. Each Pelican carries a Cassegrain telescope with a 4.03-meter effective focal length — capable of distinguishing an object the size of a laptop from 519 km up. Pelican-4 launched August 26, 2025 aboard a SpaceX Falcon-9 NAOS rideshare from Vandenberg. It was commissioned in late 2025 as part of the constellation that helped Planet turn its first profitable year as a company.
65316.t: Very High Resolution Commercial Imaging (30 cm)

65486.d: A Chinese Shiyan technology test satellite in geostationary orbit, continuing a program that has tested propulsion, communications, proximity operations, and materials technologies from GEO altitude. Each Shiyan satellite contributes to China's accelerating GEO capability development — building the industrial and engineering experience that enables China to deploy advanced geostationary spacecraft at an increasingly competitive pace.

65544.d: Lead satellite of the third Yaogan-40 triplet, continuing China's polar-orbit global naval ELINT surveillance buildout. Three generations into the program, Yaogan-40 has established a steady replenishment cadence extending China's naval signals triangulation capability to any ocean on Earth, a marked expansion beyond the Pacific-centric coverage of the program's Yaogan-25 through Yaogan-31 predecessors.
65544.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (Polar Orbit Triplet)

65545.d: Second satellite of the third Yaogan-40 triplet, part of China's continuing investment in globally-capable naval surveillance from polar orbit. As the PLA Navy's operational footprint has expanded into the Indian Ocean, the Red Sea, and the South Atlantic, Yaogan-40's worldwide coverage has become increasingly relevant to supporting Chinese naval deployments far beyond the traditional Western Pacific theater.
65545.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (Polar Orbit Triplet)

65546.d: Third satellite completing the third Yaogan-40 triplet. Nine satellites across three generations now give China's polar-orbit ELINT constellation the ability to triangulate naval radio and radar emissions across any ocean basin on the planet — a genuinely global naval surveillance capability that took China roughly fifteen years to build, starting from the first regional Yaogan-9 triplet in 2010.
65546.t: Chinese ELINT Naval Surveillance (Polar Orbit Triplet)

65563.d: Yaogan-45, launched September 9, 2025 on a modified Long March 7A — the rocket's first-ever mission to medium Earth orbit rather than its usual geostationary transfer trajectory, requiring strengthened fuel tanks and an altered engine ignition sequence. Yaogan-45 settled into a circular 7,500 km altitude orbit at just 20° inclination, an unprecedented profile for the Yaogan program that analyst Jonathan McDowell assessed as 'presumably for wide area surveillance' — trading the sharp resolution of low Earth orbit for dramatically longer, more persistent viewing time over any chosen region. The orbit sits within the Van Allen radiation belt's inner edge, an unusual environmental tradeoff for a surveillance mission.

65565.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-601, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes the constellation's global mesh network for military communications.
65565.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65565.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65566.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-602, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes the low-latency optical inter-satellite link backbone connecting sensors to shooters worldwide.
65566.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65566.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65567.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-603, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes the SDA's 'pizza box' satellite bus design built for mass production rather than individual customization.
65567.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65567.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65568.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-604, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes a network designed so that no single satellite loss meaningfully degrades overall coverage.
65568.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65568.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65569.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-605, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes the architecture underpinning the Pentagon's Joint All-Domain Command and Control concept.
65569.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65569.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65570.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-606, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes a constellation explicitly designed to be cheap and numerous enough that adversaries have little incentive to target any single satellite.
65570.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65570.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65571.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-607, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes the same Link 16 tactical data link technology fighter jets and ships have used for decades, now relayed through space.
65571.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65571.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65572.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-608, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes a demonstrated real-world capability: relaying live tactical data between US and allied Five Eyes nations in November 2023 trials.
65572.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65572.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65573.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-609, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes the optical mesh network intended to eventually connect with a much larger, still-developing Space Data Network backbone.
65573.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65573.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65574.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-610, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes one of 154 operational Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites plus four demonstration spacecraft that make up this generation.
65574.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65574.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65575.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-611, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes part of a constellation that, per a 2025 GAO report, faced real development delays and had not yet fully proven its laser communication capability at initial deployment.
65575.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65575.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65576.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-612, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes a satellite built to a roughly $14 million average unit cost — cheap by military satellite standards specifically so the constellation can be proliferated at scale.
65576.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65576.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65577.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-613, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes the SDA's answer to a persistent Pentagon question: how do you build space infrastructure that survives a war rather than becoming an easy first target.
65577.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65577.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65578.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-614, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes a mesh architecture that can reroute data around any single failed or destroyed satellite automatically.
65578.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65578.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65579.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-615, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes part of the broader Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture the White House has cited as foundational to the future Golden Dome missile defense system.
65579.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65579.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65580.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-616, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes one of dozens of nearly identical spacecraft that, individually, are unremarkable — but collectively form a genuinely novel military communications capability.
65580.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65580.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65581.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-617, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes a satellite whose value lies entirely in the mesh it's part of, not in any capability unique to itself.
65581.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65581.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65582.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-618, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes part of York Space Systems' contribution to a Transport Layer split across multiple competing manufacturers to avoid single-vendor risk.
65582.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65582.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65583.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-619, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes a demonstration that commercial-style mass production techniques can be applied directly to military space hardware.
65583.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65583.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65584.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-620, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes the connective tissue meant to link America's missile-tracking satellites directly to interceptors and commanders in near-real time.
65584.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65584.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65585.d: PRAETORIAN SDA-621, one of York Space Systems' Tranche 1 Transport Layer satellites for the US Space Development Agency's Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture — the Pentagon's answer to a fundamental problem: how do you build military space infrastructure that isn't an easy, high-value target? Rather than a handful of expensive satellites, the SDA is deploying over 150 nearly identical, mass-produced Transport Layer spacecraft in low Earth orbit, connected via optical laser inter-satellite links into a resilient global mesh network. This satellite contributes a single node in a satellite network that the Space Force intends to keep expanding roughly every two years as new tranches replace and add to the old.
65585.a: York Space Systems / US Space Development Agency
65585.t: US Military Mesh Communications (PWSA Transport Layer)

65588.d: Indonesia's latest geostationary communications satellite, the fifth in the Nusantara ('Archipelago') series. Indonesia's systematic investment in domestic geostationary satellite capacity reflects the government's recognition that for a country of 17,000 islands, satellite is as fundamental to national infrastructure as roads or ports. Each new Nusantara satellite extends the reach of digital services — broadband, government platforms, banking — to the island communities that terrestrial networks will reach last.

65815.d: The most advanced Fengyun-3 polar satellite, FY-3H carries upgraded microwave and ozone instruments. Each successive Fengyun-3 demonstrates China's growing mastery — from copying Western instruments to pioneering unique orbital strategies like FY-3E's dawn-dusk orbit that no other nation has attempted operationally.

66052.d: A piece of Russian scientific hardware now drifting in its own orbit after being detached from the exterior of the International Space Station. On October 16, 2025, Roscosmos cosmonauts Kud-Sverchkov and Mikaev exited the ISS and retrieved this HRC (High Resolution Camera) monoblock — part of a compact radio-optical detector system mounted on the station's exterior to test Earth observation and ecological monitoring technology. After removal during the spacewalk, the unit was released from the ISS and catalogued as a separate orbiting object. It is now slowly descending, losing altitude to atmospheric drag until it re-enters. A piece of a telescope, orbiting alone, watching the Earth it was designed to photograph.

66142.d: One of the latest TJS satellites, launched in 2025 as China continues to expand its classified GEO military presence. The TJS program has now been active for a decade, and its scale has grown from a single experimental satellite to what analysts describe as a mature military communications and possibly signals intelligence constellation operating across the entire geostationary arc.

66300.d: ICEYE-X65, one of the first ICEYE satellites deployed into an unusual 45° inclination — a marked departure from the sun-synchronous polar orbits typical of the rest of the fleet. This lower-inclination orbit trades polar coverage for better revisit performance over equatorial and mid-latitude regions, letting ICEYE tailor part of its constellation specifically to customers whose areas of interest sit closer to the equator than the poles.

66301.d: ICEYE-X66, launched into the same distinctive 45° inclination as its sibling ICEYE-X65, part of a deliberate orbital plane diversification strategy. Rather than adding every new satellite to the existing polar constellation, ICEYE is building out complementary lower-inclination coverage optimized for specific regional customer demand.

66302.d: ICEYE-X67, completing a small trio of satellites sharing the unusual 45°-inclined orbital plane first established with ICEYE-X65 and X66. Together they extend ICEYE's radar revisit density specifically over the world's lower latitudes, complementing the company's much larger polar-orbiting SAR fleet.

66311.d: India's replacement military naval communications satellite, updating the capability introduced by GSAT-7 with higher power, more frequency bands, and improved coverage of the Indian Ocean. GSAT-7R continues the Indian Navy's development of real-time maritime communications and situational awareness — essential for a navy that increasingly operates across the full width of the Indo-Pacific as India's strategic interests expand beyond traditional South Asian boundaries.

66313.d: Yaogan-46, launched November 2-3, 2025, joining Yaogan-45 in the same unprecedented 7,500 km, 20°-inclined medium Earth orbit — the second satellite in what analysts believe is an entirely new Chinese wide-area surveillance system. Chinese state media described Yaogan-46's applications as disaster prevention, land resource survey, water conservancy, and meteorology — a description notably different from the standard boilerplate used for most other Yaogan satellites, suggesting this may genuinely be a distinct program rather than another naval ELINT triplet in disguise.

66315.d: Sentinel-1D, launched November 4, 2025 on the final flight completing Europe's first-generation Copernicus radar constellation — a 12-year journey that began with Sentinel-1A in 2014. Built by Thales Alenia Space, Sentinel-1D carries C-band synthetic aperture radar that images Earth's surface day or night, through cloud, storm, or complete darkness, and — like its sibling Sentinel-1C — a new AIS receiver that identifies ships by their transponder signals directly alongside the radar imagery of the vessels themselves. Declared fully operational May 1, 2026, it now flies a three-satellite overlap with Sentinel-1A and 1C through mid-2026 before gradually taking over Sentinel-1A's aging ground track, ensuring uninterrupted radar coverage of Earth's ice, oil spills, ship traffic, and land subsidence for at least another seven years.
66315.t: C-band SAR Imaging (Copernicus — First Generation Complete)

66454.d: The second ViaSat-3 satellite, covering Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Viasat-3 F2 learned from F1's reflector deployment issue, incorporating design modifications to reduce the same risk. Despite the F1 setback, Viasat pressed forward with its multi-satellite strategy, betting that its GEO high-throughput architecture can compete with LEO broadband constellations like Starlink in markets where the price and latency tradeoffs favor established GEO operators.

66514.d: Sentinel-6B continues the most precise sea level measurement record in history into the 2030s. The combined Sentinel-6A and 6B data will determine whether ocean rise is accelerating decade over decade — the key measurement for infrastructure planning in Miami, Shanghai, Amsterdam, Mumbai, and every other coastal city planning its future against a moving sea.

66545.d: Lead satellite of the Shijian-30 trio, launched mid-November 2025 as part of an unusually active year for China's Shijian technology demonstration program — six Shijian launches in 2025 alone, compared to just one in each of the two prior years. The Shijian-30 group arrived weeks after the culmination of one of the program's most consequential missions to date: Shijian-25's world-first satellite-to-satellite refueling of the long-dormant Shijian-21, an operation the US Space Force monitored closely using its own GSSAP inspector satellites.

66546.d: Second satellite of the Shijian-30 trio, part of a 2025 surge in Chinese Shijian program activity that also included Shijian-25's satellite refueling demonstration and the classified, multi-institution Shijian-26 mission. Of the roughly 50 Shijian satellites launched since the program began in 1971, some 38 remain operational — making it one of China's longest-running and most consistently active experimental satellite lines.

66547.d: Third satellite completing the Shijian-30 trio, launched alongside its two formation-mates in November 2025. The Shijian program's stated mission — to 'put into practice' or establish best practices for new satellite technologies — has in recent years increasingly overlapped with capabilities carrying clear military significance, including robotic satellite servicing, in-orbit refueling, and the proximity operations first demonstrated by Shijian-21's 2022 towing of a defunct BeiDou satellite into a graveyard orbit.

66549.d: A recent Shijian technology demonstration satellite testing advanced GEO capabilities. China's SJ series demonstrates Beijing's approach to military space technology development — test boldly in orbit with acknowledged technology demonstration satellites, then incorporate proven capabilities into operational systems. This approach differs from the US model of extensive ground testing before flight, allowing China to advance capabilities faster.

66655.d: Part of China's growing CAS500 commercial satellite family offering cost-effective 2-meter optical imaging. These platforms represent China's broader strategy to build commercial space capabilities that compete globally — developing the industrial ecosystem for full satellite self-sufficiency.

66657.d: SPIRONE, a small satellite deployed as part of a large 2025 rideshare mission carrying numerous compact payloads to orbit simultaneously. Missions like this have become the standard route for university programs, national space agencies, and commercial startups to test new technology or conduct focused science experiments without the cost of a dedicated launch vehicle.

66704.d: Flock 4H-1, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in a single 2025 batch, contributing to Planet Labs' constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global imaging. This satellite is part of the constellation's daily-revisit backbone, imaging nearly all of Earth's landmass every single day.

66705.d: Flock 4H-2, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in a single 2025 batch, contributing to Planet Labs' constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global imaging. This satellite is one of 200+ active SuperDoves feeding Planet's continuously updated global basemap.

66706.d: Flock 4H-3, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in a single 2025 batch, contributing to Planet Labs' constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global imaging. This satellite is contributing to the agricultural, environmental, and disaster-response monitoring that draws on Planet's aggregate daily coverage.

66707.d: Flock 4H-4, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in a single 2025 batch, contributing to Planet Labs' constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global imaging. This satellite is carrying the standard 47-megapixel, 8-band PSB.SD sensor shared across the entire modern SuperDove generation.

66708.d: Flock 4H-5, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in a single 2025 batch, contributing to Planet Labs' constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global imaging. This satellite is producing 3-meter resolution imagery across a 32.5 × 19.6 km swath on every pass.

66709.d: Flock 4H-6, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in a single 2025 batch, contributing to Planet Labs' constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global imaging. This satellite is one of 36 identical satellites launched together in this batch, each imaging a slightly different ground track to maximize combined coverage.

66710.d: Flock 4H-7, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in a single 2025 batch, contributing to Planet Labs' constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global imaging. This satellite is part of Planet's mature, production-phase constellation where new Flocks replenish capacity rather than represent any single technological leap.

66711.d: Flock 4H-8, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in a single 2025 batch, contributing to Planet Labs' constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global imaging. This satellite is functionally identical to its launch siblings — the constellation's value lies in the aggregate coverage, not any one spacecraft.

66712.d: Flock 4H-9, one of 36 SuperDoves launched together in a single 2025 batch, contributing to Planet Labs' constellation of 200+ active satellites providing daily 3-meter global imaging.
