66751.d: ICEYE-X58, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is flying alongside dozens of sister satellites in a mesh of overlapping orbital planes designed to minimize the time between successive images of any single point on Earth. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

66752.d: ICEYE-X59, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is ICEYE's Gen4 satellites now deliver resolution as fine as 16 cm — a leap from the 10-meter imagery the company's original 2018 satellite produced, achieved in barely six years of continuous design iteration. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

66753.d: ICEYE-X60, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is part of the record 22-satellite deployment year that pushed ICEYE's total constellation past 60 spacecraft by early 2025. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

66754.d: ICEYE-X61, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is contributing to ICEYE's core commercial promise: imagery that doesn't wait for clear skies, delivered to insurers, militaries, and disaster responders who cannot afford to wait days for cloud cover to lift. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

66755.d: ICEYE-X62, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is one of the satellites underpinning ICEYE's dedicated national security contracts, including missions supporting Ukraine's battlefield surveillance, Poland's MikroSAR program, and Greece's national space effort. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

66820.d: KOMPSAT-7 (Arirang-7), launched December 1, 2025 on a Vega-C from Kourou after a launch delayed from its original 2022 target. Built by South Korea's Korea Aerospace Research Institute, KOMPSAT-7 carries the country's sharpest civilian optical imager yet — the AEISS-HR sensor resolves objects down to roughly 30 cm in panchromatic mode — paired for the first time with a dedicated infrared module sensitive enough to detect the heat signature of a wildfire, a volcanic eruption, or an urban heat island. A control moment gyroscope lets it snap onto a new target almost instantly, and over a terabit of onboard storage means it can bank huge volumes of imagery before ever passing over a ground station. KOMPSAT-7 released its first public images in March 2026, with a twin satellite, KOMPSAT-7A, expected to follow in 2026 to double the constellation's revisit rate.
66820.t: Korean Sub-Meter Optical + Infrared Earth Observation Satellite

66906.d: Russian-origin hardware jettisoned from the International Space Station exterior during a 2025 EVA, now tracking independently in low Earth orbit. Objects like DUPLEX are a recurring artifact of the ISS Russian segment — experiments are mounted externally on Zvezda, Nauka, or Poisk modules, operated for their test duration, then removed during spacewalks and released into free orbit to gradually re-enter. The ISS has spawned hundreds of such tracked objects. DUPLEX, likely named for its dual-function design, has now completed its mission. It is silently descending toward an atmospheric re-entry of its own, a very small piece of a very large space station finding its own path back to Earth.

66988.d: Yaogan-47, launched December 9, 2025 on a Long March 4B into a conventional sun-synchronous low Earth orbit — a return to the traditional Yaogan imaging regime after the unprecedented medium-Earth-orbit deployments of Yaogan-45 and Yaogan-46 just months earlier. Analyst Jonathan McDowell described Yaogan-47 simply as 'a conventional large LEO imaging spy satellite,' suggesting China is running its wide-area MEO surveillance experiment (Yaogan-45/46) in parallel with, rather than as a replacement for, its established high-resolution LEO reconnaissance satellite line.

66990.d: Part of China's continuing TJS classified GEO satellite program. The rapid cadence of TJS launches — several per year through 2024 and 2025 — reflects China's strategic determination to achieve parity with the United States in geostationary military capability. Each new TJS satellite makes China's space-based military architecture more resilient, redundant, and difficult to counter.

67226.d: One of the newest TJS satellites. Chinese officials have not acknowledged the military nature of the TJS series, but the operational pace and scale of the program, combined with observed anomalous behaviors, have convinced US Space Force and independent analysts that TJS represents a major Chinese military GEO capability development program rather than any experimental communications initiative.

67246.d: China's third and most advanced Fengyun-4 geostationary weather satellite, expanding the constellation to provide comprehensive coverage across Asia and the Indo-Pacific. FY-4C introduces further improvements in imaging cadence and accuracy, with a scanning period fast enough to track individual thunderstorm cell development every minute. Together the three FY-4 satellites form the most capable Chinese geostationary weather constellation ever, with coverage matching the sophistication of GOES and Meteosat systems.

67300.d: Tianhui-7, part of China's decades-long PLA stereo mapping and terrain intelligence program that began with the original Tianhui-1 in 2010. Each Tianhui generation has refined the accuracy and coverage of China's independent 3D elevation database — the digital terrain models underlying everything from artillery targeting calculations to civilian infrastructure planning — building a topographic intelligence archive that no foreign or commercial data source can substitute for.
67300.a: CAST / PLA Navigation and Survey Bureau

67302.d: One of two Shijian-29 satellites launched together, demonstrating China's growing capability to deploy complex paired GEO systems. Twin GEO technology demonstrators allow China to test formation flying, inter-satellite links, and distributed payload architectures — capabilities essential for both future military GEO constellations and next-generation commercial communications systems.

67304.d: The third COSMO-SkyMed Second Generation satellite gives Italy sub-4-hour revisit over Mediterranean targets — the capability to provide near-continuous monitoring of the world's most-watched waterway for illegal migration, arms smuggling, and strategic naval movement.

67372.d: ICEYE-X63, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is flying in ICEYE's standard sun-synchronous polar orbit, ensuring consistent lighting-independent imaging geometry across every single pass. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

67373.d: ICEYE-X64, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is part of the Finnish company's push toward sub-daily global revisit, the operational threshold that makes persistent monitoring — not just occasional snapshots — commercially possible. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

67403.d: The companion to Shijian-29A, demonstrating China's twin-satellite GEO technology testing approach. Flying two identical platforms in close orbital proximity allows Chinese engineers to test distributed computing across spacecraft, formation control algorithms, and coordinated observation — capabilities that would be directly applicable to a military constellation that needs satellites to work cooperatively.

67433.d: Yaogan-50 (01), launched into an unusual 142° inclination — a retrograde orbit inclined more than 90° from the equator, placing it among the rarer orbital geometries used by any Chinese reconnaissance satellite. This unconventional inclination, paired with the Yaogan designation's typical reconnaissance implications, has drawn attention from trackers, though the specific mission and sensor payload remain unconfirmed as of this writing.
67433.t: Chinese Military Satellite (Assessed Reconnaissance, Unusual Orbit)

67556.d: MR-2, a small satellite or technology demonstration payload launched in early 2026, part of the steady flow of compact rideshare missions that has made orbital access dramatically more affordable for research institutions and small companies over the past decade. Precise details of MR-2's specific experiment or sensor payload have not been widely publicized.

67683.d: KNACKSAT-2, Thailand's second domestically-developed university CubeSat, deployed from the ISS's Japanese Kibo module on February 3, 2026. Built by King Mongkut's University of Technology North Bangkok, the 3U spacecraft continues the KNACKSAT-1 mission's proof that Thai engineering students could design and fly a satellite entirely in-house. It hosts multiple payloads including an APRS amateur radio digipeater — callsign HSØK — coordinated through the International Amateur Radio Union so ham operators worldwide can relay messages through it, alongside standard engineering telemetry beaconed once a minute.
67683.a: King Mongkut's University of Technology North Bangkok (Thailand)

67684.d: A 2U CubeSat developed by Sapienza University of Rome and deployed from the ISS's Japanese Kibo module via JAXA's J-SSOD #35 small satellite deployer in October 2025. CORAL represents the Italian university space program at its most tangible — student-built hardware ejected from the same airlock that has launched hundreds of CubeSats from 17 countries over the past decade. The ISS Kibo module has become the world's most accessible satellite launch platform, allowing universities to deploy directly from 400 km altitude without booking a dedicated launch. It shares its orbit with the station that built it.

67685.d: Gxiba-1, Mexico's satellite selected in the sixth round of JAXA's KiboCUBE program, deployed from the ISS on February 3, 2026. Gxiba-1 is built specifically to photograph active Mexican volcanoes and study how their ash plumes disperse through the atmosphere — a genuinely practical application for a country whose volcanic activity, from Popocatépetl to Colima, directly affects aviation safety and public health for millions of nearby residents. Its selection through KiboCUBE reflects JAXA and UNOOSA's program to give developing spacefaring nations a direct, low-cost route to orbit via the ISS's Kibo airlock.

67686.d: UiTMSAT-2, the first nanosatellite designed, developed, and led entirely by an ASEAN university, deployed from the ISS on February 3, 2026. Built by Universiti Teknologi MARA (UiTM) in Malaysia as the culmination of the multi-year ASEANSAT program — a collaboration also involving universities in the Philippines and Thailand, mentored by Japan's Kyushu Institute of Technology — the 1U CubeSat carries a high-resolution Earth-imaging camera and an IoT satellite communications payload designed for smart agriculture and disaster monitoring across Southeast Asia. It follows UiTMSAT-1's 2018 mission, which first put Malaysia on the nanosatellite map.
67686.t: Malaysian University CubeSat (ISS-Deployed, ASEAN Regional Program)

67687.d: LEOPARD, a joint Japanese-Singaporean CubeSat built by Kyushu Institute of Technology and Nanyang Technological University, deployed from the ISS on February 3, 2026. Its name spells out its mission — Light intensity Experiment with On-orbit Positioning and satellite Ranging Demonstration — using multispectral cameras to study horizon airglow, the faint natural light scattering visible at Earth's atmospheric edge, alongside experimental on-board processing of one-way radio signals for satellite positioning. The techniques it validates are explicitly framed as groundwork for future university-led lunar missions, extending small-satellite engineering experience well beyond low Earth orbit.
67687.a: Kyushu Institute of Technology (Japan) / Nanyang Technological University (Singapore)

67688.d: HMU-SAT2, nicknamed 'Teruteru' after the Japanese good-weather charm doll, deployed from the ISS on February 3, 2026. Built jointly by Hokkaido University of Science and Nagano Prefecture's Komagane Technical High School — making it, remarkably, a satellite co-designed by working high schoolers — HMU-SAT2 carries LED lights that flash Morse code messages down to Earth, a technology demonstration built explicitly for educational outreach. It's one of the plainest reminders in orbit that spaceflight has become accessible enough for teenagers to help build something that actually flies.
67688.a: Hokkaido University of Science / Komagane Technical High School (Japan)

67756.d: Russia's latest Elektro-L geostationary weather satellite, launched in 2025 and continuing modernization of Russian meteorological infrastructure. As climate change intensifies Arctic weather extremes — disrupting Russia's northern shipping routes, oil infrastructure built on permafrost, and seasonal agriculture patterns — high-quality geostationary weather monitoring has become a strategic national necessity, not merely a scientific service.

67757.d: Iran's satellite broadcasting service, named after Jam-e Jam — the mythical cup of King Jamshid in Persian mythology that was said to reflect the entire world. Jam-e Jam is also Iran's international state broadcasting brand. The satellite provides Iranian state television programming across the Middle East, Europe, and Central Asia — an instrument of Iranian soft power, projecting Farsi-language news, cultural programming, and political messaging to diaspora communities and international audiences.

68126.d: EchoStar's latest satellite, providing enhanced capacity for DISH Network's direct-to-home television service. EchoStar-25 arrives as DISH navigates the transformation from a traditional satellite TV provider to a modern streaming-integrated service — the satellite carrying linear television channels while DISH's wireless (Boost Mobile) business and streaming partnerships evolve around it. The satellite ensures continuity of DISH's satellite television infrastructure regardless of the company's strategic digital transformation.

68196.d: Yaogan-50 (02), launched into the same unusual 142° retrograde inclination as its predecessor. Two satellites now share this rare orbital geometry, suggesting a deliberate — if still publicly unconfirmed — design choice for whatever surveillance or technology demonstration role the Yaogan-50 program serves within China's broader reconnaissance satellite ecosystem.
68196.t: Chinese Military Satellite (Assessed Reconnaissance, Unusual Orbit)

68377.d: SuperView Neo-2 (05), part of China Siwei's expanding next-generation commercial imaging fleet, adding a fifth satellite to the Neo-2 sub-constellation. Each addition compounds the network's global revisit frequency, part of China's broader push to field commercial Earth observation capability competitive with Western operators like Planet and Maxar.
68377.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

68378.d: SuperView Neo-2 (06), completing a six-satellite Neo-2 sub-constellation that, combined with the parallel Neo-1 and Neo-3 lines, gives China Siwei one of the most extensive next-generation commercial imaging fleets of any single operator outside the United States and Europe.
68378.t: Chinese Commercial Very High Resolution Imaging (Next-Gen)

68494.d: ICEYE-X71, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is contributing dwell-mode imaging capability, staring at a fixed target for up to 25 seconds to sharpen resolution and reduce speckle noise for detailed structural monitoring. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

68496.d: ICEYE-X73, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is one of dozens of satellites that helped ICEYE become the world's largest operator of commercial synthetic aperture radar by satellite count. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

68497.d: ICEYE's 75th SAR satellite and one of its latest Gen4 spacecraft — the most capable commercial synthetic aperture radar platform ever flown. Launched in 2026, ICEYE-X75 delivers up to 16 cm resolution radar imagery regardless of weather, cloud cover, smoke, or complete darkness. Where optical satellites need sunlight and clear skies, SAR satellites fire their own microwave pulses at the Earth and measure the return — making them the only commercial option for monitoring floods beneath storm clouds, detecting ships at night, or watching wildfires through their own smoke. ICEYE's Finland-founded constellation crossed 70 satellites in 2026 after raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation — the rapid commercialization of what was once purely military technology.

68500.d: ICEYE-X74, co-launched with ICEYE-X75, is one of the Finnish company's latest fourth-generation SAR satellites offering a 400 km field of regard for high-resolution imagery — four times the coverage area of previous generations. At 85 kg, each ICEYE satellite is a fraction of the size of traditional SAR spacecraft, yet delivers imagery that was classified military capability just a decade ago. ICEYE-X74 serves both ICEYE's commercial constellation and potentially contributes capacity to one of the company's dedicated government contracts — including missions for the Greek National Space Program, the Polish Armed Forces' MikroSAR program, and Ukraine's battlefield surveillance under a German Ministry of Defence-funded Rheinmetall-ICEYE partnership.

68502.d: ICEYE-X76, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is part of ICEYE's constellation supporting the 2025-2026 growth phase that saw the company's total fleet more than double in under two years. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

68504.d: ICEYE-X72, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is flying alongside dozens of sister satellites in a mesh of overlapping orbital planes designed to minimize the time between successive images of any single point on Earth. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

68835.d: An advanced optical Earth observation satellite in Saudi Arabia's sovereign program. Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 explicitly targets domestic satellite manufacturing — and the Kingdom's investment in operational reconnaissance satellites is building the engineering base to eventually achieve it.

68995.d: ICEYE-X78, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is ICEYE's Gen4 satellites now deliver resolution as fine as 16 cm — a leap from the 10-meter imagery the company's original 2018 satellite produced, achieved in barely six years of continuous design iteration. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

68996.d: ICEYE-X82, one of the Finnish company's commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites — spacecraft that fire their own microwave pulses at Earth and read the echo, producing usable imagery regardless of cloud cover, smoke, or total darkness. This satellite is part of the record 22-satellite deployment year that pushed ICEYE's total constellation past 60 spacecraft by early 2025. ICEYE has grown from a single 100 kg proof-of-concept in 2018 into the world's largest commercial SAR operator, raising over €1 billion at a €10 billion valuation along the way.

69013.d: China's CAS500-2 commercial observation platform demonstrates China's ability to offer reliable mid-resolution Earth observation at competitive prices. As Chinese satellite manufacturers enter the global market for commercial platforms, they are following the path of Chinese electronics — from domestic supply chain to world export.

69049.d: Tianzhou-10, China's tenth and heaviest-ever cargo resupply mission to the Tiangong space station, launched May 11, 2026 aboard a Long March 7 and docking with Tianhe's aft port just five hours later. Its 6,300 kg of cargo included fresh fruit, steaks, and chicken wings for the crew's oven, 700 kg of station propellant, a new modular Feitian spacesuit engineered for 20 spacewalks over four years, and a Hong Kong University of Science and Technology-built instrument to monitor global carbon dioxide and methane emissions. Tianzhou-10 also debuted an intelligent onboard diagnostic system that autonomously detects technical faults — software China says will become a safety baseline for future crewed lunar missions.

69123.d: Launched just weeks ago in May 2026, SMILE is humanity's first attempt to photograph Earth's magnetic shield in X-rays — imaging the magnetosphere the way a doctor images a body. This ESA-Chinese joint mission will map how the boundary of Earth's magnetic field moves and flexes in response to solar wind for up to 40 continuous hours per orbit, using a soft X-ray imager and UV aurora camera simultaneously. It will answer a deceptively simple question we've never been able to answer before: what does Earth's magnetic shield actually look like from the outside?

69235.d: One of the most recently launched TJS satellites, continuing China's relentless expansion of its classified GEO military program. The TJS constellation now spans the geostationary arc with multiple satellites providing overlapping coverage — an architecture that provides redundancy against both technical failures and potential adversary interference. TJS-24 represents the current frontier of China's military GEO presence.

69320.d: A cataloged object designated 'Object A' from a 2026 launch, part of the debris and sub-payload tracking that follows nearly every orbital mission. Objects like this are typically hardware shed during payload separation or deployment — adapters, covers, or small fragments — that the US Space Surveillance Network catalogs individually to maintain an accurate picture of everything sharing an orbital lane with active satellites.

69570.d: Shijian-31, one of the newest satellites in China's long-running technology demonstration program as of this writing, launched into the 63.4°-inclined orbital regime shared with several of China's ELINT and reconnaissance satellite families. Following a 2025 marked by the program's most consequential year yet — including a world-first in-orbit satellite refueling operation via Shijian-25 and 21 — Shijian-31's specific experimental purpose had not been publicly detailed at time of writing, continuing the program's decades-long pattern of operating with minimal public disclosure.

07646.d: The world's oldest actively tracked geodetic satellite — a solid brass sphere 24 cm across launched in 1975 and still flying. Laser ranging stations fire pulses at its 60 retroreflectors and time the returning light to millimeter precision, measuring how Earth's gravitational field changes as ice sheets melt and water moves around the planet. Still delivering science after 50 years in orbit.

08820.d: A 60 cm brass sphere covered in 426 retroreflectors, LAGEOS-1 is the most precisely tracked object ever placed in orbit. Ground stations bounce laser pulses off it to measure distance within millimeters — tracking the slow drift of tectonic plates, the tiny wobble of Earth's axis, and how melting ice sheets redistribute Earth's mass. Launched in 1976, it carries a plaque showing Earth's continents as they'll look in 8 million years. LAGEOS is designed to survive that long.

05560.d: A US Air Force Space Test Program satellite launched in 1971 that is still in orbit 55 years later — one of the oldest tracked objects in low Earth orbit that has not re-entered. ASTEX (SESP 1971-2) tested a celestial mapping infrared scanner, a 457-meter flexible roll-up solar array (the largest ever flown at the time), and instruments measuring energetic particle interactions with the ionosphere. The satellite worked until December 20, 1973, when a command telling the transmitter to stop was issued and failed — the transmitter kept broadcasting until it wore itself out. The spacecraft went silent in 1973 but never came down. It circles Earth today at 720 km altitude, a 50-year-old relic of Nixon-era space technology that has outlasted every mission it was ever built to support.

04327.d: The satellite that proved ion propulsion works in space — and is still up there 56 years later. SERT 2 (Space Electric Rocket Test 2), launched February 3, 1970, carried two mercury ion thrusters and the largest solar arrays NASA had ever flown, operating them for thousands of hours in the first long-duration electric propulsion test in history. One thruster ran 2,011 hours before a grid short stopped it; the second ran 3,781 hours. NASA reactivated the thrusters periodically through 1981, completing over 500 restarts. The technology validated here is the direct ancestor of the ion propulsion systems now used for station-keeping on virtually every commercial geostationary satellite and for deep-space missions like Dawn, Hayabusa, and BepiColombo. The spacecraft itself, now silent, continues to orbit Earth — the great-grandfather of modern electric propulsion, still flying.
04327.t: Ion Propulsion Pioneer (Silent — Still Orbiting After 55 Years)

01361.d: LCS-1 (Lincoln Calibration Sphere 1), launched May 6, 1965 and, remarkably, still the oldest continuously used spacecraft in orbit today — over 60 years later. LCS-1 is nothing more than a hollow, precisely machined aluminum sphere 1.12 meters across, built by MIT's Lincoln Laboratory with absolutely no power, electronics, or fuel of any kind. Its entire purpose is to give ground-based radar systems a perfectly known reference target with a fixed, precisely measured radar cross-section, allowing radar operators worldwide to calibrate their instruments against it. With a predicted orbital lifetime of roughly 30,000 years, LCS-1 will very likely outlast every other object humans have ever put into space — including, quite possibly, humanity's own written record of having built it.
01361.t: Radar Calibration Sphere (Oldest Continuously Used Spacecraft)
